response:
**Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)**  
*Let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb {R}\) be continuous.  If \(y\) is any real number between
\(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) (that is, \(f(a)\le y\le f(b)\) or \(f(b)\le y\le f(a)\)),
then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that \(f(c)=y\).*

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof

Without loss of generality we may assume  

\[
f(a)<f(b)
\]

and that the number \(y\) satisfies  

\[
f(a)<y<f(b).
\]

(If \(f(b)<f(a)\) we simply reverse the roles of \(a\) and \(b\); the case
\(y=f(a)\) or \(y=f(b)\) is trivial.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 1.  A useful set

Define

\[
S=\{x\in[a,b]\mid f(x)\le y\}.
\]

*Non‑emptiness.*  
Because \(f(a)<y\), we have \(f(a)\le y\); thus \(a\in S\) and \(S\neq\varnothing\).

*Bounded above.*  
Every element of \(S\) lies in \([a,b]\); hence \(S\) is bounded above by \(b\).

By the completeness axiom of \(\mathbb R\), the set \(S\) has a least upper
bound.  Denote

\[
\alpha=\sup S\in[a,b].
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 2.  The function value at \(\alpha\)

We claim that \(f(\alpha)=y\).

*Suppose first that \(f(\alpha)<y\).*  
Set \(\varepsilon=y-f(\alpha)>0\).
By continuity of \(f\) at the point \(\alpha\), there exists \(\delta>0\) such that

\[
|x-\alpha|<\delta \;\Longrightarrow\; |f(x)-f(\alpha)|<\varepsilon .
\]

In particular, if \(x\in(\alpha,\alpha+\delta)\) (and still \(x\le b\)), then

\[
f(x) < f(\alpha)+\varepsilon = y .
\]

Thus \(x\in S\).  But \(x>\alpha\), contradicting that \(\alpha=\sup S\)
is an upper bound for \(S\).

*Suppose now that \(f(\alpha)>y\).*  
Let \(\varepsilon=f(\alpha)-y>0\).
Again by continuity, choose \(\delta>0\) so that

\[
|x-\alpha|<\delta \;\Longrightarrow\; |f(x)-f(\alpha)|<\varepsilon .
\]

If \(x\in(\alpha-\delta,\alpha)\) (and \(x\ge a\)), then

\[
f(x) > f(\alpha)-\varepsilon = y .
\]

Hence \(x\notin S\).  But \(x<\alpha\) and \(x\in(a,b]\) implies that
\(x\) is still an upper bound of \(S\); since \(x<\alpha\), \(\alpha\) would not be the
least upper bound – again a contradiction.

Thus neither \(f(\alpha)<y\) nor \(f(\alpha)>y\) is possible, so

\[
\boxed{\,f(\alpha)=y\, } .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 3.  Conclusion

Because \(\alpha\in[a,b]\) and \(f(\alpha)=y\), we have exhibited a point
\(c=\alpha\) with the required property.  This completes the proof of the
Intermediate Value Theorem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

The proof works unchanged if \(f(b)<f(a)\); we simply reverse the roles of
\(a\) and \(b\) when defining the set \(S\).  The theorem therefore holds for
any continuous function on a closed interval. ∎

**Proposition.**  
Let \(G\) be a group and let \(H\le G\) be a subgroup with \([G:H]=2\).  
Then \(H\) is normal in \(G\).

---

### Proof

Since \([G:H]=2\), the set of left cosets of \(H\) has exactly two elements:  

\[
G/H=\{\,H,\; g_{0}H\,\},
\]

where \(g_{0}\) is any element not in \(H\).  
Similarly the set of right cosets has exactly two elements

\[
H\backslash G=\{\,H,\; Hg_{0}\,\}.
\]

Take an arbitrary element \(g\in G\).

* **Case 1.** \(g\in H\).  
  Then both \(gH\) and \(Hg\) equal \(H\).

* **Case 2.** \(g\notin H\).  
  Then \(gH\) is the other left coset, hence \(gH=G\setminus H\).  
  Likewise \(Hg\) is the other right coset, so \(Hg=G\setminus H\).

Thus in either case we have \(gH=Hg\).  
Since this holds for every \(g\in G\), we conclude that

\[
gH = Hg \quad\text{for all } g\in G,
\]

which is exactly the definition of normality. Therefore \(H\trianglelefteq G\).

\(\square\)

---

### Remarks

* The argument uses only the fact that there are exactly two left (or right) cosets.  
* In group‑theoretic language, the action of \(G\) on the set of cosets \(G/H\) yields a homomorphism \(G\to S_2\).  Its kernel is the largest normal subgroup contained in \(H\); but because \(S_2\) has order \(2\), this kernel must be \(H\) itself, again showing \(H\trianglelefteq G\).  
* The result is often phrased as “every subgroup of index \(2\) is normal”; it is one of the simplest non‑trivial normality criteria.

Let  

\[
\mathscr{A}\;=\;\{A_{0},A_{1},A_{2},\dots \}
\]

be a **countable family** of **countable sets**.  
We prove that  

\[
U=\bigcup_{n\in \mathbb N}A_{n}
\]

is countable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Enumerating each \(A_{n}\)

Because every \(A_{n}\) is countable, for each \(n\in\mathbb N\) there exists a
bijection  

\[
f_{n}\;:\;\mathbb N \longrightarrow A_{n}.
\]

(If we adopt the convention that a set is *countable* iff it admits a
bijection with a subset of \(\mathbb N\), this is immediate.  In any case
we only need a **surjection** from \(\mathbb N\) onto \(A_{n}\);
the existence of a bijection follows from the definition of “countable”.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  A surjection from \(\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\) onto \(U\)

Define  

\[
g:\mathbb N\times\mathbb N \longrightarrow U ,\qquad
g(n,k)=f_{n}(k).
\]

*Surjectivity:*  
Take any \(u\in U\).  
Then \(u\in A_{n}\) for some \(n\).  
Since \(f_{n}\) is onto \(A_{n}\), there is \(k\in\mathbb N\) with
\(f_{n}(k)=u\).  Thus \(g(n,k)=u\), proving that \(g\) is onto.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  \(\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\) is countable

A standard bijection (Cantor’s pairing function) is  

\[
\pi:\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\longrightarrow\mathbb N,\qquad
\pi(n,k)=\frac{(n+k)(n+k+1)}{2}+k .
\]

It is easy to verify that \(\pi\) is one‑to‑one and onto; hence
\(\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\) is countable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Concluding that \(U\) is countable

Because \(\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\) is countable and we have a
surjection \(g:\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\to U\),
the set \(U\) is countable (the image of a countable set under a
surjection is countable).  

Thus

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \bigcup_{n\in\mathbb N}A_{n}\text{ is countable}.}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Remarks

* We used only **countable choice**: for each \(n\) we chose an
enumeration \(f_{n}\).  
If one works in ZF alone, the statement “every countable union of
countable sets is countable” is equivalent to the axiom of countable
choice, so the above construction is valid under the usual set‑theoretic
assumptions (ZF+DC or ZFC).

* The proof also shows how to produce an explicit enumeration of
\(U\): list the elements in the “diagonal” order  

\[
f_{0}(0),\, f_{0}(1),\, f_{1}(0),\, f_{0}(2),\, f_{1}(1),\, f_{2}(0),\,
f_{0}(3),\dots
\]

which traverses all pairs \((n,k)\in\mathbb N\times\mathbb N\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Hence every countable union of countable sets is countable. ∎

**Theorem.**  
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a continuous bijection.  
If \(X\) is compact and \(Y\) is Hausdorff, then \(f\) is a homeomorphism.

---

### 1.  Compact subsets of a Hausdorff space are closed  

Let \(K\subseteq Y\) be compact.  
Take \(y\notin K\).  
For every \(x\in K\) the Hausdorff condition gives disjoint open sets
\(U_x\ni x\) and \(V_x\ni y\).  
\(\{U_x:x\in K\}\) is an open cover of \(K\); choose a finite subcover
\(U_{x_1},\dots,U_{x_n}\).  
Set  
\[
V = \bigcap_{i=1}^n V_{x_i},
\qquad
U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n U_{x_i}.
\]
Then \(y\in V\), \(K\subseteq U\), and \(U\cap V=\varnothing\).
Thus \(V\) is an open neighbourhood of \(y\) disjoint from \(K\); hence
\(y\) is in the complement of \(K\).  
So every point outside \(K\) has a neighbourhood disjoint from \(K\),
i.e. \(K\) is closed.

---

### 2.  \(f\) is a closed map

Let \(C\subseteq X\) be closed.  
Because \(X\) is compact, \(C\) is compact.  
The image of a compact set under a continuous map is compact, so
\(f(C)\) is compact in \(Y\).  
By the observation above, a compact subset of a Hausdorff space is
closed.  
Hence \(f(C)\) is closed in \(Y\).

Thus \(f\) sends closed sets in \(X\) to closed sets in \(Y\); i.e. \(f\)
is a **closed map**.

---

### 3.  Continuity of the inverse

Let \(A\subseteq Y\) be closed.  
Because \(f\) is bijective, \(f^{-1}(A)\) is the unique subset of \(X\)
whose image under \(f\) is \(A\).  
Since \(f\) is closed, the image of a closed subset of \(X\) is closed in
\(Y\).  
Consequently, the preimage of a closed set under \(f^{-1}\) is closed
in \(X\).  
That is exactly the definition of continuity of \(f^{-1}\).

---

### 4.  Conclusion

We have shown:

* \(f\) is continuous (given),
* \(f\) is bijective (given),
* \(f^{-1}\) is continuous (proved above).

Therefore \(f\) is a homeomorphism between \(X\) and \(Y\).

\[
\boxed{\text{A continuous bijection }X\to Y \text{ with } X \text{ compact and } Y \text{ Hausdorff is a homeomorphism}.}
\]

Let \(f:\mathbb R\rightarrow\mathbb R\) be differentiable at every point of \(\mathbb R\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Even function  \(\Longrightarrow\)  derivative is odd  

Assume that \(f\) is **even**:
\[
f(-x)=f(x)\qquad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb R .
\]

For any \(x\) we compute the derivative of \(f\) at \(-x\) by the definition

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(-x+h)-f(-x)}{h}.
\]

Because \(f\) is even,
\[
f(-x+h)=f(-(x-h))=f(x-h),
\qquad
f(-x)=f(x).
\]
Thus

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x-h)-f(x)}{h}.
\]

Introduce \(k=-h\); when \(h\to0\) we also have \(k\to0\).  
The numerator becomes \(f(x+k)-f(x)\) and the denominator \(h=-k\). Hence

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{k\to0}\frac{f(x+k)-f(x)}{-k}
      =-\lim_{k\to0}\frac{f(x+k)-f(x)}{k}
      =-f'(x).
\]

Therefore
\[
f'(-x)=-\,f'(x)\quad\text{for all }x,
\]
i.e. the derivative of an even function is odd.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Odd function  \(\Longrightarrow\)  derivative is even  

Assume now that \(f\) is **odd**:
\[
f(-x)=-\,f(x)\qquad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb R .
\]

Again

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(-x+h)-f(-x)}{h}.
\]

Using oddness,
\[
f(-x+h)=f(-(x-h))=-\,f(x-h),\qquad
f(-x)=-\,f(x).
\]
So

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{-\,f(x-h)+f(x)}{h}
      =\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x)-f(x-h)}{h}.
\]

Let \(k=-h\); then \(k\to0\) as \(h\to0\) and

\[
f'(-x)=\lim_{k\to0}\frac{f(x)-f(x+k)}{-k}
      =\lim_{k\to0}\frac{f(x+k)-f(x)}{k}
      =f'(x).
\]

Hence

\[
f'(-x)=f'(x)\quad\text{for all }x,
\]
showing that the derivative of an odd function is even.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Summary  

- If \(f\) is even (\(f(-x)=f(x)\)), then \(f'\) is odd (\(f'(-x)=-f'(x)\)).
- If \(f\) is odd (\(f(-x)=-f(x)\)), then \(f'\) is even (\(f'(-x)=f'(x)\)).

These results hold at every point where \(f\) is differentiable. The proofs rely only on the definitions of even/odd functions and the standard limit definition of the derivative.

**Answer – Every non‑zero ideal of a PID is principal**

A *principal ideal domain* (PID) is a commutative ring \(R\) with identity such that **every** ideal of \(R\) is generated by a single element.  
Hence, if \(R\) is a PID and \(I\subseteq R\) is a non‑zero ideal, then by the very definition of a PID, \(I\) is of the form
\[
I=(a)=\{ra\mid r\in R\}
\]
for some \(a\in I\) (necessarily \(a\neq0\)).  

---

### Why the statement is not a “trivial restatement”

Although the definition of a PID already tells us that each ideal is principal, it is often useful to see *why* a non‑zero ideal can be written in that way.  
A standard constructive argument (used for Euclidean domains, which are in particular PIDs) goes as follows:

1. **Choose a non‑zero element of the ideal.**  
   Let \(I\neq\{0\}\). Pick \(a\in I,\ a\neq0\).

2. **Pick one of minimal “size”.**  
   In a Euclidean domain \(R\) there is a norm \(N:R\to\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}\) satisfying the Euclidean algorithm.  
   Consider the set \(\{\,N(x)\mid x\in I,\ x\neq0\,\}\).  
   Because \(N\) takes only non‑negative integer values, this set has a least element, say \(m=N(a)\).

3. **Show that the ideal is generated by \(a\).**  
   Let \(x\in I\). By the Euclidean algorithm, we can write
   \[
   x=qa+r \qquad\text{with } r\in R,\ N(r)<N(a)=m .
   \]
   Subtracting \(qa\) from \(x\) gives \(r=x-qa\in I\) (since \(I\) is an ideal).  
   If \(r\neq0\), then \(N(r)<m\), contradicting the minimality of \(m\).  
   Hence \(r=0\), so \(x=qa\in(a)\).

   Thus every element of \(I\) lies in \((a)\), giving \(I\subseteq(a)\).  
   Conversely, \(a\in I\), so \((a)\subseteq I\).  
   Therefore \(I=(a)\).

This argument shows concretely that any non‑zero ideal of a Euclidean domain – and therefore of any PID – is principal. It also demonstrates the constructive way to find a generator: pick an element of minimal norm.

---

### Bottom line

* In a PID, **by definition** every ideal (including every non‑zero ideal) is principal.
* For Euclidean domains (a special class of PIDs), the constructive proof above explains why this holds: any non‑zero ideal contains an element of minimal norm, and that element generates the entire ideal.

So the statement is true: **every non‑zero ideal in a PID is principal.**

Let  

\[
u\in C^{2}(\Omega ),\qquad \Delta u=0 \ \text{ in }\Omega ,
\]

and let \(B_{R}(x_{0})\subset \Omega\) be a ball of radius \(R\).
We prove that

\[
\boxed{\,u(x_{0})=\frac{1}{|\partial B_{R}(x_{0})|}
      \int_{\partial B_{R}(x_{0})}u\,dS
      =\frac{1}{|B_{R}(x_{0})|}
      \int_{B_{R}(x_{0})}u\,dx\, }         \tag{1}
\]

(the **mean–value property**).  
For simplicity we first treat the ball centred at the origin; the
general case follows by translating the coordinates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Spherical averages

For \(0<r<R\) let

\[
A(r)=\frac{1}{\omega _{n-1}}\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u(r\theta )\,d\theta ,
\qquad
\omega_{n-1}=|S^{\,n-1}| ,
\]

(the average of \(u\) on the sphere of radius \(r\)).  
Because \(u\) is \(C^{2}\), differentiation may be carried under the
integral sign.

The Laplacian in polar coordinates is

\[
\Delta u
   =u_{rr}+\frac{n-1}{r}\,u_{r}
      +\frac{1}{r^{2}}\Delta_{S}u ,
\]

where \(\Delta_{S}\) is the Laplace–Beltrami operator on \(S^{n-1}\).
Integrating this identity over the unit sphere gives

\[
0=\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\Delta u(r\theta )\,d\theta
 =\frac{d^{2}}{dr^{2}}\!\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u(r\theta )\,d\theta
   +\frac{n-1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u(r\theta )\,d\theta
   +\frac{1}{r^{2}}\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\Delta_{S}u(r\theta )\,d\theta .
\]

The last term vanishes because the integral of a Laplace–Beltrami
operator over a closed surface is zero:

\[
\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\Delta_{S}u(r\theta )\,d\theta
   =\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\operatorname{div}_{S}\nabla_{S}u(r\theta )\,d\theta
   =0 .
\]

Thus, with \(I(r)=\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u(r\theta )\,d\theta\),

\[
I''(r)+\frac{n-1}{r}\,I'(r)=0 .                                 \tag{2}
\]

Equation (2) is an ordinary differential equation for \(I\).  
Its general solution is

\[
I(r)=C_{1}+C_{2}r^{\,2-n}.                                         \tag{3}
\]

Dividing (3) by \(\omega_{n-1}\) we obtain the formula for the
spherical average:

\[
A(r)=\frac{C_{1}}{\omega_{n-1}}
      +\frac{C_{2}}{\omega_{n-1}}\,r^{\,2-n}.                       \tag{4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Determination of the constants

The continuity of \(u\) at the centre \(0\) implies that
\(A(r)\) stays bounded as \(r\to 0\).  
In (4) the term \(r^{2-n}\) blows up unless \(C_{2}=0\)
(\(n\ge 2\); for \(n=1\) the formula is trivial).  
Hence \(C_{2}=0\) and

\[
A(r)=\frac{C_{1}}{\omega_{n-1}}\qquad (0<r<R).
\]

Letting \(r\to 0\) gives \(A(0)=u(0)\) by continuity, and therefore
\(C_{1}=\omega_{n-1}u(0)\).  Consequently

\[
A(r)=u(0)\qquad (0<r<R).
\]

Thus the average of \(u\) over every sphere centred at the origin
equals the value of \(u\) at the centre:

\[
\frac{1}{|\partial B_{r}(0)|}\int_{\partial B_{r}(0)}u\,dS=u(0).
\]

Translating back to an arbitrary centre \(x_{0}\) gives the first
identity in (1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Mean value over the ball

Write the average over the ball in terms of spherical averages:

\[
\frac{1}{|B_{R}|}\int_{B_{R}}u(x)\,dx
 =\frac{1}{\omega_{n}R^{n}}
   \int_{0}^{R}\!\!\left(\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u(r\theta )\,d\theta\right)
   r^{\,n-1}\,dr
 =\frac{1}{\omega_{n}R^{n}}
   \int_{0}^{R} A(r)\,\omega_{n-1} r^{\,n-1}\,dr .
\]

Using \(A(r)=u(0)\) we get

\[
\frac{1}{|B_{R}|}\int_{B_{R}}u\,dx
 =\frac{u(0)}{\omega_{n}R^{n}}
   \int_{0}^{R}\omega_{n-1} r^{\,n-1}\,dr
 =u(0).
\]

This proves the second identity in (1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Conclusion

For every ball \(B_{R}(x_{0})\subset\Omega\),

\[
u(x_{0})=\frac{1}{|\partial B_{R}(x_{0})|}
          \int_{\partial B_{R}(x_{0})}u\,dS
          =\frac{1}{|B_{R}(x_{0})|}
          \int_{B_{R}(x_{0})}u\,dx .
\]

Hence every harmonic function satisfies the mean value property.  
∎

Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space.  
Recall:

* **Compactness** – every open cover of \(X\) has a finite subcover.  
* **Sequential compactness** – every sequence in \(X\) has a convergent subsequence.  
  In metric spaces compactness \(\Longleftrightarrow\) sequential compactness.

We prove that compactness forces both completeness and total boundedness.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Compact  \(\Longrightarrow\)  Complete  

Let \((x_n)\) be a Cauchy sequence in \(X\).  
By sequential compactness there exists a subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) and a point \(x\in X\) such that  

\[
x_{n_k}\xrightarrow{k\to\infty}x .
\]

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Because \((x_n)\) is Cauchy, there is \(N\) with  

\[
d(x_n,x_m)<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\qquad(n,m\ge N).
\]

Choose \(k\) large enough so that \(n_k\ge N\) and  

\[
d(x_{n_k},x)<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]

For any \(n\ge N\),

\[
d(x_n,x)\le d(x_n,x_{n_k})+d(x_{n_k},x)<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}=\varepsilon .
\]

Hence \(x_n\to x\).  Therefore every Cauchy sequence converges in \(X\); \(X\) is complete.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Compact  \(\Longrightarrow\)  Totally bounded  

Assume, to obtain a contradiction, that \(X\) is **not** totally bounded.  
Then there exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that no finite family of
\(\varepsilon\)-balls covers \(X\).  

Construct a sequence \((x_n)\) inductively:

* Pick any \(x_1\in X\).
* Having chosen \(x_1,\dots ,x_{n-1}\), the union
  \(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} B(x_i,\varepsilon)\) does not cover \(X\); hence
  there is a point \(x_n\in X\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} B(x_i,\varepsilon)\).
  Thus \(d(x_n,x_i)\ge\varepsilon\) for every \(i<n\).

Consequently

\[
d(x_i,x_j)\ge\varepsilon\qquad\text{for all }i\neq j .
\]

The sequence \((x_n)\) cannot have a Cauchy subsequence, because two distinct
terms are at least \(\varepsilon\) apart.  
But in a sequentially compact space every sequence must have a convergent (hence Cauchy) subsequence.  
This contradiction shows that the assumption was false; hence \(X\) **is** totally bounded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Conclusion  

Every compact metric space is sequentially compact, and the two properties
proved above imply that it is both complete and totally bounded. ∎

Let  

\[
E(z)=e^{\,z},\qquad C(z)=\cos z,\qquad S(z)=\sin z \qquad (z\in \mathbb C).
\]

All three functions are entire (analytic everywhere).  
Hence their Taylor series about the origin are obtained by differentiating
at \(z=0\) and dividing by \(n!\):

\[
\boxed{\;E(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{z^{\,n}}{n!}\;},\qquad
\boxed{\;C(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{z^{\,2k}}{(2k)!}\;},\qquad
\boxed{\;S(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{z^{\,2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}\;}.
\]

These series converge for every complex number (their radius of convergence is
\(\infty\)).  
Therefore we may substitute \(z=i\theta\) (\(\theta\in\mathbb R\)) in the first
series and rearrange the resulting terms because the series is absolutely
convergent.

---

### 1.  Expand \(e^{i\theta}\)

\[
e^{i\theta}= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(i\theta)^{n}}{n!}
          = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{i^{\,n}\theta^{\,n}}{n!}.
\]

Write the terms with \(n=2k\) (even) and \(n=2k+1\) (odd):

* **Even \(n=2k\):**  
  \(i^{\,2k}= (i^{2})^{k}=(-1)^{k}\).  
  The contribution is \((-1)^{k}\displaystyle\frac{\theta^{\,2k}}{(2k)!}\).

* **Odd \(n=2k+1\):**  
  \(i^{\,2k+1}=i\,i^{\,2k}=i(-1)^{k}\).  
  The contribution is \(i\,(-1)^{k}\displaystyle\frac{\theta^{\,2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}\).

Hence

\[
e^{i\theta}= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{\theta^{\,2k}}{(2k)!}
            + i\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{\theta^{\,2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}.
\]

---

### 2.  Recognize the Taylor series for \(\cos\theta\) and \(\sin\theta\)

By the formulas displayed above,

\[
\cos\theta=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{\theta^{\,2k}}{(2k)!},
\qquad
\sin\theta=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\frac{\theta^{\,2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}.
\]

Thus the two series that appear in the expression for \(e^{i\theta}\)
are exactly \(\cos\theta\) and \(\sin\theta\), respectively.

---

### 3.  Conclude Euler’s formula

Putting the two observations together,

\[
e^{i\theta}
= \cos\theta + i\,\sin\theta .
\]

Because the power series for \(e^{z}\), \(\cos z\), and \(\sin z\) converge
absolutely for every complex \(z\), all manipulations above are justified.
Therefore Euler’s identity holds for every real \(\theta\); by analytic
continuation it holds for every complex \(\theta\) as well.

\[
\boxed{\,e^{i\theta}= \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\,}
\]

**Theorem.**  
For every real (or complex, or field‑valued) matrix \(A\) we have  

\[
\rank(A)=\rank(A^{\mathsf T}).
\]

In other words, the column rank of \(A\) equals the row rank of \(A\), and
the transpose does not change the rank.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof (using elementary row/column operations)

Let \(A\) be an \(m\times n\) matrix.  
Apply a sequence of elementary row operations to reduce \(A\) to reduced
row‑echelon form \(R\).  
Row operations do **not** change the row space of a matrix, and they do
change the column space only in a very controlled way, but the crucial
fact is:

\[
\rank(A)=\rank(R).
\]

The matrix \(R\) has the following structure (after reordering rows if
necessary):

\[
R=\begin{bmatrix}
I_r & 0 \\
0   & 0
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad 0\le r\le \min(m,n).
\]

Here \(I_r\) is the \(r\times r\) identity matrix.  
The integer \(r\) is the number of pivot columns in \(R\) and equals
\(\rank(A)\).

Now take the transpose:

\[
R^{\mathsf T}=
\begin{bmatrix}
I_r & 0\\
0   & 0
\end{bmatrix}^{\mathsf T}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
I_r & 0\\
0   & 0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

so \(R^{\mathsf T}\) has the same shape and the same rank \(r\).
Because \(R^{\mathsf T}\) is the reduced row‑echelon form of \(A^{\mathsf T}\)
(the transpose of a reduced row‑echelon matrix is a reduced
column‑echelon matrix, and the sequence of elementary column operations
used to obtain it are exactly the transpose of the row operations that
produced \(R\)), we have

\[
\rank(A^{\mathsf T})=\rank(R^{\mathsf T})=r.
\]

Since \(\rank(A)=r\) as well, we conclude

\[
\rank(A)=\rank(A^{\mathsf T}).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Alternative viewpoint (linear–map argument)

Let \(A\colon \mathbb{F}^n\to\mathbb{F}^m\) be the linear map defined by
\(A(x)=Ax\).  
Its transpose \(A^{\mathsf T}\colon \mathbb{F}^m\to\mathbb{F}^n\) is the
adjoint map with respect to the standard dot product.  
By the rank–nullity theorem

\[
\rank(A)+\dim\ker(A)=n,\qquad
\rank(A^{\mathsf T})+\dim\ker(A^{\mathsf T})=m.
\]

Moreover, \(\ker(A^{\mathsf T})=\operatorname{row}(A)^{\perp}\) and
\(\operatorname{row}(A)=\operatorname{col}(A^{\mathsf T})\).  
A short calculation shows

\[
\rank(A^{\mathsf T}) = n-\dim\ker(A)=\rank(A).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Thus the rank of a matrix is invariant under transposition:  

\[
\boxed{\;\rank(A)=\rank(A^{\mathsf T})\;}
\]

for every matrix \(A\) over any field.

**Theorem (Heine–Cantor).**  
Let \(f:[a,b]\rightarrow\mathbb {R}\) be continuous on the closed bounded interval  
\([a,b]\).  Then \(f\) is *uniformly continuous* on \([a,b]\); that is,

\[
\forall\,\varepsilon>0\ \exists\,\delta>0\text{ such that }
|x-y|<\delta \implies |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon
\qquad(x,y\in[a,b]).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof (by contradiction and sequences)

Assume that \(f\) is **not** uniformly continuous.  
Then there exists \(\varepsilon_{0}>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\) we can
find points \(x,y\in[a,b]\) with

\[
|x-y|<\delta \quad\text{and}\quad |f(x)-f(y)|\ge\varepsilon_{0}.
\tag{1}
\]

Choose \(\delta=\tfrac{1}{n}\) for \(n=1,2,\dots\).  
From (1) we obtain sequences \((x_{n})\) and \((y_{n})\) in \([a,b]\) satisfying

\[
|x_{n}-y_{n}|<\frac{1}{n}, \qquad |f(x_{n})-f(y_{n})|\ge\varepsilon_{0}
\qquad(n=1,2,\dots).
\tag{2}
\]

Because \([a,b]\) is compact, the bounded sequence \((x_{n})\) has a convergent
subsequence; denote it again by \((x_{n})\) and let  

\[
x_{n}\longrightarrow c\in[a,b].
\]

From \(|x_{n}-y_{n}|<1/n\) we also have  

\[
|y_{n}-c|\le |y_{n}-x_{n}|+|x_{n}-c|\longrightarrow 0,
\]

so \(y_{n}\to c\) as well.

Since \(f\) is continuous at \(c\),

\[
f(x_{n})\longrightarrow f(c)\quad\text{and}\quad f(y_{n})\longrightarrow f(c).
\]

Consequently,

\[
|f(x_{n})-f(y_{n})|\longrightarrow 0.
\]

But by (2) each term of the sequence is at least \(\varepsilon_{0}\), which is a
positive number.  This contradiction shows that our assumption was false.

Therefore the function \(f\) **is uniformly continuous** on \([a,b]\). ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Alternate proof via compactness (open‑cover argument)

For \(\varepsilon>0\) and each \(x\in[a,b]\) continuity gives a \(\delta(x)>0\) such that  

\[
|x-y|<\delta(x)\;\Rightarrow\;|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon .
\]

Let \(U_{x}=(x-\tfrac{\delta(x)}{2},\,x+\tfrac{\delta(x)}{2})\).
The family \(\{U_{x}\}_{x\in[a,b]}\) is an open cover of the compact set \([a,b]\);
hence a finite subcover \(U_{x_{1}},\dots,U_{x_{m}}\) exists.
Set  

\[
\delta=\min_{1\le i\le m}\frac{\delta(x_{i})}{2}>0 .
\]

If \(|x-y|<\delta\), then \(x\) lies in some \(U_{x_{i}}\); because
\(\delta\le\frac{\delta(x_{i})}{2}\), the ball \(U_{x_{i}}\) contains \(y\) as well,
and thus \(|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon\).
This gives the same uniform continuity conclusion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence any continuous function on a compact interval is uniformly continuous. ∎

**Theorem.**  
If \(R\) is a finite integral domain, then \(R\) is a field.

---

### Proof

Let \(R\) be an integral domain.  
By definition

* \(R\) is a commutative ring with identity \(1\neq 0\);
* \(R\) has no zero–divisors, i.e.  
  \(ab=0 \Rightarrow a=0 \text{ or } b=0\).

Let \(a\in R,\;a\neq 0\).  
Consider the map

\[
f_a : R \longrightarrow R,\qquad f_a(x)=ax .
\]

**1.  \(f_a\) is injective.**  
If \(f_a(x)=f_a(y)\), then \(ax=ay\).  
Subtracting gives \(a(x-y)=0\).  
Since \(a\neq0\) and \(R\) has no zero–divisors, we must have \(x-y=0\); hence \(x=y\).

**2.  \(f_a\) is surjective.**  
\(R\) is finite, so an injective map from a finite set to itself is automatically surjective.  
Thus \(f_a\) is a bijection.

**3.  Existence of a multiplicative inverse.**  
Because \(f_a\) is surjective, there is some \(b\in R\) with

\[
f_a(b)=ab=1 .
\]

Thus \(a\) has a right inverse \(b\).  
But \(R\) is commutative, so \(ab=ba=1\); \(b\) is also a left inverse.  
Hence every non‑zero element of \(R\) is a unit.

Since \(R\) already satisfies commutativity, contains an identity \(1\neq0\), and every non‑zero element is a unit, \(R\) is a field.

\(\square\)

---

### Remarks

* The finiteness is crucial: in an infinite integral domain a non‑zero element need not have a multiplicative inverse (e.g. \(\mathbb{Z}\)).
* The argument uses only that the multiplication map \(x\mapsto ax\) is injective; in any finite set injective \(\Rightarrow\) surjective, so the map is a permutation.  
  Consequently the non‑zero elements of a finite integral domain form an abelian group under multiplication, exactly the multiplicative group of a field.

**Theorem.**  
The set  

\[
\mathbb A=\{\,\alpha\in \mathbb C\mid \alpha \text{ is a root of a non‑zero polynomial } 
p(x)=a_nx^n+\dots +a_1x+a_0,\;a_i\in\mathbb Z\,\}
\]

is countable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Countability of the set of integer polynomials

For a fixed positive integer \(n\) denote

\[
P_n=\{\,p(x)=a_nx^n+\cdots +a_1x+a_0\mid a_i\in\mathbb Z,\;a_n\neq0\,\}.
\]

The coefficient vector \((a_n,a_{n-1},\dots ,a_0)\) belongs to  
\(\mathbb Z^{\,n+1}\setminus\{0\}\).  
Since \(\mathbb Z\) is countable, the Cartesian product \(\mathbb Z^{\,n+1}\) is countable (by induction on \(n+1\) using the Cantor pairing function).  
Removing the single point \((0,\dots ,0)\) does not change countability; hence each \(P_n\) is countable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Finite number of roots for each polynomial

Let \(p\in P_n\).  
By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, \(p\) has exactly \(n\) roots in \(\mathbb C\), counted with multiplicity.  
Thus the set  

\[
Z(p)=\{\alpha\in\mathbb C\mid p(\alpha)=0\}
\]

is a finite set of cardinality \(\le n\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  The algebraic numbers are a countable union of finite sets

Write

\[
\mathbb A=\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}\;\bigcup_{p\in P_n} Z(p).
\]

For each fixed \(n\) the inner union \(\bigcup_{p\in P_n} Z(p)\) is a union of countably many finite sets; a countable union of finite sets is countable.  
Since \(\mathbb A\) is a countable union (over \(n\)) of countable sets, it is countable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Explicit enumeration (optional)

We can produce an explicit listing:

1. Enumerate \(\mathbb Z^{\,n+1}\setminus\{0\}\) for each \(n\) (e.g. by Cantor’s pairing or by interleaving prime exponents).
2. For each polynomial \(p\) in that enumeration, use the fundamental theorem of algebra to compute its roots (they are the solutions of a polynomial equation).  
3. Append the roots of \(p\) to the list, ignoring repetitions.

The resulting list contains every algebraic number exactly once, proving countability constructively.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence **the set of all algebraic numbers is countable.**

Let \((X,\mathcal M)\) be a measurable space and let  

\[
f,g:X\longrightarrow \mathbb R\cup\{\pm\infty\}
\]

be measurable.  
We prove that the pointwise sum  

\[
h(x):=f(x)+g(x), \qquad x\in X,
\]

is measurable.  
The proof uses only the definition of measurability in terms of
pre‑images of half–lines.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Characterisation of measurability

A real (or extended–real) function \(u\) is measurable iff for every
\(a\in\mathbb R\)

\[
\{x\in X:\;u(x)<a\}\in\mathcal M .
\]

(Equivalently, for all \(a\) the set \(\{u>a\}\) is measurable; either
characterisation works.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The set \(\{h<a\}\)

Fix \(a\in\mathbb R\).  
We claim

\[
\{x\in X: f(x)+g(x)<a\}
   =\bigcup_{r\in\mathbb Q}\bigl(\{f<r\}\cap\{g<a-r\}\bigr).   \tag{1}
\]

**Why (1) holds.**

*If \(x\) satisfies \(f(x)+g(x)<a\).*  
Because the rationals are dense in \(\mathbb R\), we can choose a
rational number \(r\) with  

\[
f(x)<r< a-g(x).
\]

Then \(g(x)<a-r\), so \(x\in\{f<r\}\cap\{g<a-r\}\).
Hence \(x\) belongs to the right‑hand side of (1).

*If \(x\) is in the right‑hand side of (1).*  
There is a rational \(r\) with \(x\in\{f<r\}\cap\{g<a-r\}\).  
Thus \(f(x)<r\) and \(g(x)<a-r\). Adding these inequalities gives  

\[
f(x)+g(x)<r+(a-r)=a,
\]

so \(x\) satisfies the left‑hand side of (1).

Therefore (1) is valid.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Measurability of \(h\)

Because \(f\) and \(g\) are measurable, for every real number \(c\)

\[
\{f<c\},\; \{g<c\}\in\mathcal M .
\]

Consequently, for each rational \(r\),

\[
\{f<r\}\cap\{g<a-r\}\in\mathcal M .
\]

The family \(\{r\in\mathbb Q\}\) is countable; a countable union of
measurable sets is measurable.  
Thus the right‑hand side of (1) is measurable, and by (1) the left‑hand
side, \(\{h<a\}\), is measurable for every \(a\in\mathbb R\).

Hence \(h=f+g\) satisfies the defining property of a measurable
function, so \(h\) is measurable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Remarks

* The same argument works for any finite sum or any linear
combination \(a\,f+b\,g\) with real constants \(a,b\).

* If one prefers a functional-analytic view, note that the map  
  \(\Phi:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R,\;\Phi(u,v)=u+v\) is continuous,
  hence Borel‑measurable.  
  Since \((f,g):X\to\mathbb R^2\) is measurable, the composition
  \(\Phi\circ(f,g)=f+g\) is measurable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Conclusion.**  
The pointwise sum of two measurable functions is measurable.

Let  

\[
f:X\longrightarrow Y
\]

be a continuous map, \(X\) compact and \(Y\) Hausdorff, and suppose that
\(f\) is injective.  
We have to show that the restriction

\[
f:X\;\longrightarrow\;f(X)
\]

is a homeomorphism, i.e. that \(f^{-1}:f(X)\to X\) is continuous.  
Since \(f\) is already a bijection onto its image, it suffices to prove that
\(f\) is a **closed map** onto \(f(X)\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Images of closed sets are closed.

Let \(C\subseteq X\) be closed.  
Because \(X\) is compact, \(C\) is compact.  
By continuity, \(f(C)\) is a compact subset of \(Y\).  
In a Hausdorff space compact sets are closed; hence \(f(C)\) is closed in
\(Y\).  
Since \(f(C)\subseteq f(X)\), it is also closed in the subspace topology of
\(f(X)\).

Thus for every closed \(C\subseteq X\) the set \(f(C)\) is closed in
\(f(X)\). This is precisely the statement that \(f\) is a closed map onto
\(f(X)\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Continuity of the inverse.

Take an open set \(U\subseteq X\).  
Write \(U=X\setminus C\) where \(C=X\setminus U\) is closed in \(X\).  
Using the injectivity of \(f\),

\[
f(U)=f\bigl(X\setminus C\bigr)=f(X)\setminus f(C).
\]

Since \(f(C)\) is closed in \(f(X)\), its complement \(f(U)\) is open in
\(f(X)\).  
Hence the pre‑image under \(f^{-1}\) of every open set in \(X\) is open in
\(f(X)\); equivalently, \(f^{-1}:f(X)\to X\) is continuous.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Because \(f\) is a continuous bijection from \(X\) onto \(f(X)\) whose
inverse is continuous, \(f\) is a homeomorphism onto its image.  
Therefore \(f\) is an **embedding**.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

So every injective continuous map from a compact space into a Hausdorff
space is automatically a topological embedding. ∎

**Theorem (Cauchy’s theorem for \(p=2\)).**  
If \(G\) is a finite group and \(|G|\) is even, then there exists an element  
\(g\in G,\; g\neq e\) with \(g^{2}=e\); equivalently, \(G\) contains an element of
order \(2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof

Let \(G\) be a finite group of even order and write \(e\) for the identity
element.

1. **Pairing inverses.**  
   For every \(x\in G\) we have an inverse \(x^{-1}\in G\).
   If \(x\neq e\) and \(x\neq x^{-1}\), then \(x\) and \(x^{-1}\) are distinct
   elements of \(G\).
   Thus all elements of \(G\) that are not \(e\) and not involutions can be
   grouped into disjoint pairs  
   \[
   \{x,\;x^{-1}\}.
   \]
   Each such pair contributes \(2\) elements to \(|G|\).

2. **What would happen if no involution existed?**  
   Suppose, for a contradiction, that \(G\) contains **no** element of order
   \(2\).  
   Then the only element that equals its inverse is the identity \(e\).
   Consequently every element of \(G\setminus\{e\}\) can be placed into a
   distinct pair \(\{x,x^{-1}\}\).
   Hence
   \[
   |G|-1 \quad\text{is even},
   \]
   and adding the single element \(e\) would make \(|G|\) odd.

3. **Contradiction.**  
   But \(|G|\) was assumed even.  
   Therefore our supposition that no involution exists is impossible.

4. **Conclusion.**  
   There must exist an element \(a\neq e\) with \(a=a^{-1}\).  
   This implies \(a^{2}=e\), and \(a\) is not the identity, so the
   order of \(a\) is exactly \(2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence every finite group of even order contains an element of order \(2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

*Remark.*  
This argument is precisely the case \(p=2\) of Cauchy’s theorem, which
states that if a prime \(p\) divides the order of a finite group \(G\),
then \(G\) contains an element of order \(p\).  The proof above
illustrates the simplest possible form of that theorem.

**Theorem (Finite–dimensional norm equivalence).**  
Let \(\|\cdot\|_{a}\) and \(\|\cdot\|_{b}\) be two norms on \(\mathbb {R}^{n}\).  
Then there exist constants \(m,M>0\) such that

\[
m\,\|x\|_{a}\le \|x\|_{b}\le M\,\|x\|_{a}\qquad\text{for every }x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}.
\]

In other words, every two norms on a finite–dimensional vector space are equivalent.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof

Let  

\[
S_{a}:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\;:\;\|x\|_{a}=1\}
\]

be the unit sphere with respect to \(\|\cdot\|_{a}\).

---

#### 1.  Compactness of \(S_{a}\)

Because \(\|\cdot\|_{a}\) is a norm, \(S_{a}\) is closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\).  
In \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\) a set is compact iff it is closed and bounded (Heine–Borel theorem).  
Hence \(S_{a}\) is compact.

---

#### 2.  The ratio function on \(S_{a}\)

Define  

\[
f:S_{a}\longrightarrow (0,\infty),\qquad
f(x)=\frac{\|x\|_{b}}{\|x\|_{a}}=\|x\|_{b}\quad(\text{because }\|x\|_{a}=1\text{ on }S_{a}).
\]

Since \(x\mapsto\|x\|_{b}\) is continuous and \(\|x\|_{a}=1\) on \(S_{a}\),  
\(f\) is continuous on the compact set \(S_{a}\).  
Therefore, by the extreme value theorem, \(f\) attains a minimum \(m\) and a maximum \(M\) on \(S_{a}\):

\[
0<m=\min_{x\in S_{a}}f(x)\le \max_{x\in S_{a}}f(x)=M<\infty .
\]

(The minimum is strictly positive because \(f(x)>0\) for all \(x\neq 0\); if \(m=0\) a
contradiction would arise as explained below.)

---

#### 3.  Strict positivity of the minimum

Suppose, for contradiction, that \(m=0\).  
Then there exists a sequence \(\{x_{k}\}\subset S_{a}\) such that
\(f(x_{k})=\|x_{k}\|_{b}\to 0\).
Since \(S_{a}\) is compact, \(\{x_{k}\}\) has a convergent subsequence \(x_{k_{j}}\to x\) with \(x\in S_{a}\).
By continuity of \(\|\cdot\|_{b}\), \(\|x\|_{b}=\lim \|x_{k_{j}}\|_{b}=0\).
But a norm is positive definite, so \(\|x\|_{b}=0\) implies \(x=0\), contradicting \(x\in S_{a}\).
Hence \(m>0\).

---

#### 4.  The inequality for arbitrary \(x\)

Take any \(x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\), \(x\neq 0\).  
Set \(y:=x/\|x\|_{a}\); then \(\|y\|_{a}=1\), i.e. \(y\in S_{a}\).  
Using the definition of \(f\),

\[
\|x\|_{b}= \|x\|_{a}\,\|y\|_{b}
          = \|x\|_{a}\,f(y)
          \quad\text{with }\, m\le f(y)\le M .
\]

Thus

\[
m\,\|x\|_{a}\le \|x\|_{b}\le M\,\|x\|_{a}\qquad (x\neq 0).
\]

For \(x=0\) the inequalities hold trivially because both sides equal \(0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Conclusion

We have found positive constants \(m,M\) such that

\[
m\,\|x\|_{a}\le \|x\|_{b}\le M\,\|x\|_{a}\qquad\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}.
\]

Hence the two norms \(\|\cdot\|_{a}\) and \(\|\cdot\|_{b}\) are equivalent.  
Since the choice of norms was arbitrary, *every* pair of norms on \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\) is equivalent. ∎



*(The same argument works for any finite–dimensional real or complex vector space.)*

**Claim.**  
The space  
\[
C[0,1]=\{\,f:[0,1]\to\mathbb {R}\mid f\ \text{continuous}\,\}
\]
equipped with the supremum (uniform) norm  
\[
\|f\|_{\infty}\;=\;\sup _{x\in[0,1]}|f(x)|
\]
is a Banach space; that is, \((C[0,1],\|\cdot\|_{\infty})\) is complete.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  The supremum norm is a norm and defines a metric

For \(f,g\in C[0,1]\)

* \(\|f\|_{\infty}\ge 0\) and \(\|f\|_{\infty}=0\iff f\equiv 0\).  
* \(\|\alpha f\|_{\infty}=|\alpha|\|f\|_{\infty}\) for any scalar \(\alpha\).  
* \(\|f+g\|_{\infty}\le \|f\|_{\infty}+\|g\|_{\infty}\) (triangle inequality).  

Hence \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty}\) is a norm.  
Define \(d(f,g)=\|f-g\|_{\infty}\); the properties above imply that \(d\) is a metric.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Cauchy sequences in \((C[0,1],\|\cdot\|_{\infty})\)

Let \((f_{n})\) be a Cauchy sequence, i.e. for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there is \(N\) such that

\[
\|f_{n}-f_{m}\|_{\infty}<\varepsilon \qquad(n,m\ge N).
\]

Fix \(x\in[0,1]\).  
For \(n,m\ge N\),

\[
|f_{n}(x)-f_{m}(x)|\le\|f_{n}-f_{m}\|_{\infty}<\varepsilon ,
\]

hence \(\bigl(f_{n}(x)\bigr)_{n}\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) and therefore converges.  
Define

\[
f(x)\;=\;\lim_{n\to\infty}f_{n}(x),\qquad x\in[0,1].
\]

Thus we obtain a pointwise limit function \(f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Uniform convergence of \(f_{n}\) to \(f\)

Let \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Choose \(N\) such that \(\|f_{n}-f_{m}\|_{\infty}<\varepsilon\) for all \(n,m\ge N\).  
Fix \(n\ge N\) and let \(m\to\infty\).  
Because \(f_{m}\to f\) pointwise, \(\|f_{n}-f\|_{\infty}\le\varepsilon\).  
Hence

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\|f_{n}-f\|_{\infty}=0,
\]

i.e. \(f_{n}\to f\) uniformly on \([0,1]\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Continuity of the limit function

Uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous.  
To prove it directly: let \(x_{0}\in[0,1]\) and \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Choose \(N\) such that \(\|f_{n}-f\|_{\infty}<\varepsilon/3\) for all \(n\ge N\).  
Since \(f_{N}\) is continuous, there exists \(\delta>0\) such that

\[
|x-x_{0}|<\delta \;\Longrightarrow\; |f_{N}(x)-f_{N}(x_{0})|<\varepsilon/3 .
\]

Then for \(|x-x_{0}|<\delta\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
|f(x)-f(x_{0})|
&\le |f(x)-f_{N}(x)|+|f_{N}(x)-f_{N}(x_{0})|+|f_{N}(x_{0})-f(x_{0})|\\
&< \varepsilon/3+\varepsilon/3+\varepsilon/3=\varepsilon .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \(f\) is continuous at \(x_{0}\). Since \(x_{0}\) was arbitrary, \(f\in C[0,1]\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Completeness

We have shown: every Cauchy sequence \((f_{n})\subset C[0,1]\) converges in the
sup‑norm to a function \(f\in C[0,1]\).  
Therefore \((C[0,1],\|\cdot\|_{\infty})\) is complete.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 6.  Alternative short proof

A classical shortcut is:  
\(C[0,1]\) is a closed subspace of the Banach space \((B[0,1],\|\cdot\|_{\infty})\),  
where \(B[0,1]\) denotes all bounded real–valued functions on \([0,1]\).  
The set \(\{f\in B[0,1]: f\text{ is continuous}\}\) is closed in \(B[0,1]\) (because if
\(f_{n}\to f\) uniformly and each \(f_{n}\) is continuous, then \(f\) is continuous).  
Since closed subspaces of Banach spaces are Banach, \(C[0,1]\) is Banach.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence **the space of continuous functions on \([0,1]\) with the supremum norm is a Banach space.**

Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a simple graph and let  

\[
n=|V|\qquad (n>1).
\]

For every vertex \(v\in V\) denote by \(\deg(v)\) its degree.  
Because \(G\) is simple, no loops and no multiple edges are allowed, so

\[
0\le \deg(v)\le n-1 ,\qquad\forall v\in V .
\tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Claim
There exist two distinct vertices of \(G\) having the same degree.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Proof

Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that all vertex degrees are different.  
Then the \(n\) numbers \(\deg(v)\;(v\in V)\) are \(n\) distinct integers chosen
from the set \(\{0,1,\dots ,n-1\}\).  
The only way to choose \(n\) distinct integers from a set of \(n\) consecutive
integers is to take **every** integer in that set; thus

\[
\{\deg(v)\mid v\in V\}=\{0,1,2,\dots ,n-1\}. \tag{2}
\]

In particular, there is a vertex \(x\) with \(\deg(x)=n-1\) and a vertex
\(y\) with \(\deg(y)=0\).

But a vertex of degree \(n-1\) is adjacent to every other vertex, so each
other vertex (in particular \(y\)) has degree at least \(1\).
Hence \(\deg(y)\neq 0\), contradicting (2).

Therefore our assumption that all vertex degrees are distinct is impossible,
and there must be at least two vertices with the same degree. \(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Remark

The proof works for every simple graph with at least two vertices.  
The key point is that the degree of a vertex can take only the
\(n\) values \(0,1,\dots ,n-1\), and if one of them is \(n-1\) the value \(0\)
cannot appear.  Thus the set of degrees cannot contain all \(n\) values,
and consequently at least two vertices must share a degree.

Let \(V\) be an inner‑product space over \(\mathbb {R}\) or \(\mathbb {C}\) and let  
\(W\subset V\) be a finite–dimensional subspace.  
We prove that \(W\) is a closed subset of \(V\) (equivalently, that it is
complete).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  A convenient basis

Because \(W\) is finite–dimensional, we can apply the Gram–Schmidt process
to obtain an **orthonormal** basis
\[
u_{1},u_{2},\dots ,u_{n}\qquad (n=\dim W)
\]
of \(W\).  
For every \(x\in W\) the coordinates
\[
\alpha_{i}(x)=\langle x,u_{i}\rangle ,\qquad i=1,\dots ,n ,
\]
are unique and satisfy
\[
x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_{i}(x)\,u_{i},\qquad 
\|x\|^{2}= \sum_{i=1}^{n}|\alpha_{i}(x)|^{2}.                     \tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Cauchy sequences in \(W\)

Let \(\{x_{k}\}\) be a Cauchy sequence in \(W\).
From (1) we obtain, for each \(i\),

\[
|\alpha_{i}(x_{k})-\alpha_{i}(x_{l})|
   =|\langle x_{k}-x_{l},u_{i}\rangle|
   \le \|x_{k}-x_{l}\|.
\]

Since \(\{x_{k}\}\) is Cauchy, the right–hand side tends to \(0\) as
\(k,l\to\infty\); hence each scalar sequence
\(\{\alpha_{i}(x_{k})\}\) is Cauchy in \(\mathbb {F}\)
(\(\mathbb {F}=\mathbb {R}\) or \(\mathbb {C}\)).  
Completeness of \(\mathbb {F}\) gives limits \(\alpha_{i}\in\mathbb {F}\).

Define
\[
y:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_{i}\,u_{i}\in W .
\]
Using (1) again we see that
\[
\|x_{k}-y\|^{2}= \sum_{i=1}^{n}|\alpha_{i}(x_{k})-\alpha_{i}|^{2}\longrightarrow 0
\]
as \(k\to\infty\).  
Thus \(x_{k}\to y\) in \(V\), showing that every Cauchy sequence in \(W\)
converges to a point of \(W\).  
Hence **\(W\) is complete**.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Completeness implies closedness

A subspace of a normed space is closed iff it is complete.  
(If a closed set contains the limit of every convergent sequence from it,
then any Cauchy sequence converges to a point in the set, giving completeness;
conversely, completeness implies that limits of convergent sequences in the
ambient space lie in the subspace, so the subspace is closed.)

Since we have shown that \(W\) is complete, it follows that \(W\) is a
closed subset of \(V\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\boxed{\text{Every finite‑dimensional subspace of an inner‑product space is closed.}}
\]

(Indeed, the same argument works for any normed space, not just for
inner‑product spaces.)

**Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups**

> Let \(G\) be a finite abelian group.  
>  Then  
>  \[
>  G\;\cong\;\bigoplus_{i=1}^{t}\;\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k_i}
>  \mathbf Z/p_i^{\,n_{ij}}\mathbf Z ,
>  \]
>  where the \(p_i\)’s are distinct primes, each \(n_{ij}\ge 1\), and
>  the sum is a direct sum of cyclic groups of prime‑power order.

The proof is usually split into two steps.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1. Primary decomposition

Let \(|G|=\prod_{i=1}^{t}p_i^{\,e_i}\) be the prime factorisation of the
order of \(G\).
For a prime \(p\) put  

\[
G_p=\{g\in G\mid p^n g=0\text{ for some }n\ge 0\}.
\]

\(G_p\) is a subgroup of \(G\) (and is called the *\(p\)-primary component*).
The following facts are elementary group–theoretic facts.

*Lemma 1.*  
If \(p\neq q\) are primes, then \(G_p\cap G_q=\{0\}\).

*Proof.*  
Let \(x\in G_p\cap G_q\).  
Then \(p^a x=0\) and \(q^b x=0\) for some \(a,b\).
Since \(p\) and \(q\) are coprime, there are integers
\(u,v\) with \(up^a+vq^b=1\).  Hence

\[
x=(up^a+vq^b)x
  =up^ax+vq^bx=0.
\]
∎

*Lemma 2.*  
For each \(i\), the map  

\[
\phi_i : G_{p_i}\longrightarrow G,\qquad
\phi_i(x)=x
\]

is a monomorphism, and  

\[
G=\sum_{i=1}^{t} G_{p_i}.
\]

*Proof.*  
Let \(g\in G\).  Because \(|G|=\prod p_i^{e_i}\),
\[
g=\sum_{i=1}^{t}
   \bigl(\prod_{j\neq i}p_j^{\,e_j}\bigr) g .
\]
The \(i\)-th summand is killed by a power of \(p_i\), hence lies in
\(G_{p_i}\).  
Thus \(g\) is a sum of elements from the \(G_{p_i}\)’s, giving
surjectivity.  Injectivity is clear.  The intersection
\(\bigcap_i G_{p_i}=\{0\}\) follows from Lemma 1, so the sum is direct.
∎

Hence

\[
\boxed{G\;\cong\;\bigoplus_{i=1}^{t} G_{p_i}}
\tag{1}
\]

The groups \(G_{p_i}\) are called *\(p\)-primary components*; each of
them is a finite abelian \(p\)-group (every element has order a power of
\(p\)).  
So it remains to decompose a finite abelian \(p\)-group into cyclic
\(p\)-power components.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2. Decomposition of a finite abelian \(p\)-group

Let \(P\) be a non‑trivial finite abelian \(p\)-group, \(|P|=p^{\,m}\).
We prove by induction on \(m\) that

\[
P\;\cong\;\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k}\mathbf Z/p^{\,n_j}\mathbf Z ,
\qquad n_1\ge n_2\ge\cdots\ge n_k\ge1 .
\tag{2}
\]

---

#### Base case \(m=1\).

\(P\cong\mathbf Z/p\mathbf Z\) is already cyclic of prime‑power order.

---

#### Induction step.

Assume \(m>1\).  
Choose an element \(x\in P\) of maximal order \(p^{\,r}\)
(\(1\le r\le m\)).  
Set \(C=\langle x\rangle\cong\mathbf Z/p^{\,r}\mathbf Z\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
**Lemma 3 (direct summand).**  
The cyclic subgroup \(C\) is a direct summand of \(P\).

*Proof.*  
Let \(\varepsilon=p^{\,m-r}\).  
Because the exponent of \(P\) is \(p^{\,m}\), the endomorphism
\(\psi:P\to P\) given by \(\psi(g)=\varepsilon g\) is a surjective
homomorphism with kernel of size \(p^{\,r}\).
In particular \(\psi(C)=0\), so \(\ker\psi\cap C=0\).  
Thus \(\psi(P)\) is a subgroup of \(P\) of order \(p^{\,m-r}\)
and \(\psi(P)\cap C=0\).

Since \(|P/C|=p^{\,m-r}\), we obtain a natural isomorphism
\(P/C\;\cong\;\psi(P)\).
The short exact sequence  

\[
0\longrightarrow C\longrightarrow P
   \xrightarrow{\;\pi\;}P/C\longrightarrow 0
\]

therefore splits: choose a section \(s:P/C\to P\) that
identifies \(s(P/C)=\psi(P)\).
Hence \(P=C\oplus s(P/C)\).  ∎
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Let \(K=s(P/C)\); then \(P=C\oplus K\) and \(|K|=p^{\,m-r}\).

By the induction hypothesis,
\(K\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k-1}\mathbf Z/p^{\,n_j}\mathbf Z\) for suitable
exponents \(n_j\).  Adding the summand \(C\) gives (2) for \(P\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Thus every finite abelian \(p\)-group is a direct sum of cyclic
\(p\)-power groups.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3. Completing the proof

Apply the result of §2 to each primary component \(G_{p_i}\) of
(1).  
Write

\[
G_{p_i}\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k_i}\mathbf Z/p_i^{\,n_{ij}}\mathbf Z .
\]

Inserting into (1) yields

\[
G\;\cong\;\bigoplus_{i=1}^{t}\bigoplus_{j=1}^{k_i}
           \mathbf Z/p_i^{\,n_{ij}}\mathbf Z ,
\]

which is exactly the desired decomposition.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Remark

The same conclusion can be reached more algebraically by presenting
\(G\) as a finitely generated \(\mathbf Z\)-module
\(G\cong \mathbf Z^{\,r}\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{i=1}^{t}\mathbf Z/d_i\mathbf Z\)
with \(d_i\mid d_{i+1}\); the finiteness of \(G\) forces \(r=0\).
Factoring each \(d_i\) into prime powers and applying the Chinese
remainder theorem gives the decomposition above.  The two proofs are
equivalent; the group‑theoretic proof presented here is entirely
self‑contained.

**Theorem.**  
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a continuous surjection where  
\(X\) is compact and \(Y\) is Hausdorff.  
Then \(f\) is a quotient map; i.e. a subset \(U\subseteq Y\) is open iff
\(f^{-1}(U)\) is open in \(X\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  \(f\) is a closed map

Let \(C\subseteq X\) be closed.  
Since \(X\) is compact, \(C\) is compact.  
A continuous image of a compact set is compact, so \(f(C)\) is compact
in \(Y\).  
Because \(Y\) is Hausdorff, every compact subset of \(Y\) is closed.
Hence \(f(C)\) is closed in \(Y\).

Thus \(f\) sends closed sets in \(X\) to closed sets in \(Y\); i.e.  
\(f\) is a closed map.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  A closed surjection is a quotient map

Let \(V\subseteq Y\).  
We prove:

\[
V \text{ is open }\iff f^{-1}(V)\text{ is open in }X .
\]

---

**(\(\Rightarrow\))**  
If \(V\) is open, continuity of \(f\) gives that \(f^{-1}(V)\) is open.

---

**(\(\Leftarrow\))**  
Assume \(f^{-1}(V)\) is open.  
Then \(X\setminus f^{-1}(V)\) is closed in \(X\).  
Because \(f\) is closed, its image \(f(X\setminus f^{-1}(V))\) is
closed in \(Y\).

Notice that

\[
Y\setminus V=f(X\setminus f^{-1}(V)).
\]

Indeed,

* If \(y\in Y\setminus V\), choose \(x\in X\) with \(f(x)=y\) (surjectivity).  
  If \(x\in f^{-1}(V)\) then \(y=f(x)\in V\), a contradiction; hence  
  \(x\in X\setminus f^{-1}(V)\) and \(y\in f(X\setminus f^{-1}(V))\).

* Conversely, if \(y=f(x)\) with \(x\in X\setminus f^{-1}(V)\), then
  \(y\notin V\); so \(y\in Y\setminus V\).

Thus \(Y\setminus V\) is a closed subset of \(Y\); consequently \(V\)
is open.

Therefore \(V\) is open in \(Y\) iff \(f^{-1}(V)\) is open in \(X\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Since \(f\) is a continuous surjection satisfying the above equivalence,
\(f\) is a quotient map. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

*Remark.*  
A continuous surjection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is
in fact a *perfect* map (closed, continuous, with compact fibers), and
every perfect map is a quotient map.  The above argument shows this
directly using the closedness of \(f\).

**Lemma 1 –  \(\ell ^2\) is a vector space.**  
Let  

\[
\ell ^2=\Bigl\{x=(x_n)_{n\ge 1}\; ;\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^2<\infty\Bigr\}.
\]

For \(x=(x_n),y=(y_n)\in \ell ^2\) and \(\alpha\in\mathbb C\),

\[
x+y=(x_n+y_n)_{n\ge 1},\qquad 
\alpha x=(\alpha x_n)_{n\ge 1}.
\]

By Cauchy–Schwarz,

\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n+y_n|^2
\le 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^2
  +2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|y_n|^2<\infty,
\]
and
\(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|\alpha x_n|^2=|\alpha|^2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^2<\infty\).
Thus \(x+y,\alpha x\in\ell ^2\); hence \(\ell ^2\) is a complex vector space.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  The inner product

Define for \(x=(x_n),y=(y_n)\in\ell ^2\)

\[
\langle x ,y\rangle =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}x_n\overline{y_n}.
\tag{1}
\]

The series converges absolutely because of Cauchy–Schwarz:

\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n\overline{y_n}|
\le \Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^2\Bigr)^{1/2}
      \Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|y_n|^2\Bigr)^{1/2}<\infty .
\]

*Sesquilinearity.*  
For \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb C\),

\[
\langle \alpha x+\beta z , y\rangle
   =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(\alpha x_n+\beta z_n)\overline{y_n}
   =\alpha\langle x,y\rangle+\beta\langle z,y\rangle .
\]

*Conjugate symmetry.*  \(\langle x ,y\rangle=\overline{\langle y,x\rangle}\).

*Positive definiteness.*  
\(\langle x ,x\rangle=\sum|x_n|^2\ge0\), and \(\langle x ,x\rangle=0\) iff
\(x_n=0\) for all \(n\), i.e. \(x=0\).

Thus \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot\rangle\) is an inner product on \(\ell ^2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The induced norm

Define  

\[
\|x\|_2=\bigl(\langle x,x\rangle\bigr)^{1/2}
       =\Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^2\Bigr)^{1/2}\; .
\]

The usual axioms for a norm (positivity, homogeneity, triangle inequality)
follow from the properties of an inner product; in particular the
Cauchy–Schwarz inequality

\[
|\langle x ,y\rangle|\le \|x\|_2\,\|y\|_2
\]

implies the triangle inequality for \(\|\cdot\|_2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Completeness

Let \((x^{(k)})_{k\ge1}\subset\ell ^2\) be a Cauchy sequence for the norm
\(\|\cdot\|_2\).  
Fix \(n\in\mathbb N\).  
Since \(\|x^{(k)}-x^{(l)}\|_2^2=\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}|x^{(k)}_j-x^{(l)}_j|^2\),
the sequence \((x^{(k)}_n)_{k\ge1}\) of complex numbers is Cauchy and
hence converges; denote its limit by \(a_n\).  
Set \(a=(a_n)_{n\ge1}\).

---

**Claim 1.  \(a\in\ell ^2\).**  
For each \(k\),

\[
\|x^{(k)}\|_2^2=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x^{(k)}_n|^2.
\]

Because \((x^{(k)})\) is Cauchy, the norms \(\|x^{(k)}\|_2\) are bounded,
so there exists \(M>0\) with \(\|x^{(k)}\|_2\le M\) for all \(k\).
Apply Fatou’s lemma to the non‑negative series

\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|a_n|^2
  =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lim_{k\to\infty}|x^{(k)}_n|^2
  \le \liminf_{k\to\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x^{(k)}_n|^2
  \le M^2 <\infty .
\]

Hence \(a\in\ell ^2\).

---

**Claim 2.  \(x^{(k)}\to a\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\).**  
Let \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Because \((x^{(k)})\) is Cauchy, choose \(N\) such that
\(\|x^{(k)}-x^{(l)}\|_2<\varepsilon\) whenever \(k,l\ge N\).

Fix \(k\ge N\).  For each \(n\) we have \(x^{(k)}_n\to a_n\); hence
for every fixed \(m\) there exists \(L\) such that
\(|x^{(k)}_n-a_n|<\varepsilon/\sqrt{2m}\) whenever \(n\le m\) and
\(k\ge L\).
Write

\[
\|x^{(k)}-a\|_2^2
  =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x^{(k)}_n-a_n|^2
  =\sum_{n=1}^{m}|x^{(k)}_n-a_n|^2
   +\sum_{n>m}|x^{(k)}_n-a_n|^2
  =:S_1+S_2 .
\]

Choose \(m\) so large that the tail of \(a\) satisfies  
\(\sum_{n>m}|a_n|^2<(\varepsilon/2)^2\).
For \(k\ge L\),

* For \(n\le m\), \(S_1< m(\varepsilon/\sqrt{2m})^2
      =\varepsilon^2/2\).
* For \(n>m\),

\[
|x^{(k)}_n-a_n|\le |x^{(k)}_n|+|a_n|
   \;\Longrightarrow\;
S_2\le 2\sum_{n>m}|x^{(k)}_n|^2+2\sum_{n>m}|a_n|^2 .
\]

But \(\sum_{n>m}|x^{(k)}_n|^2\le\|x^{(k)}\|_2^2\le M^2\) and
\(\sum_{n>m}|a_n|^2<(\varepsilon/2)^2\), so for sufficiently large
\(k\) the second term can be made \(<\varepsilon^2/2\).
Thus \(\|x^{(k)}-a\|_2<\varepsilon\) for all \(k\) large enough.

Hence \(x^{(k)}\to a\) in the norm \(\|\cdot\|_2\).

---

Since every Cauchy sequence in \(\ell ^2\) converges to an element of
\(\ell ^2\), the space is complete.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

The vector space \(\ell ^2\) endowed with the inner product (1) is
complete with respect to the norm \(\|x\|_2=(\sum|x_n|^2)^{1/2}\).
Therefore \((\ell ^2,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)\) is a **Hilbert space**.

(Equivalently, this is a special case of the Riesz–Fischer theorem,
which characterizes the square‑summable sequences as a complete inner‑product space.)

Let  

\[
A=(a_{ij})\in M_{n}(\Bbb F), \qquad
A=[c_{1}\;c_{2}\;\dotsc\;c_{n}]
\]

be a square matrix over a field \(\Bbb F\).  
Denote by \(\sigma\) a permutation of \(\{1,\dots ,n\}\) and by
\(\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\) its sign.  
The **determinant** of \(A\) is the polynomial

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle 
\det A \;=\;\sum_{\sigma\in S_{n}}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)
\prod_{i=1}^{n} a_{i\,\sigma(i)} }\tag{1}
\]

This formula is the most convenient one to verify the required
properties.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1. Multilinearity in the columns

Fix all columns except the \(k\)-th one, say \(c_{k}\).  
Write the \(k\)-th column as \(c_{k}=u+v\) where \(u,v\in\Bbb F^{n}\).
In (1) every monomial contains exactly one entry from column \(k\); thus

\[
\begin{aligned}
\det\!\bigl(c_{1},\dotsc ,c_{k-1},u+v,c_{k+1},\dotsc ,c_{n}\bigr)
&=\sum_{\sigma}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)
\bigl((u+v)_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)
\prod_{\substack{i=1\\ i\ne k}}^{n}c_{i,\sigma(i)}  \\
&=\sum_{\sigma}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)
u_{\sigma(k)}\prod_{\substack{i=1\\ i\ne k}}^{n}c_{i,\sigma(i)}
+\sum_{\sigma}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)
v_{\sigma(k)}\prod_{\substack{i=1\\ i\ne k}}^{n}c_{i,\sigma(i)}\\
&=\det\!\bigl(c_{1},\dotsc ,c_{k-1},u,c_{k+1},\dotsc ,c_{n}\bigr) \\
&\quad+\det\!\bigl(c_{1},\dotsc ,c_{k-1},v,c_{k+1},\dotsc ,c_{n}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \(\det\) is linear in the \(k\)-th column.  
Because \(k\) was arbitrary, \(\det\) is multilinear in all columns.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2. Alternating property

#### (a) Swapping two columns changes the sign

Let \(A=[c_{1}\dots c_{k}\dots c_{\ell}\dots c_{n}]\) and
\(A'=[c_{1}\dots c_{\ell}\dots c_{k}\dots c_{n}]\) be obtained by
interchanging the \(k\)-th and \(\ell\)-th columns.
In (1) replace \(\sigma\) by the permutation \(\tau=(k\ \ell)\sigma\).
Since \(\operatorname{sgn}(\tau)= -\,\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\) and
the product of entries is the same (the two factors coming from the
interchanged columns are merely swapped), we obtain

\[
\det A'=-\,\det A .
\]

#### (b) Two equal columns give determinant \(0\)

If \(c_{k}=c_{\ell}\) for some \(k\neq \ell\), swap the \(k\)-th and
\(\ell\)-th columns.  By (a) the determinant changes sign, but the
matrix itself is unchanged, so

\[
\det A =-\det A \;\Longrightarrow\; \det A =0 .
\]

Hence \(\det\) is an **alternating** multilinear function of the columns.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3. Consequences

The properties proved above are enough to characterise the determinant:

*   A map \(\phi: M_{n}(\Bbb F)\to \Bbb F\) that is multilinear and
    alternating in the columns, and satisfies \(\phi(I_{n})=1\), is
    necessarily equal to the polynomial (1).  
    (This is the usual uniqueness argument: the polynomial (1) has all
    these properties, and any other map with them must coincide with
    it.)

Thus the determinant is a multilinear alternating function of the
columns of a matrix. The same argument, with rows instead of columns,
shows the analogous statements for the rows.

Let  

\[
f:X\longrightarrow Y
\]

be a bijection which is continuous.  
Assume  

* \(X\) is a compact metric space,
* \(Y\) is a metric space (hence Hausdorff).

We shall prove that the inverse map  

\[
f^{-1}:Y\longrightarrow X
\]

is continuous; i.e. \(f\) is a homeomorphism.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  A lemma about compact subsets of a metric space

> **Lemma.**  
> If \(K\) is a compact subset of a metric space \(Z\), then \(K\) is closed in \(Z\).

*Proof.*  In a metric space compactness is equivalent to the
Heine–Borel property: a set is compact iff every open cover has a
finite subcover.  
Let \(z\notin K\).  For each \(k\in K\) put \(r_{k}=d(z,k)>0\).
The balls \(B(z,r_{k}/2)\) form an open cover of \(\{z\}\), which is
compact; hence there is a finite subcover.  The union of the finitely
many closed balls \(B(k,r_{k}/2)^c\) contains \(K\) and is disjoint from
\(z\).  Thus \(z\) is in the complement of \(K\), proving that
\(K^c\) is open. ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  \(f\) is a closed map

Let \(C\subseteq X\) be closed.  
Because \(X\) is compact, every closed subset of \(X\) is compact:
\(C\) is compact.

Since \(f\) is continuous, the image of a compact set is compact, so
\(f(C)\) is compact in \(Y\).  
By the lemma, \(f(C)\) is closed in \(Y\).  
Hence

\[
\boxed{\,C\text{ closed in }X\;\Longrightarrow\; f(C)\text{ closed in }Y\,}
\]

i.e. \(f\) is a *closed map*.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Continuity of \(f^{-1}\)

Let \(D\subseteq X\) be closed.  
Because \(f\) is a bijection, \(f^{-1}(D)\) is exactly the set of all
\(y\in Y\) with \(f^{-1}(y)\in D\).  
But \(f^{-1}(D)=f(D)\) as sets in \(Y\).  
By the preceding paragraph \(f(D)\) is closed in \(Y\).  
Thus the pre‑image under \(f^{-1}\) of every closed set of \(X\) is
closed in \(Y\); equivalently, \(f^{-1}\) is continuous.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

\(f\) is continuous by hypothesis; \(f^{-1}\) is continuous by step 3.
Therefore \(f\) is a homeomorphism between the compact metric space
\(X\) and the metric space \(Y\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

*Remark.*  
The argument works for any continuous bijection from a compact
Hausdorff space onto a Hausdorff space, not only for metric spaces.  
The metric hypothesis is used only to guarantee that compact sets are
closed. ∎

Let \((X,\Sigma ,\mu )\) be a measure space and  
\(f:X\to[0,\infty ]\) a measurable function.  
A **simple function** is a finite linear combination of indicator
functions of measurable sets; i.e.

\[
s(x)=\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\,1_{E_{j}}(x),\qquad a_{j}\ge 0,\;E_{j}\in\Sigma .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Construction of the approximating sequence

For each positive integer \(n\) define

\[
\varphi _{n}(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{2^{n}n-1}\frac{k}{2^{n}}
\,\mathbf 1_{\{\,k/2^{n}\le f(x)<(k+1)/2^{n}\,\}}(x)
\;+\;
n\,\mathbf 1_{\{\,f(x)\ge n\,\}}(x).
\tag{1}
\]

The sets

\[
A_{k,n}:=\{x:\;k/2^{n}\le f(x)<(k+1)/2^{n}\},\qquad 
A_{\infty ,n}:=\{x:\;f(x)\ge n\}
\]

are measurable because \(f\) is measurable (pre‑images of Borel sets
are in \(\Sigma\)).  Hence each \(\varphi _{n}\) is a finite sum of
measurable indicator functions; therefore \(\varphi _{n}\) is simple and
measurable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Monotonicity

Let \(x\in X\) and \(n\in\mathbb N\).  
If \(f(x)\ge n\) then \(\varphi _{n}(x)=n\) and \(\varphi _{n+1}(x)=n+1\ge
\varphi _{n}(x)\).  
If \(f(x)<n\), choose \(k\) with \(k/2^{n}\le f(x)<(k+1)/2^{n}\).
For the next level \(n+1\) the same \(x\) belongs to the set
\(\{(k\!+\!1)/2^{n+1}\le f(x)<(k\!+\!2)/2^{n+1}\}\), so

\[
\varphi _{n+1}(x)=\frac{k+1}{2^{\,n+1}}\ge\frac{k}{2^{\,n}}=\varphi _{n}(x).
\]

Thus \(\varphi _{n}\le\varphi _{n+1}\) for every \(n\); the sequence is
pointwise increasing.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Pointwise convergence to \(f\)

Fix \(x\in X\).  

*If \(f(x)=\infty\):*  
Then \(\varphi _{n}(x)=n\) for all \(n\), and \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\varphi
_{n}(x)=\infty=f(x)\).

*If \(f(x)=r<\infty\):*  
Let \(k_{n}=\bigl\lfloor 2^{\,n}r\bigr\rfloor\).  
Then \(k_{n}/2^{\,n}\le r<(k_{n}+1)/2^{\,n}\), and
\(\varphi _{n}(x)=k_{n}/2^{\,n}\).  
As \(n\to\infty\) the dyadic fractions \(k_{n}/2^{\,n}\) converge to
\(r\) (they are just the truncations of the binary expansion of \(r\)).
Hence

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\varphi _{n}(x)=r=f(x).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Conclusion

The sequence \(\{\varphi _{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) satisfies

1. each \(\varphi _{n}\) is a simple measurable function;
2. \(\varphi _{n}\le \varphi _{n+1}\) for all \(n\);
3. \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\varphi _{n}(x)=f(x)\) for every
\(x\in X\).

Therefore every non‑negative measurable function is the pointwise
limit of an increasing sequence of simple functions.  ∎

**Bézout’s identity**

> For any integers \(a,b\), not both zero, there exist integers
> \(x,y\) such that  
> \[
> \gcd(a,b)=ax+by .
> \]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Preparations

Let  

\[
S=\{\,ax+by \mid x,y\in\mathbb Z\,\}\subseteq\mathbb Z
\]

and put

\[
S^{+}=\{\,s\in S \mid s>0\,\}.
\]

*The set \(S^{+}\) is non‑empty.*

Indeed, at least one of \(a,b\) is non–zero; say \(|a|>0\).  
Choosing \(x=1,\;y=0\) gives \(ax+by=a\), and \(|a|>0\) belongs to
\(S^{+}\).

*The set \(S^{+}\) has a least element.*

By the Well–Ordering Principle every non‑empty subset of \(\mathbb N\)
has a smallest element.  Hence there is a positive integer

\[
d=\min S^{+}\quad\text{(so }d>0\text{)} .
\]

Because \(d\in S\), there are integers \(x_{0},y_{0}\) with

\[
d=ax_{0}+by_{0}. \tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  \(d\) divides \(a\) and \(b\)

Take the Euclidean division of \(a\) by \(d\):

\[
a=qd+r ,\qquad 0\le r<d ,
\]
for some integers \(q,r\).

Subtract \(q\) times (1):

\[
\begin{aligned}
r &=a-dq
   =a-\bigl(ax_{0}+by_{0}\bigr)q \\
  &=a(1-qx_{0})+b(-qy) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \(r\) is an integer linear combination of \(a\) and \(b\); i.e.  
\(r\in S\).  Moreover \(0\le r<d\).  
If \(r>0\), it would belong to \(S^{+}\) but be smaller than the minimal
element \(d\)—impossible.  Hence \(r=0\), so \(d\mid a\).

The same argument with \(b\) in place of \(a\) shows \(d\mid b\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  \(d\) is the greatest common divisor

Let \(c\) be any integer that divides both \(a\) and \(b\).  
Then \(c\mid ax+by\) for all \(x,y\); in particular \(c\mid d\).
Thus every common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\) divides \(d\).

Since \(d\) itself divides \(a\) and \(b\), it is a common divisor, and
no common divisor can exceed it.  Therefore

\[
d=\gcd(a,b).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

We have exhibited integers \(x_{0},y_{0}\) (the ones in (1)) such that

\[
\gcd(a,b)=ax_{0}+by_{0}.
\]

Hence Bézout’s identity holds for all integers \(a,b\) (with the
convention \(\gcd(0,0)\) undefined; for \(a=0,b\neq0\) we obtain
\(x=0,\;y=\pm1\)).  ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Remarks**

* The argument uses only elementary facts: the Well‑Ordering
Principle, the division algorithm, and the definition of the greatest
common divisor.

* The constructive part (finding \(x_{0},y_{0}\)) can be turned into
the *extended Euclidean algorithm*, which actually produces such
coefficients efficiently.  The existence proof above guarantees that
the algorithm will succeed.

**Theorem.**  
Let \(I\subset \mathbb {R}\) be an open interval and let  
\(f:I\longrightarrow\mathbb {R}\) be convex and continuous on \(I\).  
Then for every \(x_{0}\in I\) there exist \(\delta>0\) and \(L>0\) such that  

\[
|f(x)-f(y)|\le L\,|x-y|\qquad (x,y\in (x_{0}-\delta,x_{0}+\delta)\cap I).
\]

In other words \(f\) is locally Lipschitz on \(I\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Left and right derivatives of a convex function

For a convex function \(f\) defined on an open interval, the one–sided
derivatives

\[
f'_-(x):=\lim_{h\downarrow 0}\frac{f(x)-f(x-h)}{h},
\qquad
f'_+(x):=\lim_{h\uparrow 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}
\]

exist for every \(x\in I\) (they may be \( \pm\infty\) at the boundary
points of \(I\)).  
Moreover, \(f'_-\) and \(f'_+\) are non‑decreasing functions on \(I\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The secant slopes lie between the one–sided derivatives

Let \(x<y\) be in \(I\).  
From convexity we know that the graph of \(f\) lies below the chord
joining \((x,f(x))\) and \((y,f(y))\), and above all tangents that
touch the graph to the left of \(x\) and to the right of \(y\).
Formally, for any \(z\in(x,y)\),

\[
f'_-(z)\le\frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}\le f'_+(z).
\]

Taking the infimum over \(z\in(x,y)\) on the left and the supremum on
the right yields

\[
\inf_{z\in(x,y)} f'_-(z)\le
\frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}\le
\sup_{z\in(x,y)} f'_+(z). \tag{1}
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Bounding the one‑sided derivatives locally

Fix \(x_{0}\in I\).  
Because \(I\) is open, choose \(a<b\) with \(a<x_{0}<b\) and
\([a,b]\subset I\).
Since \(f'_-\) and \(f'_+\) are monotone, they are bounded on
\([a,b]\):

\[
m:=\inf_{x\in[a,b]} f'_-(x)\quad\text{and}\quad
M:=\sup_{x\in[a,b]} f'_+(x)
\]

are finite real numbers.  Let

\[
L:=\max\{\,|m|,\,|M|\,\}.
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Local Lipschitzness

Take any \(x,y\in[a,b]\) with \(x<y\).  
From (1) we have

\[
m\le \frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}\le M .
\]

Multiplying by \(y-x>0\) and taking absolute values gives

\[
|f(y)-f(x)|\le \max\{|m|,|M|\}\,(y-x)=L\,|x-y|.
\]

The same inequality holds when \(y<x\) by symmetry.
Thus \(f\) is Lipschitz on \([a,b]\) with constant \(L\).

Choosing \(\delta:=\min\{x_{0}-a,\;b-x_{0}\}\) we have

\[
(x_{0}-\delta,x_{0}+\delta)\cap I\subset [a,b],
\]

so \(f\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz on the whole neighbourhood
\((x_{0}-\delta,x_{0}+\delta)\cap I\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Conclusion

Every point \(x_{0}\in I\) possesses a neighbourhood on which the convex
continuous function \(f\) satisfies a Lipschitz estimate.
Hence every continuous real‑valued convex function on an open interval
is **locally Lipschitz**. ∎

---------------------------------------------------------------------

*Remark.*  
The Lipschitz constant \(L\) depends on the chosen neighbourhood of
\(x_{0}\); a convex function need not be globally Lipschitz
(e.g. \(f(x)=x^{2}\) on \(\mathbb R\)).  The local bound follows from
the boundedness of the one‑sided derivatives on a compact subinterval.

Let \(\Bbb R^{n}\) be equipped with the Euclidean metric  
\(d(x,y)=\|x-y\|_{2}\).
A set \(K\subset \Bbb R^{n}\) is **compact** if every open cover of \(K\)
has a finite subcover.  
In a metric space the following facts are standard:

* \(K\) compact  \(\Longleftrightarrow\)  every sequence in \(K\) has a
convergent subsequence whose limit lies in \(K\)  (sequential
compactness).

We will use this characterization to prove that a compact set is
closed and bounded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Compact  \(\Rightarrow\)  bounded

Assume that \(K\) is not bounded.  
Then for each integer \(m\ge1\) we can choose a point \(x_{m}\in K\) with  

\[
\|x_{m}\|>m .
\]

The sequence \(\{x_{m}\}\) has no convergent subsequence:  
if \(x_{m_{j}}\to y\) in \(\Bbb R^{n}\), then \(\|x_{m_{j}}\|\to
\|y\|\), but \(\|x_{m_{j}}\|\ge m_{j}\to\infty\), a contradiction.

But by sequential compactness of \(K\), every sequence in \(K\)
must possess a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in \(K\).
Hence our constructed sequence yields a contradiction.
Therefore \(K\) must be bounded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Compact  \(\Rightarrow\)  closed

Let \(x\in\overline K\), i.e. there is a sequence \(\{x_k\}\subset K\)
with \(x_k\to x\).
By sequential compactness, \(\{x_k\}\) has a convergent subsequence
\(\{x_{k_j}\}\) that converges to some point \(y\in K\).
But the whole sequence converges to \(x\), so the subsequence also
converges to \(x\).
Limits in \(\Bbb R^{n}\) are unique; hence \(x=y\).
Thus \(x\in K\), which shows that \(\overline K\subset K\).
Since the reverse inclusion is obvious, \(K\) is closed.

(Equivalently: for any \(x\notin K\), define
\(d(x,K)=\inf\{\|x-y\|:y\in K\}\).
Because the continuous function \(y\mapsto\|x-y\|\) attains its minimum
on the compact set \(K\), we have \(d(x,K)>0\).
Then the open ball \(B(x,d(x,K)/2)\) is disjoint from \(K\),
showing that the complement of \(K\) is open; hence \(K\) is closed.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Combining the two parts, every compact subset of \(\Bbb R^{n}\) is
closed and bounded.  
(This is one direction of the Heine–Borel theorem.)

**Claim.**  
Let \(X\) be a connected topological space and let \(f:X\to Y\) be continuous.  
Then the image \(f(X)\subseteq Y\) is connected.

---

### Proof

Recall that a space is *connected* iff it cannot be written as the union of two
disjoint non‑empty open subsets.  
Equivalently, a set \(A\subseteq Y\) is connected (with the subspace topology) iff there do **not** exist disjoint open sets \(U,V\subseteq Y\) such that  

\[
A\subseteq U\cup V,\qquad A\cap U\neq\varnothing,\qquad A\cap V\neq\varnothing .
\]

Assume, to reach a contradiction, that \(f(X)\) is disconnected.  
Thus there exist disjoint open sets \(U,V\subseteq Y\) satisfying the above
conditions for \(A=f(X)\).

Because \(f\) is continuous, the pre‑images \(f^{-1}(U)\) and \(f^{-1}(V)\)
are open in \(X\).  
Moreover

* They are disjoint: if \(x\in f^{-1}(U)\cap f^{-1}(V)\) then
  \(f(x)\in U\cap V=\varnothing\), impossible.
* They cover \(X\): for any \(x\in X\) we have \(f(x)\in f(X)\subseteq
  U\cup V\); hence \(x\in f^{-1}(U)\cup f^{-1}(V)\).
* They are non‑empty: because \(f(X)\cap U\neq\varnothing\), pick
  \(y\in f(X)\cap U\). There exists \(x\in X\) with \(f(x)=y\), thus
  \(x\in f^{-1}(U)\).  Similarly for \(V\).

Thus \(X\) is expressed as the union of two disjoint non‑empty open sets,
which contradicts the hypothesis that \(X\) is connected.

Hence our assumption was false, and \(f(X)\) must be connected.

---

### Remarks

* The same argument works for any continuous map: the image of a
  connected set under a continuous map is always connected.  
* The proof uses only the basic definitions of continuity, openness,
  and connectedness; no extra structure on \(X\) or \(Y\) is needed.
* In particular, if \(X\) is a connected subset of a space \(Z\) and
  \(i:X\hookrightarrow Z\) is the inclusion map (which is continuous),
  then \(i(X)=X\) is connected in \(Z\).

Thus we have shown that continuous images of connected spaces are
connected.

**Theorem.**  
Let \(F_q\) be a finite field with \(q=p^{\,n}\) elements (\(p\) prime).  
The multiplicative group  
\[
F_q^{\times}=F_q\setminus \{0\}
\]
is cyclic of order \(q-1\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  A lemma on roots of unity in a field

Let \(K\) be any field and let \(m\ge 1\).  
The polynomial \(x^{m}-1\in K[x]\) has at most \(m\) roots in \(K\).

Indeed, if \(f(x)\) is a non‑zero polynomial over a field, it has at most
\(\deg f\) distinct zeros; the only way \(x^{m}-1\) could have more than
\(m\) roots is if it were the zero polynomial, which it is not.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Finite subgroups of the multiplicative group of a field

Let \(G\le K^{\times}\) be a finite subgroup, \(|G|=n\).
For a prime \(p\mid n\) denote by \(P\) a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup of \(G\).
Since \(G\le K^{\times}\), \(G\) is abelian and so is \(P\).

#### Lemma 2.1  
\(P\) is cyclic.

*Proof.*  
Write \(|P|=p^{\,k}\).  
If \(k=1\) the result is clear.  
Assume \(k>1\).  
If \(P\) were not cyclic, the structure theorem for finite abelian \(p\)-groups
gives a decomposition
\[
P\;\cong\; H\times L ,
\qquad |H|=|L|=p^{\,\ell}\;( \ell\ge 1).
\]
