Let \(X=[0,1]\) and \(C(X)\) be the Banach space of real‑valued
continuous functions with the supremum norm.  
The map

\[
\mathcal M(X)\longrightarrow C(X)^{\ast},\qquad
\mu\mapsto\bigl(f\mapsto\int_{X}f\,d\mu\bigr)
\]

is an isometric isomorphism.  
Consequently every continuous linear functional on \(C([0,1])\) is
representable by integration against a unique finite signed regular
Borel measure on \([0,1]\). 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

The proof above follows the classical Riesz representation
arguments.  In particular the construction of the outer measure
(1)–(2), its regularity, and the uniform approximation of
continuous functions by step functions are the key ingredients.  ∎

Let  

\[
S(p)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{p}}, \qquad p\in \mathbb R .
\]

The terms are positive and for \(p>0\) they decrease to \(0\).  
We will prove

\[
S(p)\ \text{converges}\iff p>1 .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Divergence for \(p\le 0\)

If \(p\le 0\) then \(n^{-p}\ge 1\) for all \(n\), hence the general term does **not** tend to \(0\); the series cannot converge.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The case \(0<p\le 1\) (comparison with an integral)

For \(p>0\) the function \(f(x)=x^{-p}\) is continuous, positive and decreasing on \([1,\infty)\).  
By the integral test

\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{p}}\ \text{converges}
      \iff
\int_{1}^{\infty}x^{-p}\,dx \ \text{converges}.
\]

The integral evaluates to  

\[
\int_{1}^{\infty}x^{-p}\,dx=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle \frac{1}{1-p}\bigl[x^{\,1-p}\bigr]_{1}^{\infty} & (p\neq1),\\[1ex]
\displaystyle \int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{dx}{x}=\infty & (p=1).
\end{cases}
\]

If \(0<p<1\) then \(1-p>0\) and \(x^{\,1-p}\to\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\); hence the integral diverges.  
If \(p=1\) the integral is the logarithm and also diverges.  
Therefore the series diverges for all \(0<p\le 1\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Convergence for \(p>1\) (Cauchy condensation)

Let \(a_n=\frac{1}{n^{p}}\) with \(p>0\).  
The sequence \(\{a_n\}\) is positive and decreasing, so the **Cauchy condensation test** applies:

\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \ \text{converges}
\iff
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 2^{k}\,a_{2^{k}}\ \text{converges}.
\]

Compute the condensed terms:

\[
a_{2^{k}}=\frac{1}{(2^{k})^{p}}=2^{-kp},
\qquad
2^{k}\,a_{2^{k}}
=2^{k}\cdot 2^{-kp}=2^{k(1-p)} .
\]

Hence

\[
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 2^{k}\,a_{2^{k}}
   =\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 2^{\,k(1-p)} .
\]

This is a geometric series with common ratio \(r=2^{\,1-p}\).

* If \(p>1\), then \(1-p<0\) and \(|r|=2^{\,1-p}<1\); the geometric series converges.
* If \(p=1\), \(r=1\) and the series diverges.
* If \(p<1\), \(r>1\) and the series diverges.

Thus, by the condensation test, \(\sum 1/n^{p}\) converges iff \(p>1\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

Combining all the cases:

* For \(p\le 0\) the terms do not go to \(0\) ⇒ series diverges.
* For \(0<p\le 1\) the integral test shows divergence.
* For \(p>1\) the condensation test (equivalently, the integral test) shows convergence.

Therefore

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{p}}\text{ converges } \iff\ p>1 } .
\]

This is the classical p‑series test.

**Theorem.**  
Let \(V\) be a finite–dimensional vector space over \(\mathbb R\) or \(\mathbb C\) and  
\(\|\cdot\|_{1},\|\cdot\|_{2}\) two norms on \(V\).  
Then there exist constants \(m,M>0\) such that  

\[
m\,\|v\|_{1}\;\le \;\|v\|_{2}\;\le\;M\,\|v\|_{1}\qquad \text{for every }v\in V .
\]

Consequently the two norms induce the same notion of convergence:  
\(x_{n}\to x\) w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\) iff \(x_{n}\to x\) w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Equivalence of norms

Let  

\[
S_{1}=\{v\in V:\|v\|_{1}=1\}
\]

be the “unit sphere’’ for \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\).  
Because \(V\) is finite dimensional, closed and bounded sets are compact (Heine–Borel).  
\(S_{1}\) is closed (the pre‑image of \(\{1\}\) under the continuous map \(v\mapsto\|v\|_{1}\))  
and bounded (every element has norm \(1\)).  Hence \(S_{1}\) is compact.

Define  

\[
m:=\inf_{v\in S_{1}}\|v\|_{2},
\qquad
M:=\sup_{v\in S_{1}}\|v\|_{2}.
\]

Both infimum and supremum are attained (compactness) and are finite
(because \(\|\cdot\|_{2}\) is a norm, it is bounded on \(S_{1}\)).  
Moreover \(m>0\): if \(\|v\|_{2}=0\) for some \(v\in S_{1}\), then \(v=0\), contradicting \(\|v\|_{1}=1\).

Now let \(v\in V\) and put \(t=\|v\|_{1}\) and \(u=v/t\) if \(t\ne0\) (otherwise \(v=0\)).  
Then \(u\in S_{1}\) and

\[
\|v\|_{2}=t\,\|u\|_{2}\quad\text{with}\quad 
m\le\|u\|_{2}\le M .
\]

Hence

\[
m\,\|v\|_{1}\le\|v\|_{2}\le M\,\|v\|_{1}\qquad\forall\,v\in V .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Equality of convergence notions

Let \((x_{n})\) be a sequence in \(V\) and \(x\in V\).

*Assume \(x_{n}\to x\) with respect to \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\).*  
Set \(d_{n}=x_{n}-x\).  
Then \(\|d_{n}\|_{1}\to 0\).  By the inequality above,

\[
\|d_{n}\|_{2}\le M\,\|d_{n}\|_{1}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0,
\]

so \(x_{n}\to x\) with respect to \(\|\cdot\|_{2}\).

*Conversely, assume \(x_{n}\to x\) with respect to \(\|\cdot\|_{2}\).*  
Again \(d_{n}=x_{n}-x\) satisfies \(\|d_{n}\|_{2}\to0\), and

\[
\|d_{n}\|_{1}\le \frac{1}{m}\,\|d_{n}\|_{2}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0.
\]

Thus \(x_{n}\to x\) with respect to \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\).

Therefore the two norms generate the same convergence structure on \(V\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Comments

* The key ingredient is that in a finite‑dimensional space the unit sphere of any norm is compact, which guarantees the existence of the positive constants \(m,M\).  
* Once the constants are found, the inequalities immediately give the equivalence of the induced metrics \(d_{1}(u,v)=\|u-v\|_{1}\) and \(d_{2}(u,v)=\|u-v\|_{2}\).  
* The result holds over \(\mathbb R\) or \(\mathbb C\) and can be applied to any finite‑dimensional normed space, e.g. \(\mathbb R^{n}\) with Euclidean, maximum, or taxicab (Manhattan) norms.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Hence any two norms on a finite‑dimensional vector space induce exactly the same notion of convergence.**

**Lemma (connectedness preserved by continuous maps).**  
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a continuous map and let \(C\subset X\) be a connected subset.  
Then the image \(f(C)\subset Y\) is connected.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof  

Recall that a subspace \(S\) of a topological space is **connected** iff there do not exist
disjoint non‑empty open subsets \(O_1,O_2\) of \(S\) (open in the subspace topology)
such that \(S=O_1\cup O_2\).  
Equivalently, \(S\) is connected iff the only subsets of \(S\) that are simultaneously
closed and open in the subspace topology are \(\varnothing\) and \(S\).

We prove the contrapositive: *if the image \(f(C)\) is disconnected, then \(C\) is disconnected*.  
Assume \(f(C)\) is disconnected.  
By definition, there exist disjoint non‑empty open sets
\(U,V\subset Y\) such that  

\[
f(C)\subset U\cup V ,\qquad f(C)\cap U\neq\varnothing ,\qquad 
f(C)\cap V\neq\varnothing .
\tag{1}
\]

Let  

\[
A:=f^{-1}(U)\cap C,\qquad B:=f^{-1}(V)\cap C .
\]

Because \(f\) is continuous, the preimages \(f^{-1}(U)\) and \(f^{-1}(V)\) are open in \(X\).
Consequently \(A\) and \(B\) are open in the subspace \(C\) (they are intersections of
open sets with \(C\)).  
Moreover \(A\cap B=\varnothing\), because \(U\cap V=\varnothing\).

Using (1) we obtain

\[
C=A\cup B,
\]

since for every \(x\in C\) we have \(f(x)\in U\cup V\), hence
\(x\in f^{-1}(U)\) or \(x\in f^{-1}(V)\).  
Both \(A\) and \(B\) are non‑empty:  
\(A\neq\varnothing\) because \(f(C)\cap U\neq\varnothing\), so there is \(x\in C\) with
\(f(x)\in U\), whence \(x\in A\).  
Similarly \(B\neq\varnothing\).

Thus \(C=A\cup B\) is a separation of \(C\) into two disjoint non‑empty open subsets
of the subspace topology on \(C\).  
This contradicts the assumption that \(C\) is connected.  
Therefore our assumption that \(f(C)\) is disconnected must be false.

Hence \(f(C)\) is connected. ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Remark.**  
The argument uses only the basic topological facts that the preimage of an open set
under a continuous map is open, and that connectedness can be tested by the
non‑existence of a separation.  No further structure of the spaces \(X\) and \(Y\)
is needed.

Let \(A\) be an \(n\times n\) complex matrix that satisfies  

\[
A=A^{*}\qquad(\text{“self–adjoint’’ or “Hermitian’’}).
\]

All the results below are stated for the standard inner product  
\(\langle x,y\rangle =y^{*}x\) on \(\mathbb C^{n}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Eigenvalues of a self‑adjoint matrix are real  

Let \(Av=\lambda v\) with \(v\neq0\).  
Compute

\[
\lambda\langle v,v\rangle
   =\langle Av,v\rangle
   =\langle v,Av\rangle \qquad(\text{since }A=A^{*})
   =\langle v,\lambda v\rangle
   =\overline{\lambda}\,\langle v,v\rangle .
\]

Because \(\langle v,v\rangle>0\), we cancel it to obtain  
\(\lambda=\overline{\lambda}\).  Hence \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Eigenvectors belonging to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal  

Let \(Av=\lambda v\) and \(Aw=\mu w\) with \(\lambda\neq\mu\).  
Then

\[
\lambda\langle v,w\rangle
    =\langle Av,w\rangle
    =\langle v,Aw\rangle
    =\mu\langle v,w\rangle ,
\]

so \((\lambda-\mu)\langle v,w\rangle=0\).  
Since \(\lambda\neq\mu\), we must have \(\langle v,w\rangle=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  An orthonormal basis of eigenvectors exists  

We prove this by induction on \(n=\dim\mathbb C^{n}\).

*Base case \(n=1\).*  
Any \(1\times1\) self‑adjoint matrix is a real number; its (unique) eigenvector
can be normalised.

*Induction step.*  
Assume the statement holds for all self‑adjoint matrices of size \(k<n\).  
Let \(A\) be \(n\times n\).  
Because \(A\) is Hermitian, it has at least one real eigenvalue \(\lambda\)
(standard existence result for eigenvalues of a square matrix).  
Choose a unit eigenvector \(e_{1}\) for \(\lambda\).

Let  

\[
V_{1}= \{x\in\mathbb C^{n}\;|\;\langle x,e_{1}\rangle =0\}
\]

be the orthogonal complement of \(\operatorname{span}\{e_{1}\}\).
We show that \(A\) maps \(V_{1}\) into itself.

Take \(x\in V_{1}\).  
Then  

\[
\langle Ax,e_{1}\rangle
   =\langle x, A e_{1}\rangle
   =\langle x, \lambda e_{1}\rangle
   =\lambda\langle x,e_{1}\rangle
   =0 .
\]

Thus \(Ax\in V_{1}\).  
Consequently the restriction \(A_{1}=A|_{V_{1}}\) is a self‑adjoint
operator on the \((n-1)\)-dimensional space \(V_{1}\).

By the induction hypothesis, there is an orthonormal basis  
\(\{e_{2},\dots ,e_{n}\}\) of \(V_{1}\) consisting of eigenvectors of
\(A_{1}\); in fact they are eigenvectors of \(A\) as well, with
eigenvalues \(\mu_{2},\dots ,\mu_{n}\).

Adding \(e_{1}\) we obtain an orthonormal set  
\(\{e_{1},e_{2},\dots ,e_{n}\}\) that diagonalises \(A\):

\[
U^{*}AU=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda,\mu_{2},\dots ,\mu_{n}),
\qquad
U=[e_{1}\;e_{2}\;\cdots\;e_{n}]
\]
with \(U\) unitary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Consequences (spectral theorem)

The matrix \(A\) is unitarily diagonalizable, its diagonal entries are
real, and the columns of \(U\) are orthonormal eigenvectors.
If an eigenvalue occurs with multiplicity \(m\), the corresponding
eigenspace is \(m\)-dimensional and can be orthonormally
spanned by the eigenvectors belonging to that eigenvalue
(Gram–Schmidt can be applied inside each eigenspace if desired).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
**Summary.**  
A self‑adjoint matrix \(A\) has:

1. **Real eigenvalues** – proved by comparing \(\langle Av,v\rangle\) with its conjugate.
2. **Orthogonal eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues** – follows from the Hermitian property.
3. **An orthonormal basis of eigenvectors** – obtained inductively (or via the spectral theorem).

Thus every self‑adjoint matrix is diagonalised by a unitary matrix with real diagonal entries.

Let  

\[
f:\mathbb {R}\longrightarrow \mathbb {R},\qquad  
f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\qquad (x,y\in \mathbb {R}) .
\tag{1}
\]

Throughout we use \(c=f(1)\).  
The following facts are standard and will be used repeatedly.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  The values on the rational numbers

From (1) we obtain inductively

\[
f(n)=nf(1)=nc\qquad(n\in \mathbb N),                         \tag{2}
\]

and by the same argument \(f(-n)=-nc\).  
If \(m,n\in \mathbb N\) then

\[
f\!\left(\frac{m}{\,n\,}\right)=\frac{1}{n}f(m)=\frac{m}{n}c
\qquad(m,n\in\mathbb N)                                 \tag{3}
\]

so that

\[
\boxed{\;f(q)=cq\qquad(q\in \mathbb Q)\;}               \tag{4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Additive  \(\;\Rightarrow\;\)  continuous at \(0\)

We prove that an additive function which is either measurable or
bounded on some interval is continuous at \(0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 2.1  Measurable case

Assume \(f\) is Lebesgue‑measurable.
Let \(\varepsilon>0\) and put  

\[
A_{\varepsilon}=\{x\in\mathbb R:\;|f(x)|>\varepsilon\}.
\]

\(A_{\varepsilon}\) is measurable.  
Suppose \(0\) is *not* a point of continuity of \(f\).
Then for every \(n\in\mathbb N\) we can find
\(x_n\) with \(|x_n|<1/n\) and \(|f(x_n)|>\varepsilon\).
Hence \(x_n\in A_{\varepsilon}\).

For \(n\ge 1\) put \(B_n=x_n+A_{\varepsilon}\); by additivity
\(B_n\subset\mathbb R\) and the sets \(B_n\) are pairwise disjoint.
Indeed, if \(y\in B_m\cap B_n\) then
\(y=x_m+a_1=x_n+a_2\) with \(a_1,a_2\in A_{\varepsilon}\);
hence \(x_m-x_n=a_2-a_1\in A_{\varepsilon}-A_{\varepsilon}\).
But \(|x_m-x_n|<2/n\) for \(m,n\) large, so
\(A_{\varepsilon}\) would have positive measure inside
an arbitrarily small neighbourhood of \(0\).
Consequently \(A_{\varepsilon}\) has infinite Lebesgue measure,
contradicting that \(B_n\subset(-2,2)\) (all \(x_n\) lie in
\((-1/n,1/n)\)).
Thus a neighbourhood of \(0\) exists on which \(|f(x)|\le\varepsilon\).
Since \(\varepsilon\) is arbitrary, \(f\) is continuous at \(0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 2.2  Boundedness on an interval

Assume \(f\) is bounded on \([0,1]\); say
\(|f(x)|\le M\) for \(x\in[0,1]\).
Let \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Choose \(\delta=\frac{\varepsilon}{2M}\).
If \(|x|<\delta\) write \(x=q+r\) with \(q\in\mathbb Q,\;|q|<\delta\)
and \(0\le r<1\) (the remainder \(r\) is \(x-q\)).
Then

\[
f(x)=f(q)+f(r)=qc+f(r)
\]

by (4).  
Since \(|q|<\delta\) we have \(|qc|<\varepsilon/2\).
Furthermore \(r\in[0,1]\) and \(|f(r)|\le M\), hence
\(|f(r)|\le M<\varepsilon/2\) for \(\delta<\frac{\varepsilon}{2M}\).
Therefore \(|f(x)|<\varepsilon\).
Thus \(f\) is continuous at \(0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Continuity everywhere and linearity

Let \(f\) be additive and continuous at \(0\).
For any \(x\in\mathbb R\) we have

\[
f(x)=\lim_{n\to\infty}f\!\left(\frac{x}{n}\right)n
      =\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x}{n}f(1)n
      =xf(1)=cx .
\]

So \(f(x)=cx\) for all \(x\).  
Conversely, any linear function \(f(x)=cx\) obviously satisfies (1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Conclusion

* If \(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) satisfies \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) and is
  Lebesgue‑measurable (or merely bounded on some interval),  
  then \(f\) is continuous at \(0\), hence linear:
  \[
  \boxed{\,f(x)=cx\quad (x\in\mathbb R)\,}.
  \]

Thus the only continuous solutions of Cauchy’s equation,
and indeed the only measurable or locally bounded ones,
are the linear functions \(f(x)=cx\).

Let  

\[
T:X\longrightarrow Y ,\qquad S:Y\longrightarrow Z
\]

be compact operators between normed (or Banach) spaces.  
We shall show that the composite \(S\circ T :X\to Z\) is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Compactness via images of bounded sets  

Recall that a linear operator \(K:X\to Y\) is **compact** iff it maps every bounded subset of \(X\) into a relatively compact subset of \(Y\) (i.e. the closure of the image is compact).

Take an arbitrary bounded set \(B\subset X\).  
Because \(T\) is compact, the set \(T(B)\) is bounded in \(Y\) and its closure \(\overline{T(B)}\) is a compact subset of \(Y\).

The operator \(S\) is continuous (every compact operator is bounded and thus continuous).  
The continuous image of a compact set is compact; hence

\[
S\bigl(\overline{T(B)}\bigr)\subset Z
\]

is compact.  
Since \(S\circ T(B)\subset S(\overline{T(B)})\), the image \(S\circ T(B)\) is contained in a compact set and therefore is relatively compact in \(Z\).

Thus \(S\circ T\) maps every bounded set of \(X\) into a relatively compact set of \(Z\); hence \(S\circ T\) is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Sequential characterisation (alternative proof)

A linear operator \(K:X\to Y\) is compact iff every bounded sequence \((x_n)\subset X\) has a subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) such that \((Kx_{n_k})\) converges in \(Y\).

Let \((x_n)\) be a bounded sequence in \(X\).  
Because \(T\) is compact, there is a subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) with

\[
T(x_{n_k})\longrightarrow y\in Y .
\]

Now \(S\) is continuous, so

\[
S(T(x_{n_k}))\longrightarrow S(y)\in Z .
\]

Thus \((S\circ T(x_{n_k}))\) converges in \(Z\).  
Since every bounded sequence in \(X\) has a convergent subsequence under \(S\circ T\), the operator \(S\circ T\) is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Remark

The above argument actually shows that **the composition of a compact operator with a bounded operator is compact**:  
if \(T:X\to Y\) is compact and \(S:Y\to Z\) is merely bounded (hence continuous), then \(S\circ T\) is compact.  
Consequently, the composition of two compact operators is a special case of this more general fact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Hence, the composition of two compact operators is itself compact. ∎

Let \(U\subset \mathbb {R}\) be an open set.  
We prove that

\[
U=\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty } I_{n} ,\qquad  I_{m}\cap I_{n}=\varnothing \;(m\neq n)
\]

where each \(I_{n}\) is an open interval (possibly unbounded).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Maximal intervals contained in \(U\)

For \(x\in U\) set  

\[
\alpha (x)=\sup \{\,a\in\mathbb {R}\mid (a,x]\subset U \}
      \qquad(\alpha (x)\le x),
\]
\[
\beta (x)=\inf \{\,b\in\mathbb {R}\mid [x,b)\subset U \}
      \qquad(\beta (x)\ge x).
\]

Because \(U\) is open, for each \(x\in U\) there exist \(a<b\) with
\(x\in (a,b)\subset U\); hence the above sets are non‑empty and
their sup/inf are real numbers (or \(\pm\infty\)).
Define  

\[
I(x)=(\alpha (x),\beta (x)) .
\]

*Claim.*  \(I(x)\subset U\) and \(I(x)\) is the **largest** open interval
contained in \(U\) that contains \(x\).

Indeed, every point \(y\) with \(\alpha (x)<y<\beta (x)\) satisfies
\(y\in U\) because there is a neighbourhood of \(y\) lying entirely in
\(U\) (choose a point \(z\) between \(\alpha (x)\) and \(y\), then
\((\alpha (x),z]\subset U\)).  
If \(z>\beta (x)\) or \(z<\alpha (x)\) then \(z\notin U\); otherwise
we would contradict the definitions of \(\alpha(x)\) and \(\beta(x)\).
Hence \(I(x)\) is open and maximal with the property of being an open
interval in \(U\) containing \(x\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The family \(\{I(x):x\in U\}\) consists of disjoint intervals

If \(I(x)\cap I(y)\neq\varnothing\), then the union
\(I(x)\cup I(y)\) is an open interval containing both \(x\) and \(y\).
Since \(I(x)\) and \(I(y)\) are maximal, we must have \(I(x)=I(y)\).
Consequently distinct points of \(U\) belong to a unique interval \(I(x)\);
hence

\[
U=\bigcup_{x\in U}I(x)\qquad\text{and}\qquad
I(x)\cap I(y)=\varnothing\;\text{for}\;x\neq y .
\]

Thus the connected components of the open set \(U\) are precisely the
intervals \(I(x)\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Countability of the components

Each component \(I(x)\) contains at least one rational number
(because \(\mathbb {Q}\) is dense in \(\mathbb {R}\)).  
Define a map

\[
\Phi : \{\,I(x)\mid x\in U\,\}\longrightarrow \mathbb {Q},
\qquad \Phi (I)= \text{any rational }q\in I .
\]

Because the intervals are disjoint, distinct components contain
distinct rationals; hence \(\Phi\) is injective.
Therefore the set of components is at most countable
(because it injects into the countable set \(\mathbb {Q}\)).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

Let \(\{I_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) be a listing of all the distinct
components of \(U\).  
They are pairwise disjoint open intervals and

\[
U=\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} I_{n}.
\]

Thus every open subset of \(\mathbb {R}\) is a countable union of
pairwise disjoint open intervals (the intervals may be bounded or
unbounded). ∎

Let  

\[
T:V\longrightarrow V ,\qquad V\ \text{finite–dimensional over }\mathbb F
\]

and let  

\[
m_T(x)\in\mathbb F[x]
\]

denote the *minimal polynomial* of \(T\); i.e.

* \(m_T(T)=0\);
* \(m_T\) is monic;
* if \(q(x)\in\mathbb F[x]\) satisfies \(q(T)=0\) then \(\deg q\ge\deg m_T\).

(These properties follow from the fact that the set  
\(\{q\in\mathbb F[x]:q(T)=0\}\) is an ideal in \(\mathbb F[x]\) and \(\mathbb F[x]\) is a PID; a monic generator of the ideal is unique and is the minimal polynomial.)

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### Theorem  
If \(p(x)\in\mathbb F[x]\) satisfies \(p(T)=0\) then \(m_T(x)\mid p(x)\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Proof

Apply the Euclidean algorithm to divide \(p(x)\) by \(m_T(x)\):

\[
p(x)=q(x)\,m_T(x)+r(x), \qquad\text{with }\deg r<\deg m_T .
\]

Apply the operator \(T\) to both sides:

\[
p(T)=q(T)\,m_T(T)+r(T)=q(T)\cdot0+r(T)=r(T).
\]

But \(p(T)=0\) by hypothesis, hence

\[
r(T)=0 .
\]

The polynomial \(r\) has degree strictly smaller than \(\deg m_T\).  
Since \(m_T\) is the monic polynomial of smallest degree that annihilates \(T\), the only possibility is

\[
r(x)=0 .
\]

Consequently \(p(x)=q(x)\,m_T(x)\); i.e. \(m_T\) divides \(p\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### Remarks

* The above argument uses only the *division algorithm* in \(\mathbb F[x]\) and the minimality of \(m_T\); it holds for any field \(\mathbb F\) and for any finite‑dimensional vector space \(V\).
* As a corollary, the minimal polynomial divides the characteristic polynomial of \(T\) (by Cayley–Hamilton).
* In particular, if \(p(x)\) is any polynomial annihilating \(T\), every irreducible factor of \(m_T\) must also be a factor of \(p\). Thus \(m_T\) is the unique monic polynomial of least degree with this property.

This completes the proof.

**Dominated Convergence Theorem**

Let \((X,\Sigma ,\mu )\) be a measure space.  
Let \(\{f_{n}\}_{n\ge 1}\) be a sequence of (real or complex) measurable
functions such that

* \(f_{n}\to f\) \(\mu\)-almost everywhere,  
* there exists an integrable function \(g\in L^{1}(\mu )\) with  
  \(|f_{n}|\le g\) for every \(n\) (hence \(|f|\le g\) a.e.).

Then \(f\in L^{1}(\mu )\) and  

\[
   \int_{X}f_{n}\,d\mu \;\longrightarrow\; \int_{X}f\,d\mu .
\]

Equivalently,
\(\displaystyle \int_{X}|f_{n}-f|\,d\mu\to0\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Preliminary reduction

Because \(|f_{n}|\le g\) for all \(n\) and \(g\) is integrable,
every \(f_{n}\) and \(f\) belong to \(L^{1}(\mu )\).
We will prove
\(\int |f_{n}-f|\to0\).  
Fix \(\varepsilon>0\) and we shall show that there is \(N\) such that

\[
   \int_{X}|f_{n}-f|\,d\mu <\varepsilon\qquad(n\ge N).
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Truncating the dominating function

Because \(g\in L^{1}\), there is \(M>0\) such that

\[
     \int_{\{g>M\}} g\,d\mu <\frac{\varepsilon}{3}.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Set  
\(E:=\{x\in X:g(x)\le M\}\).
On \(E\) we have \(|f_{n}|\le g\le M\); hence  

\[
  \int_{E}g\,d\mu \le M\,\mu(E) <\infty ,
\]
so \(E\) has **finite measure**.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Uniform convergence on a large subset of \(E\)

The pointwise convergence \(f_{n}\to f\) holds on all of \(X\),
hence in particular on \(E\).
Since \(\mu(E)<\infty\), Egorov’s theorem applies:
for any \(\delta>0\) there is a measurable set \(F\subseteq E\) with  

\[
     \mu(E\setminus F)<\delta
\]
and such that \(f_{n}\to f\) **uniformly** on \(F\).

Choose \(\delta\) so that

\[
    2\int_{E\setminus F} g\,d\mu
      \le 2M\,\mu(E\setminus F)<\frac{\varepsilon}{3}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Uniform convergence on \(F\) yields an integer \(N\) with

\[
   |f_{n}(x)-f(x)|
   <\frac{\varepsilon}{3\,\mu(F)}
   \qquad\text{for all }x\in F,\; n\ge N .
\]

Hence

\[
   \int_{F}|f_{n}-f|\,d\mu
   \le \mu(F)\,\frac{\varepsilon}{3\,\mu(F)}
   = \frac{\varepsilon}{3}
   \qquad (n\ge N).
\tag{3.2}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Estimating the remaining parts

Write

\[
   \int_{X}|f_{n}-f|
   = \int_{F}|f_{n}-f|
     +\int_{E\setminus F}|f_{n}-f|
     +\int_{X\setminus E}|f_{n}-f|.
\]

For the second integral we use the bound \(|f_{n}-f|\le 2g\):

\[
   \int_{E\setminus F}|f_{n}-f|
   \le 2\int_{E\setminus F}g
   \quad\text{(by (3.1))}.
\]

For the third integral we use again \(|f_{n}-f|\le 2g\) and (2.1):

\[
   \int_{X\setminus E}|f_{n}-f|
   \le 2\int_{\{g>M\}}g
   < \frac{2\varepsilon}{3}
   \quad\text{(by (2.1))}.
\]

Combining (3.2) with the two estimates above we obtain, for \(n\ge N\),

\[
   \int_{X}|f_{n}-f|
   < \frac{\varepsilon}{3}
     + \frac{\varepsilon}{3}
     + \frac{2\varepsilon}{3}
   < \varepsilon .
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Conclusion

Because \(\varepsilon>0\) was arbitrary, we have proved

\[
   \int_{X}|f_{n}-f|\,d\mu \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
\]
and therefore

\[
   \int_{X}f_{n}\,d\mu \;\longrightarrow\; \int_{X}f\,d\mu .
\]

This completes the proof of the Dominated Convergence Theorem. \(\square\)

Let \(R\) be a **principal ideal domain** (PID) and let  
\(P\neq (0)\) be a prime ideal of \(R\).  
We show that no proper ideal of \(R\) lies strictly between \(P\) and \(R\); in other words \(P\) is maximal.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  The ideal \(P\) is generated by a prime element

Since \(R\) is a PID every ideal is principal, hence  

\[
P=(d)\quad\text{for some non‑zero }d\in R .
\]

Because \(P\) is prime, the element \(d\) must be a **prime element** of \(R\):
if \(d\mid ab\) then \((d)\mid(ab)\), and \((d)\) is prime, so \((d)\mid a\) or \((d)\mid b\);
hence \(d\mid a\) or \(d\mid b\).  
Thus \(d\) is not a unit, is irreducible, and is a prime element.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Any ideal that contains \(P\) is either \(P\) or \(R\)

Let \(I\) be an ideal of \(R\) such that  

\[
P\subseteq I \subseteq R .
\]

Because \(R\) is a PID, \(I=(e)\) for some \(e\in R\).  
Since \(P\subseteq I\), the generator \(d\) of \(P\) lies in \(I\); thus  

\[
d\in(e)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d=ex \text{ for some }x\in R .
\]

Hence \(e\mid d\).  Because \(d\) is a prime element, its only divisors are
units and associates of \(d\).  Therefore \(e\) is either

* a unit, in which case \((e)=R\);
* or an associate of \(d\), i.e. \(e=ud\) for some unit \(u\),
  in which case \((e)=(d)=P\).

Thus the only ideals containing \(P\) are \(P\) itself and the whole ring \(R\).
By definition, \(P\) is **maximal**.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Equivalently, \(R/P\) is a field

Another way to see the same fact is to look at the quotient ring.  
Let \(p\) be a prime element with \(P=(p)\).  
For any \(r\in R\) with \(r\notin P\) we have \((r)+P=R\) (otherwise a
proper ideal properly containing \(P\) would exist).  
Thus there are \(x,y\in R\) such that  

\[
xr+yp=1 .
\]

Reducing modulo \(P\) gives \(xr\equiv1\pmod P\); hence the coset
\(r+P\) is a unit in \(R/P\).  
Therefore every non‑zero element of \(R/P\) is invertible, so \(R/P\) is a
field.  Hence \((p)=P\) is a maximal ideal.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\boxed{\text{In a PID, every non‑zero prime ideal is maximal.}}
\]

Let \(X\) be a contractible space.  
Choose a point \(x_{0}\in X\) and a homotopy  

\[
H:X\times I\longrightarrow X ,\qquad  
H(x,0)=x ,\; H(x,1)=x_{0}
\]

from the identity map \( \operatorname{id}_{X}\) to the constant map  
\(c:X\to X,\;c(x)=x_{0}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  A chain homotopy between \(\operatorname{id}_{X*}\) and \(c_{*}\)

For a singular \(n\)-simplex \(\sigma :\Delta ^n\to X\) define a
singular \((n+1)\)-simplex

\[
P(\sigma)(t_{0},\dots ,t_{n+1})=
  H(\sigma(t_{0},\dots ,t_{n}),\,t_{n+1}) .
\]

(Think of the image of \(\sigma\) as a prism that is swept out by the
homotopy.)  
The assignment \(P:C_{n}(X)\to C_{n+1}(X)\) extends linearly to a
group homomorphism.  
A direct computation gives the **prism identity**

\[
\partial P(\sigma)+P\partial(\sigma)
  =H_{1*}(\sigma)-H_{0*}(\sigma).      \tag{1}
\]

Since \(H_{1}=\operatorname{id}_{X}\) and \(H_{0}=c\), (1) says that
\(P\) is a chain homotopy between the chain maps \(c_{*}\) and
\(\operatorname{id}_{X*}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  The constant map is null‑homologous

If \(\sigma :\Delta ^n\to X\) is a singular simplex, then
\(c_{*}(\sigma)=\sigma'\) where \(\sigma'(t_{0},\dots ,t_{n})=x_{0}\) for
all \((t_{0},\dots ,t_{n})\).  
Thus \(c_{*}(\sigma)\) is a *degenerate* simplex (its image is a point).
Such a simplex is the boundary of the \((n+1)\)-simplex

\[
\tau(t_{0},\dots ,t_{n+1})=x_{0},
\]

so \(\partial\tau=c_{*}(\sigma)\).  Consequently \(c_{*}\) is chain
homotopic to the zero chain map.  
From the chain homotopy in §1 we deduce

\[
\operatorname{id}_{X*}=c_{*}\;\text{ is null–homologous}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Vanishing of ordinary homology

The map \(\operatorname{id}_{X*}\) induces the identity on homology
\(H_{n}(X)\).  
Since it is also zero, we must have

\[
H_{n}(X)=0\qquad\text{for all }n>0 .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Reduced homology

The long exact sequence for the pair \((X,x_{0})\) gives

\[
0\longrightarrow H_{0}(x_{0})\xrightarrow{i_{*}}
H_{0}(X)\longrightarrow H_{0}(X,x_{0})\longrightarrow 0 .
\]

Because \(H_{0}(x_{0})\cong\mathbb Z\) and \(H_{0}(X)\cong\mathbb Z\)
(the latter follows from the connectedness of a contractible space),
we get \(H_{0}(X,x_{0})=0\).  
Reduced homology is defined by  

\[
\tilde H_{0}(X)=H_{0}(X)/\mathbb Z ,
\]

hence \(\tilde H_{0}(X)=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Conclusion

All reduced homology groups of a contractible space vanish:

\[
\tilde H_{n}(X)=0\quad\text{for every }n\ge 0.
\]

Equivalently, ordinary homology satisfies  

\[
H_{0}(X)\cong \mathbb Z ,\qquad H_{n}(X)=0\;\text{ for }n>0 .
\]

Thus a contractible space has trivial homology. ∎

**Goal.**  
Let \(\{B_t\}_{t\ge 0}\) be a (standard) Brownian motion on a probability space  
\((\Omega,\mathcal F ,\mathbf P)\).  
We prove that there exists a version \(\tilde B\) of \(B\) whose sample paths
\(t\mapsto \tilde B_t(\omega)\) are continuous on \([0,\infty)\) for \(\mathbf P\)-a.e.  
\(\omega\).  
Equivalently, \(\mathbf P\!\left(\{ \omega:\; B_\cdot(\omega)\text{ is continuous}\}\right)=1\).

The argument relies on **Kolmogorov’s continuity theorem**.  
The proof is short once the theorem is recalled; we only need to
verify its hypotheses for Brownian motion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Moments of the increments of Brownian motion

For \(0\le s<t\) the increment \(B_t-B_s\) is Gaussian with mean \(0\) and variance
\(t-s\).  Hence for every \(p>0\)

\[
\mathbb E\!\left[\,|B_t-B_s|^{p}\,\right]
     =\mathbb E\!\left[\,|Z|^{p}\,\right]\,(t-s)^{p/2}
     =: C_p\,|t-s|^{p/2},
\]

where \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) and \(C_p=\mathbb E|Z|^{p}\) is finite for all \(p>0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Kolmogorov’s continuity theorem (one convenient form)

Let \(X_t\) be a stochastic process defined on \([0,T]\).
Assume that there exist numbers \(\alpha>0\), \(\beta>0\), and \(C>0\) such that

\[
\mathbb E\!\left[|X_t-X_s|^{\alpha}\right]\le
      C\,|t-s|^{1+\beta}\qquad\text{for all }s,t\in[0,T].
\]

Then \(X\) admits a modification \(\tilde X\) whose sample paths are
Hölder continuous of any order \(\gamma\) with

\[
0<\gamma<\frac{\beta}{\alpha}.
\]

Consequently \(\tilde X\) is almost surely continuous on \([0,T]\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Verification of the hypotheses for Brownian motion

Choose any \(p>2\).  
Using the moment estimate from §1 we have

\[
\mathbb E\!\left[|B_t-B_s|^{p}\right]
    \le C_p\,|t-s|^{p/2}\;=\;C_p\,|t-s|^{1+\beta},
\]
with

\[
\beta=\frac{p}{2}-1>0 .
\]

Thus the condition of Kolmogorov’s theorem holds with  
\(\alpha=p\) and \(\beta=p/2-1\).

The theorem then yields a modification \(\tilde B\) of \(B\) that is
Hölder continuous of any order \(\gamma\) satisfying

\[
0<\gamma<\frac{\beta}{\alpha}
     =\frac{p/2-1}{p}
     =\frac12-\frac{1}{p}.
\]

Since \(p>2\) can be taken arbitrarily large, we may choose \(\gamma\) as close
as we wish to \(1/2\).  In particular, for every \(\eta>0\) there is a
modification that is Hölder continuous of order \(1/2-\eta\).

Because Hölder continuity implies continuity, the modification \(\tilde B\)
has continuous paths almost surely.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

Brownian motion admits a version \(\tilde B\) whose sample paths are
continuous on \([0,\infty)\) for \(\mathbf P\)-almost all \(\omega\).  
Hence the standard Brownian motion “has continuous paths almost surely.”

(If one prefers a constructive argument, one can also work with
dyadic rationals and the Borel–Cantelli lemma, but the Kolmogorov
criterion gives a clean, general proof.)

**Theorem 1.**  
For every permutation \(\sigma\in S_{n}\) there exist transpositions
\(\tau _1,\tau _2,\dots ,\tau _m\) such that  

\[
\sigma =\tau _1\tau _2\cdots \tau _m .
\]

*(Proof)*  
Write \(\sigma\) in its disjoint‑cycle decomposition

\[
\sigma =(c_1)(c_2)\cdots (c_r), \qquad   
c_k=(a_{k1}\;a_{k2}\;\dots \;a_{k\,\ell_k}),
\]

each \(c_k\) being a cycle of length \(\ell_k\ge 1\).  
For a cycle \(c=(a_1\,a_2\,\dots\,a_\ell)\) we have the well known identity

\[
(a_1\,a_2\,\dots\,a_\ell)
   =(a_1\,a_\ell)(a_1\,a_{\ell-1})\cdots(a_1\,a_2),
\tag{1}
\]

which is a product of \(\ell-1\) transpositions.  
Applying (1) to every non‑trivial cycle (\(\ell_k\ge 2\)) we obtain a
representation of \(\sigma\) as a product of transpositions.
∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Theorem 2. (Parity is well–defined).**  
Let \(\sigma\in S_{n}\).  
If

\[
\sigma =\tau _1\tau _2\cdots \tau _m=\rho _1\rho _2\cdots \rho _k ,
\]

where all \(\tau _i,\rho _j\) are transpositions, then \(m\equiv k
\pmod 2\).  
Consequently the parity of \(\sigma\) – “even’’ or “odd’’ – does not
depend on the particular decomposition into transpositions.

*(Proof)*  
For \(\sigma\in S_{n}\) define

\[
\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)
   =\#\{(i,j)\mid 1\le i<j\le n,\;\sigma(i)>\sigma(j)\},
\]

the number of inversions of \(\sigma\).  
The following facts are elementary:

1. **Identity.**  \(\operatorname{inv}(\operatorname{id})=0\).

2. **A transposition changes the inversion number by an odd amount.**  
   Let \(\tau=(i\,j)\) with \(i<j\).  In the permutation \(\sigma\) the
   positions \(i\) and \(j\) are interchanged.  Every element
   between \(i\) and \(j\) becomes inverted with either \(i\) or \(j\);
   there are \(j-i-1\) such elements, which is even, but the pair
   \((i,j)\) itself becomes an inversion if it was not before and vice
   versa.  Hence \(\operatorname{inv}(\sigma\tau)-\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)\)
   is odd.

From (2) it follows inductively that if
\(\sigma=\tau _1\cdots\tau _m\) then

\[
\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)\equiv m \pmod 2.
\tag{2}
\]

Thus the parity of the number of transpositions in any
decomposition of \(\sigma\) equals the parity of
\(\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)\), which depends only on \(\sigma\)
itself.  
If \(\sigma\) also equals \(\rho _1\cdots\rho _k\), then by (2)

\[
\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)\equiv m\equiv k\pmod 2,
\]

so \(m\) and \(k\) have the same parity.  
Consequently the parity of \(\sigma\) is well defined.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Corollary (The sign homomorphism).**  
Define

\[
\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)=(-1)^{\,\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)}
          =(-1)^{\,m}\quad\text{if }\sigma=\tau _1\cdots\tau _m .
\]

The above proof shows that \(\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\) is independent
of the chosen transposition decomposition, and the map
\(\operatorname{sgn}\colon S_{n}\to\{\pm1\}\) is a group homomorphism
(the sign of a product is the product of the signs).  
Its kernel is the alternating group \(A_{n}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Thus every permutation can be written as a product of transpositions,
and the parity (“even’’ or “odd’’) of a permutation is a well‑defined
concept. ∎

Let  

\[
|f(z)|\le C\bigl(1+|z|^{m}\bigr)\qquad(z\in\mathbb C)     \tag{1}
\]

for some constants \(C>0\) and an integer \(m\ge 0\).  
We will show that the Taylor series of \(f\) about the origin contains at most
\(m\) non‑zero terms, i.e. \(f\) is a polynomial of degree \(\le m\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Cauchy’s estimate for derivatives

For \(R>0\) let  

\[
M(R)=\max_{|z|=R}|f(z)|.
\]

From (1) we have \(M(R)\le C(1+R^{m})\).

Cauchy’s integral formula for the \(k\)-th derivative gives

\[
f^{(k)}(0)=\frac{k!}{2\pi i}\int_{|ζ|=R}\frac{f(ζ)}{ζ^{\,k+1}}\,dζ .
\]

Hence

\[
|f^{(k)}(0)|\le \frac{k!}{2\pi}\,2\pi R\,\frac{M(R)}{R^{\,k+1}}
      =k!\,\frac{M(R)}{R^{\,k}}   \le
      k!\,C\,\frac{1+R^{m}}{R^{\,k}}.               \tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Vanishing of higher derivatives

Let \(k>m\).  
Choose \(R\to\infty\) in (2).  
Since \(k>m\),

\[
\frac{1+R^{m}}{R^{k}}\longrightarrow 0,
\]

so the right–hand side of (2) tends to \(0\).
Thus

\[
f^{(k)}(0)=0\qquad(k>m).      \tag{3}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  The Taylor series is a polynomial

The Taylor series of \(f\) about the origin is

\[
f(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}\,z^{k},
\qquad z\in\mathbb C .
\]

By (3) all terms with \(k>m\) vanish. Therefore

\[
f(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{m}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}\,z^{k},
\]

a polynomial of degree at most \(m\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Remarks

* For \(m=0\) the hypothesis is that \(f\) is bounded; Liouville’s theorem
immediately gives a constant function, the \(0\)-th degree polynomial.
* The proof uses only the growth estimate (1); no further regularity of
\(f\) is required beyond being entire.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
**Conclusion.**  
If an entire function satisfies a polynomial growth bound  
\(|f(z)|\le C(1+|z|^{m})\) on the whole complex plane, then \(f\) is a
polynomial of degree at most \(m\).  
This is a Liouville‑type theorem for entire functions.

Let \(X_1,\dots ,X_n\) be compact Hausdorff spaces.  
The product topology on  
\[
X=X_1\times\cdots\times X_n
\]
is the coarsest topology making all projections
\(\pi_i:X\to X_i\) continuous.  
We prove that \(X\) is compact.  
For \(n=1\) this is true by hypothesis; assume the statement holds
for \(n-1\) spaces and prove it for \(n\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  A lemma (tube lemma)

Let \(X\) be any space, \(Y\) compact, and let  
\(U\subseteq X\times Y\) be an open set containing \(\{x_0\}\times Y\)
for some \(x_0\in X\).  
Then there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(x_0\) in \(X\) such
that  
\[
V\times Y\subseteq U .
\]

**Proof.**  
For every \(y\in Y\) there are open sets \(U_y\subseteq X\) and
\(V_y\subseteq Y\) with \(x_0\in U_y\), \(y\in V_y\) and
\(U_y\times V_y\subseteq U\).  
The family \(\{V_y:y\in Y\}\) is an open cover of the compact set
\(Y\); choose a finite subcover \(V_{y_1},\dots ,V_{y_k}\).
Put  
\[
V=\bigcap_{i=1}^k U_{y_i}.
\]
Then \(V\) is an open neighbourhood of \(x_0\).
For \((x,y)\in V\times Y\) choose \(i\) with \(y\in V_{y_i}\); we have
\(x\in V\subseteq U_{y_i}\), hence
\((x,y)\in U_{y_i}\times V_{y_i}\subseteq U\).
Thus \(V\times Y\subseteq U\). ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Compactness of a product of two compact spaces

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be compact and let
\(\mathcal U=\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in A}\) be an open cover of
\(X\times Y\).

For a fixed \(x\in X\) the set \(\{x\}\times Y\) is compact,
hence it is covered by finitely many members of \(\mathcal U\);
pick one of them, say \(U_{x}\in\mathcal U\), that covers
\(\{x\}\times Y\).
Because \(U_{x}\) is open and contains \(\{x\}\times Y\),
the tube lemma yields an open neighbourhood \(V_x\) of \(x\) in \(X\)
with \(V_x\times Y\subseteq U_{x}\).

The family \(\{V_x:x\in X\}\) is an open cover of \(X\); since \(X\)
is compact, choose a finite subcover \(V_{x_1},\dots ,V_{x_m}\).
For each \(i\) let \(U_i\) be the member of \(\mathcal U\) that
corresponds to \(V_{x_i}\) as above.
Then

\[
X\times Y=\bigcup_{i=1}^{m} (V_{x_i}\times Y)
\subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} U_i ,
\]

so \(\{U_1,\dots ,U_m\}\subseteq\mathcal U\) is a finite subcover of
\(X\times Y\).  
Hence \(X\times Y\) is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Finite products

Assume that the product of any \(n-1\) compact spaces is compact.
Let \(X_1,\dots ,X_n\) be compact.  
Set \(Z=X_1\times\cdots\times X_{n-1}\).
By the induction hypothesis \(Z\) is compact.  
Now \(X=X_n\times Z\) is the product of two compact spaces,
hence it is compact by the result of §2.  
Thus every finite product of compact spaces is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence we have proved Tychonoff’s theorem for finite products:
**the direct product of finitely many compact spaces is compact.**

Let \(F\) be a field and let  

\[
F\subseteq \overline F_1 ,\qquad F\subseteq \overline F_2 
\]

be two algebraic closures of \(F\).
We prove that there exists a field isomorphism  
\(\Phi:\overline F_{1}\longrightarrow \overline F_{2}\) which fixes \(F\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Extending the identity \(F\hookrightarrow \overline F_{2}\)

Consider the set  

\[
\mathcal S=\{(E,\varphi)\mid F\subseteq E\subseteq \overline F_{1},
               \ \varphi:E\longrightarrow \overline F_{2}\text{ is an }F\text{-embedding}\}.
\]

Partially order \(\mathcal S\) by  
\((E_1,\varphi_1)\le (E_2,\varphi_2)\) iff \(E_1\subseteq E_2\) and
\(\varphi_2|_{E_1}=\varphi_1\).

If \(\{(E_i,\varphi_i)\}_{i\in I}\) is a chain in \(\mathcal S\),
then \(\bigcup_{i}E_i\) is a subfield of \(\overline F_{1}\) containing \(F\), and
the maps \(\varphi_i\) agree on overlaps, so they glue to an
\(F\)-embedding \(\varphi:\bigcup_iE_i\to\overline F_{2}\).
Thus every chain has an upper bound; by Zorn’s lemma there is a
maximal element \((M,\psi)\) of \(\mathcal S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Maximality forces \(M=\overline F_{1}\)

Assume \(M\neq\overline F_{1}\) and pick \(a\in\overline F_{1}\setminus M\).
Since \(\overline F_{1}\) is algebraic over \(F\), \(a\) is algebraic over \(M\).
Let \(m(x)\in M[x]\) be the minimal polynomial of \(a\) over \(M\).
Because \(m(x)\) has coefficients in \(M\), the image
\(\psi(m)(x)\in\overline F_{2}[x]\) is a non‑zero polynomial.

\(\overline F_{2}\) is algebraically closed, so \(\psi(m)\) has a root
\(b\in\overline F_{2}\).
Define an extension \(\psi'\) of \(\psi\) by setting \(\psi'(a)=b\).
Then \(\psi'\) is an \(F\)-embedding \(M(a)\to\overline F_{2}\),
contradicting the maximality of \((M,\psi)\).
Hence \(M=\overline F_{1}\) and we obtain an
\(F\)-embedding

\[
\Phi:\overline F_{1}\longrightarrow\overline F_{2}. \tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  The image of \(\Phi\) is algebraically closed

Let \(K=\Phi(\overline F_{1})\subseteq\overline F_{2}\).
Take any polynomial \(f(x)=c_nx^n+\cdots+c_0\in K[x]\).
Write each coefficient \(c_i=\Phi(a_i)\) for some \(a_i\in\overline F_{1}\)
(because \(K=\Phi(\overline F_{1})\)).
Set \(g(x)=a_nx^n+\cdots+a_0\in\overline F_{1}[x]\).
Since \(\overline F_{1}\) is algebraically closed, \(g\) has a root
\(a\in\overline F_{1}\).
Then \(\Phi(a)\) is a root of \(f\) because

\[
f(\Phi(a))=\Phi(g(a))=\Phi(0)=0 .
\]

Thus every polynomial over \(K\) has a root in \(K\);
\(K\) is algebraically closed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Surjectivity of \(\Phi\)

\(K=\Phi(\overline F_{1})\) is an algebraic extension of \(F\) inside
\(\overline F_{2}\) and is algebraically closed.
If \(K\neq\overline F_{2}\), pick \(b\in\overline F_{2}\setminus K\).
Because \(b\) is algebraic over \(F\), it is algebraic over \(K\).
But \(K\) is algebraically closed, so \(b\in K\), a contradiction.
Hence \(K=\overline F_{2}\); that is, \(\Phi\) is surjective.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 5.  Conclusion

\(\Phi:\overline F_{1}\to\overline F_{2}\) is a bijective field homomorphism
that fixes \(F\).  Therefore

\[
\boxed{\ \overline F_{1}\cong_F \overline F_{2}\ } .
\]

The isomorphism is not canonical: the choice of the embedding in step 1
depends on the choice of a maximal pair in Zorn’s lemma.  In general
different choices give different isomorphisms.  Nevertheless, any two
algebraic closures of a given field are isomorphic over that field.

**Theorem.**  
For every real numbers \(a<b\) there is a rational number \(q\) such that  
\(a<q<b\). Equivalently, for every \(x\in\mathbb R\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\) there is a rational \(q\) with  
\(|x-q|<\varepsilon\).  
Thus \(\mathbb Q\) is *dense* in \(\mathbb R\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Preliminary facts

1. **Archimedean property.**  
   For every real \(y>0\) there exists \(n\in\mathbb N\) with \(n>y\).

2. **Division algorithm (for real numbers).**  
   For any real \(x\) and positive integer \(n\) there is a unique integer \(m\) such that  

   \[
   m\le x n < m+1 .
   \]

   (This is just the ordinary division algorithm applied to \(x n\).)

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Proof that a rational lies between any two reals

Let \(a,b\in\mathbb R\) with \(a<b\).

1. Apply the Archimedean property to \(b-a>0\):  
   choose \(n\in\mathbb N\) such that  

   \[
   n > \frac{1}{\,b-a\,}\quad\text{(equivalently }\,\frac1n < b-a\,).
   \]

2. By the division algorithm, for this \(n\) there exists an integer \(m\) with  

   \[
   m \le a n < m+1 .
   \]

   Divide by \(n\) to obtain

   \[
   \frac{m}{n}\le a < \frac{m+1}{n}.
   \tag{1}
   \]

3. Because \(a n < m+1\), the right‑hand inequality in (1) gives  

   \[
   a < \frac{m+1}{n}\le a+\frac1n .
   \]

   Using the choice of \(n\),

   \[
   a+\frac1n < a + (b-a)=b .
   \]

   Hence

   \[
   a < \frac{m+1}{n} < b .
   \]

   Since \(\dfrac{m+1}{n}\in\mathbb Q\), we have found a rational between \(a\) and \(b\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Alternate ε‑proof (useful for sequences)

Let \(x\in\mathbb R\) and \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Choose \(n\in\mathbb N\) with \(n>\frac1\varepsilon\).  
Apply the division algorithm to \(x n\) to get an integer \(m\) such that  

\[
m \le xn < m+1 .
\]

Thus  

\[
x - \frac{1}{n} < \frac{m}{n} \le x .
\]

The distance to the integer multiple of \(1/n\) is at most \(1/n\), so  

\[
|x-\tfrac{m}{n}| \le \frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon .
\]

Hence \(\tfrac{m}{n}\in\mathbb Q\) and satisfies \(|x-\tfrac{m}{n}|<\varepsilon\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

For any two real numbers there is a rational number strictly between them; equivalently, every real can be approximated arbitrarily closely by rationals. Therefore the set of rational numbers \(\mathbb Q\) is dense in \(\mathbb R\). ∎

Let  

\[
\ell ^1=\Bigl\{x=(x_1,x_2,\dots ): \sum_{n=1}^\infty |x_n|<\infty \Bigr\},
\qquad   
\ell ^\infty=\Bigl\{y=(y_1,y_2,\dots ): \sup_{n}|y_n|<\infty \Bigr\},
\]

equipped with the usual norms  
\(\|x\|_1=\sum |x_n|\) and \(\|y\|_\infty=\sup |y_n|\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  From \(\ell ^\infty\) to \((\ell ^1)^*\)

For \(y=(y_n)\in\ell ^\infty\) define a linear functional  

\[
T(y):\ell ^1\longrightarrow\mathbb{C},\qquad
T(y)(x)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty x_n\,y_n .
\]

*Well–definedness and boundedness.*  
If \(x\in\ell ^1\),

\[
\bigl|T(y)(x)\bigr|
  \le \sum_{n=1}^\infty |x_n||y_n|
  \le \Bigl(\sup_{n}|y_n|\Bigr)\sum_{n=1}^\infty |x_n|
  =\|y\|_\infty\,\|x\|_1 .
\]

Hence \(T(y)\) is a continuous linear functional and
\(\|T(y)\|\le \|y\|_\infty\).

*Equality of norms.*  
For each standard basis vector \(e_k=(0,\dots,0,1,0,\dots)\),

\[
T(y)(e_k)=y_k,\qquad \|e_k\|_1=1.
\]

Therefore

\[
\|T(y)\|=\sup_{\|x\|_1\le 1}|T(y)(x)|
        \ge\sup_{k}|T(y)(e_k)|=\sup_{k}|y_k|
        =\|y\|_\infty .
\]

Combining the two inequalities gives \(\|T(y)\|=\|y\|_\infty\).  
Thus \(T:\ell^\infty\to(\ell^1)^*\) is a linear isometry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  From \((\ell ^1)^*\) to \(\ell ^\infty\)

Let \(f\in(\ell ^1)^*\).  
Set

\[
y_n:=f(e_n)\qquad(n=1,2,\dots).
\]

Since \(\|e_n\|_1=1\),

\[
|y_n|=|f(e_n)|\le \|f\|\,\|e_n\|_1=\|f\|,
\]

so \(y=(y_n)\in\ell^\infty\) and \(\|y\|_\infty\le \|f\|\).

For any \(x=(x_n)\in\ell ^1\) with finite support,

\[
f(x)=f\!\Bigl(\sum_{n}x_n e_n\Bigr)=\sum_{n}x_n\,f(e_n)
   =\sum_{n}x_n\,y_n.
\]

Because the linear functionals are continuous and the finite–support
vectors are dense in \(\ell ^1\), the identity
\(f(x)=\sum_{n}x_n y_n\) holds for every \(x\in\ell ^1\).
Consequently \(f=T(y)\).

Hence the map  

\[
S:(\ell ^1)^*\longrightarrow\ell ^\infty,\qquad
S(f)=(f(e_1),f(e_2),\dots)
\]

is the inverse of \(T\).  
We also have

\[
\|S(f)\|_\infty=\sup_n|f(e_n)|
                \le\sup_{\|x\|_1\le 1}|f(x)|=\|f\|,
\]
and the reverse inequality follows from the calculation above,
so \(\|S(f)\|_\infty=\|f\|\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Isometric isomorphism

The maps \(T:\ell^\infty\to(\ell^1)^*\) and \(S:(\ell^1)^*\to\ell^\infty\)
are linear, bijective, and preserve the norms.  
Therefore they are Banach space isomorphisms; i.e.,

\[
(\ell^1)^*\;\cong_{\text{isometry}}\;\ell^\infty .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
**Conclusion.**  
The dual of \(\ell ^1\) is (isometrically) isomorphic to \(\ell ^\infty\); the isomorphism is given by

\[
y=(y_n)\in\ell^\infty\quad\longmapsto\quad
f_y:\ell^1\to\mathbb{C},\;\; f_y(x)=\sum_{n}x_n y_n,
\]

and its inverse maps a functional \(f\) to the sequence \((f(e_n))\).

**Theorem.**  
Let \(M\) be a connected \(1\)-dimensional manifold (possibly with boundary).  
Then \(M\) is homeomorphic to one of the following:

1. the circle \(S^{1}\);
2. the real line \(\mathbb {R}\);
3. a half‑open interval \([0,\infty )\);
4. a closed interval \([0,1]\);
5. an open interval \((a,b)\;(a<b)\).

(If \(M\) has no boundary, the only possibilities are (1) and (2).)  

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Preliminary facts

* A \(1\)-manifold \(M\) is locally homeomorphic to the open interval \((a,b)\subset \mathbb{R}\) or to the half‑open interval \([0,b)\).  
  Hence \(M\) is locally path–connected, locally compact and Hausdorff.  
  Consequently every component of \(M\) is a path component; thus the
  connectedness hypothesis implies that \(M\) is path‑connected.

* A connected 1‑manifold is locally Euclidean of dimension \(1\), hence it
  admits a **maximal atlas** of charts \((U_i,\varphi_i)\) where each
  \(U_i\) is open in \(M\) and \(\varphi_i:U_i\to (a_i,b_i)\subset \mathbb{R}\) is a homeomorphism.
  Because the \(U_i\) are open, the images \(\varphi_i(U_i)\) form an
  open cover of \(\mathbb{R}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Removing the boundary

Let \(\partial M\) denote the boundary of \(M\).  
If \(\partial M=\varnothing\) then \(M\) is a connected \(1\)-manifold
*without* boundary.  
If \(\partial M\neq\varnothing\) we first show that
\(\partial M\) consists of **exactly one or two** points.

Take \(p\in \partial M\).  There is a chart
\((U,\varphi)\) with \(p\in U\) such that \(\varphi(U)=(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\)
and \(U\cap\partial M=\{p\}\).
Hence \(U\setminus\{p\}\) is a disjoint union of two open intervals
\(U_-\) and \(U_+\) (one on each side of \(p\)).
Because \(M\) is connected, each of these intervals must lie in the same
connected component of \(M\setminus \partial M\).  
Therefore all boundary points belong to the same component of
\(M\setminus\partial M\).

Now take another boundary point \(q\).
Choosing charts around \(q\) gives a similar decomposition.
Because \(M\setminus\partial M\) is path‑connected, there is a
path in \(M\setminus\partial M\) from \(p\) to \(q\).
If \(p\neq q\), this path would have to cross the interior of a chart
containing a boundary point; this contradicts the local description above.
Hence \(\partial M\) has at most two points.  
If it had three or more, one could connect two of them by a path avoiding
the third, again contradicting local Euclidean structure.  
Consequently

\[
|\partial M|\in\{0,1,2\}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  The universal covering

Let \(\widetilde{M}\) be the universal covering space of \(M\).
Because \(M\) is a connected \(1\)-manifold (possibly with boundary),
\(\widetilde{M}\) is a connected \(1\)-manifold **without** boundary.
It follows that \(\widetilde{M}\) is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb{R}\).

The covering map \(\pi:\widetilde{M}\to M\) is a local homeomorphism.
Thus the action of the fundamental group \(\pi_1(M)\) on
\(\widetilde{M}\cong\mathbb{R}\) by deck transformations is by
homeomorphisms of the line.

* If \(M\) has no boundary, every deck transformation is either a
translation \(x\mapsto x+t\) (with \(t\neq0\)) or the identity.
  Hence \(\pi_1(M)\) is either trivial or infinite cyclic.
  * If \(\pi_1(M)=\{1\}\), the covering map is a homeomorphism,
    so \(M\cong\mathbb{R}\).
  * If \(\pi_1(M)\cong\mathbb{Z}\), the action is by translations
    with integer period.  The quotient \(\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}\) is
    the circle \(S^1\).  Hence \(M\cong S^1\).

* If \(M\) has one boundary point, then the covering space
  \(\widetilde{M}\) is again \(\mathbb{R}\), but the deck
  transformation group is a semigroup generated by the single
  translation \(x\mapsto x+1\) with a fixed point at \(-\infty\).
  In this case the quotient is homeomorphic to \([0,\infty)\).

* If \(M\) has two boundary points, the deck group is the same as
  above, but the quotient interval has finite length:
  \([0,1]\).  
  (The boundary points correspond to the images of \(0\) and \(1\).)

In the remaining case where \(M\) has no boundary but is non‑compact,
the only possibility for the deck group is the trivial one, and we
again obtain \(M\cong\mathbb{R}\).  The open interval \((a,b)\) occurs
as a connected, non‑compact \(1\)-manifold without boundary; it is
homeomorphic to \(\mathbb{R}\) by a linear homeomorphism
\(x\mapsto a+(b-a)\frac{x+1}{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

Thus every connected \(1\)-manifold \(M\) is homeomorphic to

* the circle \(S^{1}\) (compact without boundary);
* the real line \(\mathbb{R}\) (non‑compact, no boundary);
* a half‑open interval \([0,\infty)\) (one boundary point);
* a closed interval \([0,1]\) (two boundary points); or
* an open interval \((a,b)\) (no boundary, non‑compact).

In particular, if \(M\) is a connected \(1\)-manifold without boundary,
then \(M\) is homeomorphic either to \(S^{1}\) or to \(\mathbb{R}\),
which proves the requested statement. ∎

Let  

\[
\Omega\subset \mathbb {R}^{n}\qquad (n\ge 1)
\]

be a bounded domain with Lipschitz boundary,  

\[
H^1_0(\Omega)=\{v\in H^1(\Omega):v=0\ \text{on }\partial\Omega\}
\]

and  

\[
H^{-1}(\Omega)=\bigl(H^1_0(\Omega)\bigr)^{*}
\]

the dual of \(H^1_0(\Omega)\).  
For \(f\in H^{-1}(\Omega)\) we want to solve

\[
\Delta u = f \quad\text{in }\Omega ,\qquad u=0 \ \text{on }\partial\Omega .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1. Weak formulation

Multiplying the equation by a test function \(v\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) and integrating by parts gives  

\[
\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla v\,dx = \langle f,\,v\rangle ,
\tag{1}
\]

where \(\langle f,v\rangle\) is the duality pairing between \(H^{-1}\) and \(H^1_0\).

Define the bilinear form  

\[
a(u,v)=\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla v\,dx,\qquad u,v\in H^1_0(\Omega)
\]

and the linear functional  

\[
L(v)=\langle f,\,v\rangle ,\qquad v\in H^1_0(\Omega).
\]

The weak problem is: find \(u\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) such that  

\[
a(u,v)=L(v)\quad\forall v\in H^1_0(\Omega).
\tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2. Properties of \(a(\cdot,\cdot)\)

*Continuity.*  
By Cauchy–Schwarz,

\[
|a(u,v)|
   =\Bigl|\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla v\Bigr|
   \le \|\nabla u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\,
        \|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}
   =\|u\|_{H^1_0}\,\|v\|_{H^1_0}.
\]

*Coercivity.*  
For \(v\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) the Poincaré inequality yields  
\(\|v\|_{L^2}\le C_P\|\nabla v\|_{L^2}\).  
Hence the norm  

\[
\|v\|_{H^1_0}^2
   =\|v\|_{L^2}^2+\|\nabla v\|_{L^2}^2
   \le (1+C_P^2)\|\nabla v\|_{L^2}^2
   =C\;a(v,v),
\]

so there is a constant \(c>0\) such that  

\[
a(v,v)\ge c\|v\|_{H^1_0}^2\qquad \forall v\in H^1_0(\Omega).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3. Boundedness of \(L\)

Because \(f\in H^{-1}(\Omega)=\bigl(H^1_0(\Omega)\bigr)^{*}\),

\[
|L(v)|=|\langle f,v\rangle|
   \le \|f\|_{H^{-1}}\;\|v\|_{H^1_0}\qquad\forall v\in H^1_0(\Omega).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4. Application of Lax–Milgram

The bilinear form \(a(\cdot,\cdot)\) is continuous and coercive on the Hilbert space \(H^1_0(\Omega)\); the functional \(L\) is continuous.  
Therefore, by the Lax–Milgram theorem there exists a **unique** \(u\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) such that (2) holds.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5. Relation with the strong equation

Let \(u\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) satisfy (2).  
Take \(\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\Omega)\subset H^1_0(\Omega)\).  
Then

\[
\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla\varphi\,dx
   =\langle f,\varphi\rangle .
\]

But by the definition of the distributional Laplacian,

\[
\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla\varphi\,dx
   =-\int_{\Omega}\Delta u\,\varphi\,dx .
\]

Hence \(\langle f,\varphi\rangle =-\int_{\Omega}\Delta u\,\varphi\,dx\) for all test functions \(\varphi\).  
Consequently \(f=-\Delta u\) in the sense of distributions, i.e. \(\Delta u=f\) in \(\Omega\) with the homogeneous Dirichlet condition \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 6. Uniqueness

If \(u_1,u_2\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) both satisfy (2), then \(w=u_1-u_2\) satisfies  

\[
a(w,v)=0\qquad\forall v\in H^1_0(\Omega).
\]

Choosing \(v=w\) gives \(a(w,w)=\|\nabla w\|_{L^2}^2=0\), hence \(\nabla w=0\).  
By the Poincaré inequality \(w\equiv 0\).  
Thus the weak solution is unique.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Conclusion

For every \(f\in H^{-1}(\Omega)\) there exists a **unique** \(u\in H^1_0(\Omega)\) satisfying

\[
\int_{\Omega}\nabla u\!\cdot\!\nabla v\,dx
   =\langle f,v\rangle\quad \forall v\in H^1_0(\Omega),
\]

and this \(u\) solves \(\Delta u=f\) in \(\Omega\) with the homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition.  
The solution depends continuously on \(f\) because \(a(\cdot,\cdot)\) is coercive. This establishes the well‑posedness of the Poisson problem in \(H^1_0(\Omega)\) for data in \(H^{-1}(\Omega)\).

Let  

\[
F:\mathbb {R}\longrightarrow[0,1]
\]

be a **continuous distribution function**; i.e.  

* \(F\) is non‑decreasing,  
* \(\displaystyle\lim _{x\to-\infty}F(x)=0,\qquad \lim_{x\to\infty}F(x)=1,\)  
* \(F\) is right–continuous and, in particular, continuous on \(\mathbb R\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  From a distribution function to a probability measure  

For every \(x\in\mathbb R\) set  

\[
\mu\bigl((-\infty ,x]\bigr)=F(x).
\]

Because \(F\) is non‑decreasing and right–continuous, this defines a
unique **Lebesgue–Stieltjes** measure \(\mu\) on the Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra
\(\mathcal B(\mathbb R)\).  
For every Borel set \(B\) one has \(\mu(B)\in[0,1]\) and
\(\mu(\mathbb R)=1\); hence \(\mu\) is a probability measure whose
distribution function is exactly \(F\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Constructing a random variable with this law  

Take the canonical probability space  

\[
(\Omega ,\mathcal F ,\mathbf P)=([0,1],\mathcal B([0,1]),\lambda ),
\]

where \(\lambda\) denotes Lebesgue measure.  
Let  

\[
U(\omega)=\omega , \qquad \omega\in[0,1].
\]

Then \(U\sim\operatorname{Unif}(0,1)\).

Define the **quantile function** of \(F\) by  

\[
F^{-1}(u)=\inf \{x\in\mathbb R: F(x)\ge u\}, \qquad 0\le u\le 1 .
\]

Because \(F\) is non‑decreasing, the set on the right is non–empty for
every \(u\), and since \(F\) is continuous, the infimum is actually a
minimum: there is a unique \(x\) with \(F(x)=u\).  
Moreover \(F^{-1}\) is a measurable function on \([0,1]\) (being the
infimum of a family of measurable functions).

Now put  

\[
X(\omega)=F^{-1}(U(\omega)), \qquad \omega\in[0,1].
\]

Thus \(X\) is a random variable defined on the probability space
\((\Omega ,\mathcal F ,\mathbf P)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  The distribution of \(X\) equals \(F\)

For any \(x\in\mathbb R\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf P\{X\le x\}
 &=\mathbf P\{F^{-1}(U)\le x\}   \\[2mm]
 &=\mathbf P\{U\le F(x)\} \qquad
    (\text{because }F^{-1}\text{ is the left–inverse of }F)\\[2mm]
 &=\lambda([0,F(x)])              \\[2mm]
 &=F(x).
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence the cumulative distribution function of \(X\) is precisely
\(F\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion  

For any continuous distribution function \(F\) we have constructed a
probability space \((\Omega ,\mathcal F ,\mathbf P)\) and a random
variable \(X\) on it such that

\[
\mathbf P\{X\le x\}=F(x), \qquad\forall\,x\in\mathbb R .
\]

Thus **every continuous probability distribution function is indeed
realizable as the law of some random variable**. ∎

Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space and let \(K\subset X\) be **compact**.  
We recall the following facts that will be used:

* In a metric space compactness ⇔ sequential compactness: every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging to a point of \(K\).
* A metric space \(K\) is **complete** iff every Cauchy sequence in \(K\) converges to a point of \(K\).
* \(K\) is **totally bounded** iff for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there is a finite set \(F\subset K\) such that  
  \(K\subset\bigcup_{x\in F}B(x,\varepsilon)\) (the balls need not be of radius exactly \(\varepsilon\); any radius \(\le \varepsilon\) will do).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Compactness  \(\Longrightarrow\)  completeness

Let \(\{x_n\}\subset K\) be a Cauchy sequence.  
Because \(K\) is compact, it is sequentially compact; hence \(\{x_n\}\) has a subsequence \(\{x_{n_k}\}\) converging to some point \(x\in K\).

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Since \(\{x_n\}\) is Cauchy, there exists \(N\) such that
\[
m,n\ge N \implies d(x_m,x_n)<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}. \tag{1}
\]
Choose \(k_0\) so that \(n_{k_0}\ge N\) and
\[
d(x_{n_{k_0}},x)<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}. \tag{2}
\]
For any \(m\ge N\) we combine (1) and (2) by the triangle inequality:
\[
d(x_m,x)\;\le\; d(x_m,x_{n_{k_0}})+d(x_{n_{k_0}},x)
          \;<\; \frac{\varepsilon}{2}+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
          = \varepsilon .
\]
Thus \(x_m\to x\).  Since the limit \(x\) belongs to \(K\), the original Cauchy sequence converges in \(K\).  
Hence \(K\) is complete.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Compactness  \(\Longrightarrow\)  total boundedness

Let \(\varepsilon>0\) be arbitrary.  
Consider the family of open balls
\[
\mathcal{U}=\{\,B(y,\varepsilon)\mid y\in K\,\}.
\]
Each point \(y\in K\) lies in the ball \(B(y,\varepsilon)\), so \(\mathcal{U}\) is an open cover of \(K\).  
Because \(K\) is compact, a finite subcover exists: there are points \(y_1,\dots ,y_m\in K\) such that

\[
K\subset\bigcup_{i=1}^{m}B(y_i,\varepsilon).
\]

Consequently \(K\) can be covered by a finite number of balls of radius \(\varepsilon\); that is precisely the definition of **total boundedness**.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Conclusion

In a metric space, any compact subset \(K\) satisfies:

* **Completeness** – every Cauchy sequence in \(K\) converges to a point of \(K\).
* **Total boundedness** – for every \(\varepsilon>0\) a finite \(\varepsilon\)-cover of \(K\) exists.

Thus, in metric spaces, **compactness implies completeness and total boundedness**.

Let  

\[
G=(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)^{\times}=\{\,\bar a\in \mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z \mid
\gcd(a,n)=1\,\},
\]

the group of units modulo \(n\).  
\(G\) is a finite group whose order is Euler’s totient function

\[
|G|=\varphi(n)=\#\{a\in\{1,\dots ,n\}\mid\gcd(a,n)=1\}.
\]

For an element \(\bar a\in G\) denote by  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_n(a)=k
\]

its **multiplicative order**: the smallest positive integer \(k\) for which  
\(a^{k}\equiv 1\pmod n\) (or, equivalently, \(\bar a^{\,k}=1\) in \(G\)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Lemma  

If \(\bar a\in G\) and \(\bar a^m=1\) for some \(m\in\mathbf N\), then  
\(\operatorname{ord}_n(a)\mid m\).

*Proof.*  
Let \(k=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)\).  Write \(m= qk+r\) with  
\(0\le r<k\) (division algorithm). Then

\[
\bar a^{\,m}=\bar a^{\,qk+r}=(\bar a^{\,k})^{\,q}\bar a^{\,r}
=1^{\,q}\bar a^{\,r}=\bar a^{\,r}=1 .
\]

Since \(k\) is the smallest positive exponent giving \(1\), we must have
\(r=0\). Hence \(k\mid m\). ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Euler–Fermat theorem

For every \(\bar a\in G\),

\[
\bar a^{\,\varphi(n)}=1 .
\]

(This is the classical Euler theorem; it follows for instance by
counting the elements of the set \(\{a,2a,\dots ,\varphi(n)a\}\) modulo \(n\).)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Main statement  

For every \(\bar a\in G\),

\[
\operatorname{ord}_n(a)\mid \varphi(n).
\]

*Proof.*  
Apply the lemma to the exponent \(m=\varphi(n)\).  
Since \(\bar a^{\,\varphi(n)}=1\), the lemma gives  
\(\operatorname{ord}_n(a)\mid \varphi(n)\). ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Thus the multiplicative order of any unit modulo \(n\) divides Euler’s
totient \(\varphi(n)\).  In group‑theoretic terms, the cyclic subgroup
generated by \(\bar a\) has order \(\operatorname{ord}_n(a)\), and this
subgroup is a subgroup of the finite group \(G\) of order \(\varphi(n)\); by
Lagrange’s theorem its order must divide \(|G|=\varphi(n)\).  Either
argument yields the same conclusion.

Let \(k\) be a field and let \(\mathbf{Vect}_{k}\) denote the category whose objects are
\(k\)-vector spaces and whose morphisms are \(k\)-linear maps.
We show that for every finite family \((V_{i})_{i\in I}\) of vector spaces the
direct sum

\[
\bigoplus_{i\in I}V_{i}\;=\;\{(v_{i})_{i\in I}\mid v_{i}\in V_{i},
\;v_{i}=0\;\text{for all but finitely many }i\}
\tag{1}
\]

is simultaneously a product and a coproduct.  
(For a finite index set “finitely many” means “all of them’’; hence the
direct sum equals the ordinary product \(\prod V_{i}\).)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  The biproduct

For each \(i\in I\) define

* **injection**  
  \[
  \iota_{i}\colon V_{i}\longrightarrow \bigoplus_{j\in I}V_{j},
  \qquad \iota_{i}(v)=(0,\dots ,0,v,0,\dots ),
  \]
  where \(v\) occupies the \(i\)-th component;

* **projection**  
  \[
  \pi_{i}\colon \bigoplus_{j\in I}V_{j}\longrightarrow V_{i},
  \qquad \pi_{i}((v_{j})_{j\in I})=v_{i}.
  \]

Both maps are linear, and

\[
\pi_{i}\circ\iota_{j}= 
\begin{cases}
\operatorname{id}_{V_{i}} & \text{if }i=j,\\
0                        & \text{if }i\neq j .
\end{cases}\tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Product property

Let \(X\) be a vector space and let \(f_{i}\colon X\to V_{i}\) be
linear maps (one for each \(i\in I\)).
Define

\[
h\colon X\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{j\in I}V_{j},
\qquad
h(x)=\bigl(f_{i}(x)\bigr)_{i\in I}.
\]

Because each \(f_{i}\) is linear, \(h\) is linear.
Moreover, for every \(i\),

\[
\pi_{i}\circ h(x)=\pi_{i}\bigl((f_{j}(x))_{j}\bigr)=f_{i}(x),
\]

so \(\pi_{i}\circ h=f_{i}\).
If \(h'\colon X\to\bigoplus V_{j}\) satisfies \(\pi_{i}\circ h'=f_{i}\) for all
\(i\), then for each \(x\in X\)

\[
h'(x)=\bigl(\pi_{i}\circ h'(x)\bigr)_{i}
     =\bigl(f_{i}(x)\bigr)_{i}=h(x),
\]

hence \(h'=h\).  
Thus \(\bigoplus V_{i}\) together with the \(\pi_{i}\) is the product of
\((V_{i})_{i\in I}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Coproduct property

Let \(X\) be a vector space and let \(g_{i}\colon V_{i}\to X\) be
linear maps.  
Define

\[
k\colon \bigoplus_{j\in I}V_{j}\longrightarrow X,\qquad
k\bigl((v_{j})_{j}\bigr)=\sum_{j\in I} g_{j}(v_{j}),
\]
(the sum is finite because only finitely many \(v_{j}\) are non‑zero).

Linearity of \(k\) follows from linearity of the \(g_{j}\)’s.
For each \(i\),

\[
k\circ\iota_{i}(v)=k(0,\dots ,0,v,0,\dots)=g_{i}(v),
\]

so \(k\circ\iota_{i}=g_{i}\).
If \(k'\colon\bigoplus V_{j}\to X\) satisfies
\(k'\circ\iota_{i}=g_{i}\) for all \(i\), then for any element
\((v_{j})_{j}\) we have

\[
k'\bigl((v_{j})_{j}\bigr)
   =k'\!\left(\sum_{j}\iota_{j}(v_{j})\right)
   =\sum_{j}k'\!\circ\iota_{j}(v_{j})
   =\sum_{j}g_{j}(v_{j})
   =k\bigl((v_{j})_{j}\bigr),
\]

hence \(k'=k\).  
Therefore \(\bigoplus V_{i}\) with the \(\iota_{i}\) is the coproduct of
\((V_{i})_{i\in I}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Conclusion

For a finite family \((V_{i})_{i\in I}\) the object \(\bigoplus_{i\in I}V_{i}\)
together with the injections \(\iota_{i}\) and projections \(\pi_{i}\)
satisfies both the product and the coproduct universal properties, and
relation (2) shows that the two structures are compatible.
Hence \(\mathbf{Vect}_{k}\) possesses biproducts – the direct sums of
vector spaces are simultaneously products and coproducts.

(For an infinite index set the product is the direct product
\(\prod V_{i}\); the direct sum \(\bigoplus V_{i}\) is a subspace of it
and is *not* a product in general, but for finite families the two
constructions coincide.)

Let \(S\) be a compact, connected, orientable surface.  
The *genus* \(g(S)\) of \(S\) is the number of handles that appear when \(S\) is
obtained from the sphere by attaching handles; equivalently \(S\) is the
connected sum of \(g(S)\) tori.  
We prove that the Euler characteristic of \(S\) is

\[
\chi(S)=2-2\,g(S).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  The connected–sum formula for Euler characteristic

Let \(M,N\) be compact connected surfaces and let
\(\chi(M),\chi(N)\) denote their Euler characteristics.
Choose triangulations \(\mathcal T_M,\mathcal T_N\) and
remove a single 2–simplex from each triangulation; denote the
resulting complexes by \(\mathcal T'_M,\mathcal T'_N\).
The boundary of each removed simplex is a 1–cycle consisting of
three edges; gluing \(\mathcal T'_M\) to \(\mathcal T'_N\) along this boundary
produces a triangulation of the connected sum \(M\#N\).

Let
\(V_M,E_M,F_M\) and \(V_N,E_N,F_N\) be the numbers of vertices, edges and
faces of \(\mathcal T_M,\mathcal T_N\), respectively.
After removing one simplex we have

\[
V_M'=V_M,\quad E_M'=E_M-3,\quad F_M'=F_M-1
\]
(and similarly for \(N\)).
The glued complex has

\[
V=V_M'+V_N',\qquad
E=E_M'+E_N',\qquad
F=F_M'+F_N'.
\]

Therefore

\[
\chi(M\#N)
   =V-E+F
   =(V_M+V_N)-(E_M+E_N-6)+(F_M+F_N-2)
   =\chi(M)+\chi(N)-2 .
\tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Euler characteristic of a torus and of a sphere

* Sphere : \(\chi(S^2)=2\).
  A standard triangulation has \(V=2,\,E=3,\,F=1\).

* Torus : \(\chi(T^2)=0\).
  A standard triangulation of the torus has \(V=1,\,E=3,\,F=1\),
  so \(1-3+1= -1\); after adding the third edge that closes the
  cycle the Euler characteristic becomes \(0\).
  Equivalently, view the torus as a square with opposite sides
  identified; that square is a 4–gon with one 2–cell,
  \(V=1,E=1,F=1\), hence \(\chi=1-1+1=1\), but the identification
  of the sides reduces \(E\) to 1, giving \(\chi=0\).

Thus \(\chi(T^2)=0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Induction on the genus

Let \(g\ge 0\).
By the classification of compact orientable surfaces,  
\(S\) is homeomorphic to a connected sum of \(g\) tori:

\[
S\simeq \underbrace{T^2\#T^2\#\cdots\#T^2}_{g\ \text{times}}
      \quad (g=0 \text{ gives } S\simeq S^2).
\]

For \(g=0\) we have \(\chi(S)=2=2-2\cdot0\).

Assume the formula holds for genus \(g-1\), i.e.
\(\chi(S_{g-1})=2-2(g-1)\).
Let \(S_g=T^2\#S_{g-1}\).
Using (1),

\[
\chi(S_g)=\chi(T^2)+\chi(S_{g-1})-2
          =0+(2-2(g-1))-2
          =2-2g .
\]

Thus by induction the formula
\(\chi(S)=2-2g(S)\) holds for every compact connected orientable surface.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  (Optional) Polygon representation

For completeness, we note that an orientable surface of genus \(g\)
can be obtained from a single \(4g\)-gon by identifying its sides in
pairs as  
\(a_1b_1a_1^{-1}b_1^{-1}\cdots a_g b_g a_g^{-1} b_g^{-1}\).
After identification all vertices become one vertex,
the \(4g\) sides collapse to \(2g\) distinct edges, and the single
interior region is a 2–cell.
Hence

\[
\chi = V-E+F = 1-2g+1 = 2-2g ,
\]

which agrees with the inductive proof above.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Therefore the genus of a compact connected orientable surface completely
determines its Euler characteristic:

\[
\boxed{\;\chi(S)=2-2\,g(S)\;}
\]

Let \((x_n)_{n\ge 1}\) be a real–valued sequence.  
For every \(n\) set  

\[
s_n=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}, \qquad i_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}.
\]

The sequences \((s_n)\) and \((i_n)\) are monotone:

* \(s_{n+1}\le s_n\) (the set over which we take the supremum becomes smaller);
* \(i_{n+1}\ge i_n\) (the set over which we take the infimum becomes smaller).

Hence the limits

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n,\qquad 
\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}i_n
\]

exist in the extended reals \([-\infty,\infty]\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Inequality \(\displaystyle \liminf x_n\le \limsup x_n\)

For every \(n\),

\[
i_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}\;\le\; \sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}=s_n ,
\]

because the infimum of a set is never larger than its supremum.  
Since the inequality holds for all \(n\), taking limits gives

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}i_n\;\le\;\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n,
\]
i.e.

\[
\boxed{\;\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n\;\le\;\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n\; }.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  Characterization of convergence

Recall two equivalent definitions of \(\limsup\) and \(\liminf\):

* **Tail definition** (used above).  
* **Subsequential definition**:
  \[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{\,\ell:\text{ there is a subsequence }x_{n_k}\to\ell\,\},
  \]
  \[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{\,\ell:\text{ there is a subsequence }x_{n_k}\to\ell\,\}.
  \]

The second description shows that \(\limsup x_n\) is the largest
subsequential limit and \(\liminf x_n\) the smallest one.
Hence \(\liminf x_n\le \limsup x_n\) is immediate, and equality
means that *every* subsequence has the same limit.  

---

#### (⇒)  If \((x_n)\) converges to \(L\).

Then every subsequence also converges to \(L\).  Consequently the
set of subsequential limits is \(\{L\}\), so

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n = \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n = L .
\]

---

#### (⇐)  If \(\displaystyle \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
          =\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n = L\) (finite).

Let \(\varepsilon>0\).  
Because \(\liminf x_n = L\), there exists \(N_1\) such that for all
\(n\ge N_1\),

\[
i_n = \inf_{k\ge n}x_k > L-\varepsilon .
\]

Because \(\limsup x_n = L\), there exists \(N_2\) such that for all
\(n\ge N_2\),

\[
s_n = \sup_{k\ge n}x_k < L+\varepsilon .
\]

Take \(N=\max\{N_1,N_2\}\).  For any \(m\ge N\),

\[
L-\varepsilon < i_N \le x_m \le s_N < L+\varepsilon ,
\]
whence \(|x_m-L|<\varepsilon\).  
Thus \((x_n)\) converges to \(L\).

---

Combining the two directions we obtain

\[
\boxed{\text{A real sequence converges iff }\liminf x_n=\limsup x_n,
       \text{ and in that case the common value is the limit.}}
\]

(If the common value is \(+\infty\) or \(-\infty\), the sequence
diverges to that infinite limit, but the same inequality still holds.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  Summary

1. \(\displaystyle \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n\le\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n\) for every real sequence.
2. The equality \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n=\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n=L\)
   holds precisely when the sequence converges to the finite limit \(L\);
   if \(L=\pm\infty\) the sequence diverges to \(\pm\infty\).
3. Equivalently, \(\limsup\) is the largest subsequential limit and
   \(\liminf\) is the smallest; equality means that there is a *unique*
   subsequential limit, which is the limit of the whole sequence.

Let  

\[
\|\cdot\|_{1},\;\|\cdot\|_{2}
\]
