be two norms on a finite–dimensional real or complex vector space \(V\)
(\(\dim V=n<\infty\)).
We shall prove that there exist constants \(c,C>0\) such that  

\[
c\,\|v\|_{1}\le \|v\|_{2}\le C\,\|v\|_{1}\qquad\forall\,v\in V .
\tag{1}
\]

The constants will be obtained explicitly as the minimum and the maximum of
a continuous function on a compact set.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  The unit sphere for \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\)

Denote

\[
S_{1}=\{v\in V:\|v\|_{1}=1\}.
\]

Because \(V\) is finite dimensional, the topology induced by \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\)
is the Euclidean topology and the closed and bounded sets are compact.
Hence \(S_{1}\) is compact.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  A continuous quotient map

Define the continuous map  

\[
f:S_{1}\longrightarrow \mathbf R_{\ge0},\qquad
f(v)=\frac{\|v\|_{2}}{\|v\|_{1}}=\|v\|_{2}\quad(\text{since } \|v\|_{1}=1).
\]

(If one prefers to keep the denominator, write \(f(v)=\|v\|_{2}/\|v\|_{1}\);
it is the same because on \(S_{1}\) the denominator equals \(1\).)

Since \(f\) is continuous and \(S_{1}\) is compact, \(f\) attains a minimum
and a maximum on \(S_{1}\). Set  

\[
m:=\min_{v\in S_{1}} f(v),\qquad 
M:=\max_{v\in S_{1}} f(v).
\]

Both \(m\) and \(M\) are finite real numbers.  
Because \(\|v\|_{2}\ge0\) and \(v\neq 0\) on \(S_{1}\), we have \(m\ge 0\).
We must show that \(m>0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  The minimum is strictly positive

Assume, by contradiction, that \(m=0\).
Then there exists a sequence \((v_k)\subset S_{1}\) with  
\(f(v_k)\to 0\), i.e. \(\|v_k\|_{2}\to0\).
The set \(S_{1}\) is compact, so a subsequence (still denoted \(v_k\))
converges to some \(v\in S_{1}\).
Continuity of \(\|\cdot\|_{2}\) gives  

\[
\|v\|_{2}=\lim_{k\to\infty}\|v_k\|_{2}=0 .
\]

Thus \(v=0\) (the zero vector is the only vector of zero norm for a
norm).  
But \(v\in S_{1}\) implies \(\|v\|_{1}=1\), a contradiction.
Hence \(m>0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  The inequality (1)

Let \(v\in V\) be arbitrary.

*If \(v=0\)*, (1) holds trivially.

*If \(v\neq 0\)*, write  

\[
u=\frac{v}{\|v\|_{1}}\in S_{1}.
\]

Then \(\|u\|_{1}=1\) and \(v=\|v\|_{1}\,u\).
Using \(m\le f(u)\le M\) we obtain

\[
m\le \frac{\|v\|_{2}}{\|v\|_{1}}\le M
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
m\,\|v\|_{1}\le \|v\|_{2}\le M\,\|v\|_{1}.
\]

Thus (1) holds with the explicit constants  

\[
c=m=\min_{\|w\|_{1}=1}\|w\|_{2},\qquad
C=M=\max_{\|w\|_{1}=1}\|w\|_{2}.
\]

Both constants are strictly positive and finite.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Symmetry

The same argument, interchanging the roles of \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\) and
\(\|\cdot\|_{2}\), gives a pair of constants \(c',C'\) with  

\[
c'\,\|v\|_{2}\le \|v\|_{1}\le C'\,\|v\|_{2},
\]

so the two norms are mutually bounded by each other; they are therefore
equivalent.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence we have shown that **any two norms on a finite‑dimensional
vector space are equivalent**, with explicit bounds

\[
\boxed{\;
\min_{\|w\|_{1}=1}\|w\|_{2}\;\|v\|_{1}\le\|v\|_{2}\le
\max_{\|w\|_{1}=1}\|w\|_{2}\;\|v\|_{1}\quad(\forall v\in V)
\;}
\]

and similarly with the roles of the two norms reversed.  The proof
uses only compactness of the unit sphere and continuity of the
norms; it works over \(\mathbb{R}\) or \(\mathbb{C}\).

**Statement (Identity theorem).**  
Let \(D\subset \mathbb C\) be a domain (open and connected) and  
\(f:D\to\mathbb C\) analytic.  
If the set  

\[
Z=\{\,z\in D : f(z)=0\,\}
\]

has an accumulation point \(z_{0}\in D\) (i.e. a point of \(D\) such that
every neighbourhood of \(z_{0}\) contains infinitely many zeros of \(f\)),
then \(f\equiv0\) on \(D\).

Equivalently, for a non‑trivial analytic function the zeros are isolated;
there is no accumulation point of zeros inside the domain.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof

Choose a small radius \(r>0\) such that the closed disc  

\[
\overline{B(z_{0},r)}=\{\,z:|z-z_{0}|\le r\,\}\subset D .
\]

Because \(f\) is analytic in a neighbourhood of \(z_{0}\), it has a Taylor
expansion there

\[
f(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_{n}(z-z_{0})^{\,n},
\qquad |z-z_{0}|<r ,
\tag{1}
\]
where \(a_{n}=\dfrac{f^{(n)}(z_{0})}{n!}\).

Suppose that the zeros of \(f\) accumulate at \(z_{0}\).  
Then there is a sequence \(z_{k}\ne z_{0}\) with \(z_{k}\to z_{0}\) and
\(f(z_{k})=0\) for all \(k\).

Let \(m\) be the smallest index with \(a_{m}\neq0\).  
(If no such \(m\) exists, all coefficients vanish and \(f\equiv0\) on the
disc; we are done.)  

For \(|z-z_{0}|\) small we can factor the Taylor series:

\[
f(z)= (z-z_{0})^{m}\Bigl(a_{m} + a_{m+1}(z-z_{0})+\cdots\Bigr).
\tag{2}
\]

The bracket is analytic and non‑zero at \(z=z_{0}\), hence it is non‑zero in
a punctured neighbourhood of \(z_{0}\).  
Consequently, near \(z_{0}\) the only zero of \(f\) is \(z_{0}\) itself;
all other points \(z\neq z_{0}\) in the disc satisfy \(f(z)\neq0\).
This contradicts the assumption that the zeros accumulate at \(z_{0}\).

Thus our assumption that some \(a_{m}\neq0\) is false; all Taylor
coefficients vanish:

\[
a_{0}=a_{1}=a_{2}= \cdots =0 .
\]

Hence \(f(z)\equiv0\) for \(|z-z_{0}|<r\).  
Since \(D\) is connected and \(f\) is analytic, the identity theorem
(or the principle of analytic continuation) gives that \(f\equiv0\) on the
whole domain \(D\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Conclusion

If a non‑identically‑zero analytic function \(f\) were to have an
accumulation point of zeros inside its domain, the above argument shows
that \(f\) would have to vanish identically.  
Therefore a non‑trivial analytic function can have only isolated zeros;
the set of its zeros has no accumulation point in the domain.

**Universal property of the tensor product**

> Let \(V\) and \(W\) be vector spaces over a field \(\mathbf F\).
> There exists a vector space \(T\) (called the tensor product of
> \(V\) and \(W\) and denoted \(V\otimes W\)) together with a bilinear
> map  
> \[
> \tau : V\times W \longrightarrow T
> \]
> such that for every vector space \(X\) and every bilinear map
> \(f:V\times W\to X\) there is a unique linear map
> \(\widehat f : T\to X\) with
> \[
> f=\widehat f\circ \tau .
> \]
> The pair \((T,\tau)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism.

The proof is two–fold: we first **construct** a concrete model
\(T\) and show that it satisfies the property; then we show that
any other model is isomorphic to it.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1. Construction of \(V\otimes W\)

Let \(F[V\times W]\) be the *free* \(\mathbf F\)-vector space generated by
the set \(V\times W\).
Elements of \(F[V\times W]\) are finite formal sums
\(\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i (v_i,w_i)\) with \(a_i\in\mathbf F\),
\(v_i\in V\), \(w_i\in W\).

Inside \(F[V\times W]\) we consider the subspace \(S\) generated by
the following elements (for all \(v,v',w,w'\in V,W\) and \(\alpha\in\mathbf F\)):

\[
\begin{aligned}
&(v+v',w)-(v,w)-(v',w),\\
&(v,w+w')-(v,w)-(v,w'),\\
&(\alpha v,w)-\alpha(v,w),\\
&(v,\alpha w)-\alpha(v,w).
\end{aligned}
\]

The subspace \(S\) is exactly the set of relations that encode
bilinearity.

Define  

\[
T := V\otimes W \;:=\; F[V\times W]\,/\,S .
\]

Let \(\pi :F[V\times W]\to T\) be the quotient map and define

\[
\tau(v,w):=\pi\bigl((v,w)\bigr)\qquad(v\in V,\;w\in W).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Lemma 1.  \(\tau\) is bilinear.

*Proof.*  
Take \(w\) fixed. In \(F[V\times W]\) we have
\((v+v',w)=(v,w)+(v',w)\).
Passing to the quotient gives \(\tau(v+v',w)=\tau(v,w)+\tau(v',w)\).
The same holds for addition in the second argument.  
For scalar multiplication, in the free space
\((\alpha v,w)=\alpha(v,w)\); hence the relation
\((\alpha v,w)-\alpha(v,w)\) lies in \(S\),
so in the quotient we obtain
\(\tau(\alpha v,w)=\alpha\,\tau(v,w)\).  
Analogously for scalars in the second coordinate. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2. Existence of the unique linear map for a bilinear map

Let \(X\) be any vector space and let
\(f:V\times W\to X\) be bilinear.

Define a linear map
\[
\Phi :F[V\times W]\longrightarrow X
\]
by linear extension:
\[
\Phi\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i(v_i,w_i)\right)
  := \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i\,f(v_i,w_i).
\]
Because \(f\) is bilinear, \(\Phi\) vanishes on each generator of \(S\):
for example,
\[
\Phi\!\bigl((v+v',w)-(v,w)-(v',w)\bigr)
   =f(v+v',w)-f(v,w)-f(v',w)=0 .
\]
Hence \(\Phi(S)=\{0\}\), so \(\Phi\) factors uniquely through the
quotient:

\[
\widehat f :T\;\longrightarrow\;X
\qquad\text{defined by}\qquad
\widehat f\!\bigl(\pi(u)\bigr)=\Phi(u)\; .
\]

The map \(\widehat f\) is linear, and
for every \((v,w)\in V\times W\),

\[
\widehat f\!\bigl(\tau(v,w)\bigr)
   =\widehat f\!\bigl(\pi((v,w))\bigr)
   =\Phi((v,w))
   =f(v,w).
\]

Thus \(f=\widehat f\circ \tau\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Uniqueness of \(\widehat f\)

Suppose \(g:T\to X\) is linear with \(f=g\circ\tau\).
For any \(v,w\) we have
\(g(\tau(v,w))=f(v,w)=\widehat f(\tau(v,w))\).
Since \(\tau(V\times W)\) spans \(T\) (it is the image of the set
of generators), linearity forces \(g=\widehat f\).
Hence the linear map \(\widehat f\) is unique. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3. Uniqueness of the tensor product up to isomorphism

Let \((T',\tau')\) be another vector space and bilinear map
satisfying the same universal property.
By the universal property applied to \(\tau'\) we obtain a unique
linear map \(\alpha:T\to T'\) with
\(\tau'=\alpha\circ\tau\).
Similarly, applying the property to \(\tau\) yields a linear map
\(\beta:T'\to T\) with \(\tau=\beta\circ\tau'\).

Now
\[
\beta\circ\alpha\circ\tau
   =\beta\circ\tau'
   =\tau ,
\]
so \(\beta\circ\alpha\) is the unique linear map from \(T\) to itself
with \(\beta\circ\alpha\circ\tau=\tau\).  
But the identity map also has this property; by uniqueness
\(\beta\circ\alpha=\operatorname{id}_T\).
Similarly \(\alpha\circ\beta=\operatorname{id}_{T'}\).
Thus \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism, and the pair \((T,\tau)\) is
unique up to unique isomorphism.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion

We have constructed a vector space \(T=V\otimes W\) and a bilinear
map \(\tau:V\times W\to T\) satisfying

\[
\forall\,X\;\forall\,f\in\operatorname{Bil}(V\times W,X)\;
\exists!\,\widehat f\in\operatorname{Lin}(T,X)\;
\text{with}\; f=\widehat f\circ\tau .
\]

This is precisely the *universal property* of the tensor product.
The property guarantees that any other vector space equipped with a
bilinear map having the same property is canonically isomorphic to
\(V\otimes W\).  ∎

**Theorem (Jordan normal form).**  
Let \(A\in M _{n}(\mathbf C)\).  
There exists an invertible matrix \(P\) such that  

\[
P^{-1}AP=
J=\operatorname{diag}\bigl(J_{k_{1,1}}(\lambda _1),\dots ,
J_{k_{m,\ell_m}}(\lambda _m)\bigr),
\]

where each \(\lambda _i\in\mathbf C\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\),
\(J_{k}(\lambda)\) denotes the \(k\times k\) Jordan block with eigenvalue
\(\lambda\), and the blocks are arranged arbitrarily on the diagonal.  
Thus every complex square matrix is similar to a Jordan normal form.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Decomposition into generalized eigenspaces

Because \(\mathbf C\) is algebraically closed, the characteristic polynomial
of \(A\) splits

\[
\chi_A(x)=\prod_{i=1}^{m}(x-\lambda_i)^{d_i}\qquad (\lambda_i\in\mathbf C).
\]

For each eigenvalue set  

\[
V_{\lambda_i}:=\ker\bigl((A-\lambda_iI)^{d_i}\bigr)\subset \mathbf C^{\,n}.
\]

Each \(V_{\lambda_i}\) is \(A\)-invariant and

\[
(A-\lambda_iI)^{d_i}\!\mid_{V_{\lambda_i}}=0 .
\]

The **primary‑decomposition theorem** gives

\[
\mathbf C^{\,n}=V_{\lambda_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus V_{\lambda_m}. \tag{1}
\]

With respect to a basis that is the union of bases for the \(V_{\lambda_i}\),
the matrix of \(A\) is block diagonal,

\[
A=\operatorname{diag}\bigl(A|_{V_{\lambda_1}},\dots ,
A|_{V_{\lambda_m}}\bigr). \tag{2}
\]

Thus it suffices to show that each restriction \(A|_{V_{\lambda}}\) is
similar to a Jordan block matrix.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Nilpotent part

Fix an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) and put \(N:=A-\lambda I\).
Restricted to \(V_{\lambda}\), the operator \(N\) is nilpotent:
\(N^{d}\!|_{V_{\lambda}}=0\) for some \(d\le\dim V_{\lambda}\).

Hence we only have to prove:

> **Lemma.**  
> If \(N\) is a nilpotent linear operator on a complex vector space \(V\),
> then there is a basis of \(V\) in which \(N\) is a direct sum of nilpotent
> Jordan blocks \(J_k(0)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Proof of the lemma (induction on \(\dim V\))

Let \(d=\dim V\).  
If \(d=0\) the statement is trivial.  
Assume \(d>0\) and that the lemma holds for all nilpotent operators on
spaces of dimension \(<d\).

Let \(k\) be the smallest integer with \(N^{k}=0\) (the index of
nilpotency).  
Choose \(v\in V\) with \(N^{k-1}v\neq0\) (such a vector exists because
\(N^{k-1}\neq0\)).  
Set

\[
v_k=v,\qquad v_{k-1}=Nv,\qquad\ldots,\qquad
v_1=N^{k-1}v .
\]

The vectors \(\{v_1,\dots ,v_k\}\) are linearly independent; indeed,
\(N^jv\neq0\) for \(0\le j<k\) while \(N^kv=0\).
Define  

\[
W:=\operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots ,v_k\}.
\]

\(W\) is \(N\)-invariant because \(N v_j=v_{j-1}\) (with \(v_0=0\)),
and \(N|_W\) has matrix \(J_k(0)\) with respect to the ordered basis
\((v_k,\dots ,v_1)\).

Consider the quotient space \(\overline V:=V/W\).  
The induced operator \(\overline N\) on \(\overline V\) is nilpotent
and \(\dim \overline V = d-k < d\).
By the induction hypothesis there exists a basis
\(\{\bar u_1,\dots ,\bar u_{d-k}\}\) of \(\overline V\) such that
\(\overline N\) has Jordan form.

Choose representatives \(u_i\in V\) of the classes \(\bar u_i\).
Then \(N(u_i)\) reduces modulo \(W\) to \(\overline N(\bar u_i)\);
hence the set  

\[
\mathcal B:=\{v_k,\dots ,v_1,u_1,\dots ,u_{d-k}\}
\]

is a basis of \(V\).  
With respect to \(\mathcal B\) the matrix of \(N\) is block diagonal,
the first block being \(J_k(0)\) (coming from \(W\)) and the remaining
blocks the Jordan blocks obtained for \(\overline N\).
Thus \(N\) has Jordan form.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion for the original matrix

For each eigenvalue \(\lambda_i\) we have just proved that
\(N_i:=A-\lambda_iI\) restricted to \(V_{\lambda_i}\) is similar to a
direct sum of nilpotent Jordan blocks.  
Hence

\[
A|_{V_{\lambda_i}}\sim
\lambda_i I+ N_i\;\sim\;
\operatorname{diag}\bigl(J_{k_{i,1}}(\lambda_i),\dots ,
J_{k_{i,\ell_i}}(\lambda_i)\bigr). \tag{3}
\]

Putting the blocks (3) together for all eigenvalues gives a block
diagonal matrix \(J\) of the form required.  
Let \(P\) be the matrix that changes the chosen basis into the
standard basis; then \(P^{-1}AP=J\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Uniqueness (up to order of blocks)

The multiset of eigenvalues together with the sizes of the Jordan
blocks is determined by the invariant factors (or, equivalently,
by the characteristic and minimal polynomials).  
Therefore the Jordan normal form of a complex matrix is unique up to
permutation of its blocks.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Thus every complex square matrix is similar to a Jordan normal form.**  
∎

**Theorem (Chinese remainder theorem for commutative rings).**  
Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with \(1\) and let  

\[
I_{1},I_{2},\dots ,I_{n}\subseteq R
\]

be ideals which are *pairwise comaximal* (i.e. \(I_{i}+I_{j}=R\) for all
\(i\neq j\)).  
Then

1. The natural ring homomorphism  

   \[
   \Phi:R\longrightarrow \prod_{i=1}^{n}R/I_{i},\qquad
   r\longmapsto (r+I_{1},\dots ,r+I_{n})
   \]

   is **surjective**.

2. Its kernel is the intersection of the ideals:

   \[
   \ker\Phi=\bigcap_{i=1}^{n}I_{i}.
   \]

   Moreover, because the ideals are pairwise comaximal we have  

   \[
   \bigcap_{i=1}^{n}I_{i}=I_{1}\cdots I_{n}
   \]
   (the product of the ideals).

Consequently

\[
R/\bigcap_{i=1}^{n}I_{i}\;\cong\;
\prod_{i=1}^{n}R/I_{i},
\qquad
R/(I_{1}\cdots I_{n})\;\cong\;
\prod_{i=1}^{n}R/I_{i}.
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1. Two ideals

Let \(I,J\subseteq R\) be comaximal, i.e. \(I+J=R\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Lemma 1 (surjectivity for two ideals).
For any \(a,b\in R\) there exists \(x\in R\) such that  

\[
x\equiv a\pmod{I},\qquad x\equiv b\pmod{J}.
\]

*Proof.*  
Because \(I+J=R\) we can choose \(u\in I,\;v\in J\) with \(u+v=1\).
Set  

\[
x:=av+bu .
\]

Then

\[
x-a=v(a-b)\in J\quad\text{and}\quad
x-b=u(a-b)\in I ,
\]

hence \(x\equiv a\pmod{I}\) and \(x\equiv b\pmod{J}\). ∎

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Lemma 2 (kernel for two ideals).

\[
I\cap J = IJ .
\]

*Proof.*  
Clearly \(IJ\subseteq I\cap J\).  
Let \(x\in I\cap J\).  
Using the same \(u\in I,\,v\in J\) with \(u+v=1\) as above,

\[
x = x\cdot1 = xu + xv \in IJ .
\]

Thus \(x\in IJ\), proving the equality. ∎

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
From Lemmas 1 and 2 we obtain the standard CRT for two ideals:
\[
R/(I\cap J)\;\cong\;R/I\times R/J .
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2. Induction on the number of ideals

Assume the theorem holds for \(n-1\) pairwise comaximal ideals.
Let \(I_{1},\dots ,I_{n}\) be pairwise comaximal.
Set  

\[
J:=I_{1}\cdots I_{n-1} .
\]

Because the ideals are pairwise comaximal, \(J+I_{n}=R\).
Applying the two‑ideal case to \(J\) and \(I_{n}\) gives

\[
R/(J\cap I_{n})\;\cong\; R/J\times R/I_{n}. \tag{1}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Kernel

Since \(J+I_{n}=R\), Lemma 2 for the pair \((J,I_{n})\) yields  
\(J\cap I_{n}=J\,I_{n}=I_{1}\cdots I_{n}\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Surjectivity

Let \((\bar a,\bar b)\in R/J\times R/I_{n}\) with
\(a,b\in R\).  
Lemma 1 (applied to the pair \((J,I_{n})\)) furnishes \(x\in R\) such that  

\[
x\equiv a\pmod{J},\qquad x\equiv b\pmod{I_{n}} .
\]

Hence \(\Phi(x)=(\bar a,\bar b)\); thus \(\Phi\) is surjective.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Isomorphism for \(n\) ideals

By the induction hypothesis we already know

\[
R/J \;\cong\; \prod_{i=1}^{n-1}R/I_{i}.
\]

Combining this with (1) gives

\[
R/(I_{1}\cdots I_{n})\;\cong\;
\Bigl(\prod_{i=1}^{n-1}R/I_{i}\Bigr)\times R/I_{n}
\;=\;\prod_{i=1}^{n}R/I_{i}.
\]

Thus the theorem holds for \(n\) ideals.  By induction it holds for every
finite collection of pairwise comaximal ideals.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3. Consequences

* **Uniqueness modulo the intersection.**  
If \(r,s\in R\) satisfy \(r\equiv s\pmod{I_{i}}\) for all \(i\), then
\(r-s\in I_{i}\) for all \(i\), so \(r-s\in\bigcap I_{i}\).
Conversely, if \(r-s\in\bigcap I_{i}\) then \(r\equiv s\) modulo each \(I_{i}\).
Hence the congruence class of \(r\) modulo the intersection is uniquely
determined by its classes modulo the individual ideals.

* **Explicit construction.**  
When \(I_{1}+I_{2}=R\) with \(u\in I_{1}, v\in I_{2}\) and \(u+v=1\),
a solution of \(x\equiv a\pmod{I_{1}},\,x\equiv b\pmod{I_{2}}\) is
\(x=av+bu\).  
For \(n>2\) one can construct a solution iteratively using this
two‑ideal formula.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Hence the Chinese remainder theorem holds for any commutative ring
with unit and any finite family of pairwise comaximal ideals.  
∎

**Urysohn’s Lemma (normal spaces)**  

*Let \(X\) be a normal topological space (i.e. \(X\) is \(T_{1}\) and for every pair of disjoint closed sets there exist disjoint open neighbourhoods).  
Let \(A,B\subseteq X\) be disjoint closed sets.  
Then there exists a continuous map*

\[
f:X\longrightarrow [0,1]
\]

*such that*

\[
f(A)=\{0\}\qquad\text{and}\qquad f(B)=\{1\}.
\]

In other words, the closed sets \(A\) and \(B\) can be separated by a continuous real‑valued function that takes the constant values \(0\) and \(1\) on them respectively.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof

The proof is constructive.  We build a family of open sets indexed by the rational numbers in \([0,1]\) and then define \(f\) from this family.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 1.  Constructing a “nested” family of open sets

Let  

\[
\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}=\{\,q\in\mathbb{Q}\mid 0\le q\le 1\,\}.
\]

We will construct two families of open sets
\[
\{U_{q}\}_{q\in\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}}\quad\text{and}\quad\{V_{q}\}_{q\in\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}}
\]
satisfying

1. **Covering**  
   \(A\subseteq U_{q}\) and \(B\subseteq V_{q}\) for all \(q\).

2. **Disjointness**  
   \(U_{q}\cap V_{q}=\varnothing\) for all \(q\).

3. **Monotonicity**  
   If \(s<t\) then  
   \[
   \overline{U_{s}}\subseteq U_{t}\quad\text{and}\quad \overline{V_{t}}\subseteq V_{s}.
   \]

4. **Density of the rational indices**  
   \(U_{q}\subseteq V_{q'}\) whenever \(q<q'\).

These properties are standard in the proof of Urysohn’s lemma; we sketch how to obtain them.

*Base step:*  
Take \(U_{0}=X\setminus B\) (open because \(B\) is closed).  
Let \(V_{1}=X\setminus A\).

*Inductive step:*  
Assume we have constructed all \(U_{q}\) and \(V_{q}\) for \(q\) in some finite set \(F\subset\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}\) with the required properties.  
Let \(r\) be a rational number not in \(F\).  Choose a finite set \(F'\subset F\) that contains all rationals less than \(r\) and all rationals greater than \(r\).  By the normality of \(X\), we can separate the closed sets

\[
C_{-}=\overline{\,\bigcup_{q<r}U_{q}\,}\quad\text{and}\quad
C_{+}=\overline{\,\bigcup_{q>r}V_{q}\,}
\]

by disjoint open neighbourhoods.  
Define

\[
U_{r}=X\setminus C_{+},\qquad V_{r}=X\setminus C_{-}.
\]

One checks that the monotonicity and disjointness properties are preserved; the construction can be repeated for all rationals in \([0,1]\).  
Thus the families \(\{U_{q}\}\) and \(\{V_{q}\}\) exist.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 2.  Defining the function

For each \(x\in X\) let

\[
S(x)=\{\,q\in\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}\mid x\in U_{q}\,\}.
\]

Because of property (3) above, the set \(S(x)\) is upward closed:  
if \(q\in S(x)\) and \(q<t\) then \(x\in U_{t}\).  
Consequently the set of upper bounds of \(S(x)\) has a well‑defined least element in \([0,1]\).  
Define

\[
f(x)=\inf\{\,q\in\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}\mid x\notin U_{q}\,\}
      =\sup S(x).
\]

Since \(0\le f(x)\le 1\) for all \(x\), \(f:X\to[0,1]\) is a well‑defined map.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 3.  Values on \(A\) and \(B\)

*If \(x\in A\):*  
From property (1) we have \(A\subseteq U_{q}\) for every \(q\).  
Hence \(S(x)=\mathbb{Q}_{[0,1]}\) and \(f(x)=0\).

*If \(x\in B\):*  
Similarly, \(B\subseteq V_{q}\) for all \(q\), and by property (2) we have \(U_{q}\cap V_{q}=\varnothing\).  
Thus \(x\notin U_{q}\) for every \(q>0\), so \(S(x)=\{0\}\) and \(f(x)=1\).

Therefore \(f(A)=\{0\}\) and \(f(B)=\{1\}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### 4.  Continuity of \(f\)

Let \(U\subseteq [0,1]\) be an open interval.  
We show that \(f^{-1}(U)\) is open in \(X\).

Take \(x\in f^{-1}(U)\) and let \(a<b\) be rational numbers such that \(a<b\) and \(a<b\le f(x)\le b\).  
By definition of \(f(x)\), we have \(x\in U_{a}\) and \(x\notin U_{b}\).  
Because \(U_{a}\) is open, choose an open neighbourhood \(W\subseteq U_{a}\) of \(x\).  
For any \(y\in W\),

* \(y\in U_{a}\) (by openness of \(U_{a}\)), so \(f(y)\ge a\).
* If \(f(y)<b\), then \(y\in U_{b}\), which would contradict \(x\notin U_{b}\) and the monotonicity of the \(U_{q}\)’s.  
  Hence \(f(y)\ge b\) is impossible; thus \(f(y)\le b\).

So \(f(y)\in[a,b]\subseteq U\).  
Thus \(W\subseteq f^{-pre}(U)\), showing that \(f^{-1}(U)\) is a neighbourhood of \(x\).

The same argument works for intervals of the form \((c,1]\) and \([0,d)\).  
Since these intervals form a sub‑basis for the topology on \([0,1]\), it follows that \(f\) is continuous.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Conclusion

We have constructed a continuous function \(f:X\to[0,1]\) with \(f(A)=\{0\}\) and \(f(B)=\{1\}\).  
Hence Urysohn’s lemma holds for all normal topological spaces. ∎

Let \(X\) be a complex Banach space and \(T\in \mathcal L(X)\) a bounded linear operator.  
Denote  

\[
\sigma(T)=\{\lambda\in\mathbf C:\lambda I-T \text{ is not invertible}\},\qquad  
r(T)=\sup \{|\lambda|:\lambda\in\sigma(T)\}
\]

(the spectral radius).  
The goal is to prove

\[
\boxed{\,r(T)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\, }         \tag{1}
\]

(also called Gelfand’s spectral radius formula).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 1.  Preliminary facts

* **Spectral mapping theorem.**  
  For every \(n\in\mathbb N\),

  \[
  \sigma(T^{\,n})=\{\lambda ^n:\lambda\in\sigma(T)\}\, .
  \]

  Consequently  

  \[
  r(T^{\,n})=\sup_{\lambda\in\sigma(T)}|\lambda|^n=r(T)^n .      \tag{2}
  \]

* **Resolvent estimate.**  
  If \(|\lambda|>r(T)\) then \(\lambda\in\rho(T)\) and

  \[
  \|(\lambda I-T)^{-1}\|
        \le\frac{1}{|\lambda|-r(T)}.                              \tag{3}
  \]

  Proof of (3).  Put \(S=\lambda^{-1}T\).  Then \(r(S)=r(T)/|\lambda|<1\).  
  By the Neumann series,
  \[
  (I-S)^{-1}= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}S^{\,k},
  \qquad
  \|(I-S)^{-1}\|\le\frac{1}{1-r(S)}=
   \frac{1}{1-\frac{r(T)}{|\lambda|}}
   =\frac{|\lambda|}{|\lambda|-r(T)} .
  \]
  Multiplying by \(\lambda^{-1}\) gives (3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 2.  The “easy” inequality

From (2) we have \(r(T)=r(T^{\,n})^{1/n}\).  By the definition of the
spectral radius,

\[
r(T^{\,n})\le\|T^{\,n}\| .
\]

Thus for every \(n\),

\[
r(T)\le\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}.                                   \tag{4}
\]

Taking the infimum over \(n\) yields

\[
r(T)\le\inf_{n\ge1}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}.                        \tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 3.  The reverse inequality

Let \(\rho>r(T)\).  On the circle \(|z|=\rho\) we have
\(\rho\notin\sigma(T)\); therefore \((zI-T)^{-1}\) is analytic there.
Using the Cauchy integral formula for the holomorphic functional calculus,
for every integer \(n\ge0\)

\[
T^{\,n}=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|z|=\rho}z^{\,n}(zI-T)^{-1}\,dz .
\]

Taking norms and using (3),

\[
\|T^{\,n}\|
  \le\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{|z|=\rho} |z|^{\,n}\,
         \|(zI-T)^{-1}\|\,|dz|
  \le\frac{1}{2\pi}\,(2\pi\rho)\,
        \frac{\rho^{\,n}}{\rho-r(T)}
  =\frac{\rho^{\,n+1}}{\rho-r(T)} .
\]

Hence

\[
\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}
   \le\rho\,
        \frac{\rho^{1/n}}{(\rho-r(T))^{1/n}} .
\tag{6}
\]

Letting \(n\to\infty\) in (6) gives

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\le\rho .
\]

Since \(\rho>r(T)\) was arbitrary, we obtain

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\le r(T).                \tag{7}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 4.  Existence of the limit

Set \(a_n=\log\|T^{\,n}\|\).  The submultiplicativity of the norm,
\(\|T^{\,m+n}\|\le\|T^{\,m}\|\|T^{\,n}\|\), yields

\[
a_{m+n}\le a_m+a_n ,
\]

so \(\{a_n\}\) is a subadditive sequence.  
By Fekete’s lemma,

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{n}
   =\inf_{n\ge1}\frac{a_n}{n}
   =\inf_{n\ge1}\log\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n} .
\]

Exponentiating gives

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}
   =\inf_{n\ge1}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}.                           \tag{8}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### 5.  Conclusion

From (5) and (7) we have

\[
r(T)\le\inf_{n}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\le
      \limsup_{n}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\le r(T),
\]

hence all inequalities are equalities.  By (8) the limit exists, so

\[
\boxed{\,r(T)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\,}.
\]

This is Gelfand’s spectral radius formula for bounded linear operators on
Banach spaces.  ∎

Let \(X\) be a complex Banach space and let  
\(T\in\mathcal L(X)\) be bounded.  
For a bounded operator we denote

\[
\sigma(T)=\{\lambda\in\mathbf C:\lambda I-T \text{ is not invertible}\},
\qquad  
r(T)=\sup\{|\lambda|:\lambda\in\sigma(T)\}.
\]

The aim is to show

\[
\boxed{\;r(T)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^{\,n}\|^{1/n}\;}
\tag{1}
\]

(the limit exists; it is the *spectral radius* of \(T\)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Existence of the limit

The sequence \(\alpha _n=\|T^n\|\) is **submultiplicative**:
\[
\|T^{m+n}\|=\|T^mT^n\|\le \|T^m\|\,\|T^n\| .
\]
Hence the logarithms \(b_n=\log\alpha _n\) are subadditive:
\(b_{m+n}\le b_m+b_n\).
By Fekete’s lemma

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{b_n}{n}
   =\inf_{n\ge1}\frac{b_n}{n}.
\]

Exponentiating gives the existence of the limit
\(\displaystyle L:=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^n\|^{1/n}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Lower estimate \(r(T)\le L\)

For each \(n\ge1\) the spectral mapping theorem gives

\[
\sigma(T^n)=\{\lambda^n:\lambda\in\sigma(T)\}.
\]

Consequently

\[
r(T^n)=\sup\{|\lambda^n|:\lambda\in\sigma(T)\}=(r(T))^n .
\]

By the definition of the spectral radius we have
\(r(T^n)\le\|T^n\|\).  Taking \(n\)-th roots,

\[
r(T)=\bigl(r(T^n)\bigr)^{1/n}\le\|T^n\|^{1/n}\qquad(n\ge1).
\]

Hence \(r(T)\le L\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Upper estimate \(L\le r(T)\)

Let \(\lambda\in\mathbf C\) satisfy \(|\lambda|>r(T)\).  
Since \(|\lambda|>r(T)\), the Neumann series for the resolvent
converges:

\[
(\lambda I-T)^{-1}
   =\frac1\lambda\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\frac{T}{\lambda}\Bigr)^k .
\]

Taking norms gives

\[
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\|T^k\|}{|\lambda|^{k+1}}
      \le\|(\lambda I-T)^{-1}\|.
\]

In particular, for each \(n\ge0\),

\[
\frac{\|T^n\|}{|\lambda|^{\,n+1}}\le\|(\lambda I-T)^{-1}\|,
\qquad\text{hence}\qquad
\|T^n\|\le|\lambda|^{\,n+1}\|(\lambda I-T)^{-1}\|.
\]

Taking \(n\)-th roots,

\[
\|T^n\|^{1/n}\le
|\lambda|\Bigl(\|(\lambda I-T)^{-1}\|\Bigr)^{1/n}|\lambda|^{1/n}
          \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}|\lambda|.
\]

Thus

\[
L=\limsup_{n\to\infty}\|T^n\|^{1/n}\le|\lambda|
\qquad\text{for every }|\lambda|>r(T).
\]

Letting \(|\lambda|\downarrow r(T)\) yields \(L\le r(T)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Conclusion

Combining the two inequalities,

\[
r(T)\le L\le r(T),
\]

so \(L=r(T)\).  
Since the limit \(L\) exists, we obtain the spectral radius formula

\[
\boxed{\,r(T)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|T^n\|^{1/n}\,}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

*Remark.*  
The proof works in any Banach algebra.  The crucial facts used are
the submultiplicativity of the norm, the spectral mapping theorem,
and the Neumann–series representation of the resolvent.

**Hahn–Banach – normed‑space version**

Let \(X\) be a real (or complex) normed space, \(Y\subset X\) a linear subspace and  
\(f:Y\to \mathbb K\;(=\mathbb R\text{ or }\mathbb C)\) a bounded linear functional.  
We denote  

\[
M:=\|f\|=\sup \{|f(y)|:y\in Y,\ \|y\|\le 1\}<\infty .
\]

The theorem states that there exists a bounded linear functional  
\(F:X\to\mathbb K\) such that

\[
F|_{Y}=f , \qquad \|F\|=M .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  A preliminary lemma – extending by one vector

Let \(x_{0}\in X\setminus Y\) and put \(Z:=\operatorname{span}(Y,\{x_{0}\})\).
We claim that there is a linear functional \(g:Z\to\mathbb K\) such that  

\[
g|_{Y}=f ,\qquad |g(z)|\le M\|z\|\quad(z\in Z).
\]

*Proof.*  
For \(y\in Y\) set  

\[
a(y)=-f(y)-M\|y+x_{0}\| ,\qquad  
b(y)=-f(y)+M\|y+x_{0}\|.
\]

Each \(a(y)\le b(y)\).  Let  

\[
A:=\bigcap_{y\in Y}[a(y),\,b(y)] .
\]

We show that \(A\neq\varnothing\).  
Take any \(y_{1},y_{2}\in Y\).  
Using \(|f(y_{2}-y_{1})|\le M\|y_{2}-y_{1}\|\) and the triangle
inequality for the norm we obtain

\[
\begin{aligned}
b(y_{2})-a(y_{1})
&=-f(y_{2})+M\|y_{2}+x_{0}\|+f(y_{1})+M\|y_{1}+x_{0}\|\\
&=f(y_{1}-y_{2})+M\bigl(\|y_{1}+x_{0}\|+\|y_{2}+x_{0}\|\bigr)\\
&\ge -M\|y_{1}-y_{2}\|
   +M\bigl(\|y_{1}+x_{0}\|+\|y_{2}+x_{0}\|\bigr)\\
&\ge0 ,
\end{aligned}
\]

because \(\|y_{1}+x_{0}\|+\|y_{2}+x_{0}\|\ge\|y_{1}-y_{2}\|\) by the
triangle inequality applied to the vectors \(y_{1}+x_{0}\) and
\(y_{2}+x_{0}\).
Thus the right end of the interval for \(y_{2}\) is at least the left
end of the interval for \(y_{1}\); the same holds for every pair
\(y_{1},y_{2}\).  Consequently the intersection \(A\) is a non‑empty
closed interval.  Pick any \(c\in A\).

Define \(g\) on \(Z\) by  
\(g(y+\alpha x_{0})=f(y)+\alpha c\quad (y\in Y,\;\alpha\in\mathbb K)\).
Because \(c\in[a(y),b(y)]\) for every \(y\), a simple case–by–case check
(using \(\alpha>0\), \(\alpha<0\) and \(\alpha=0\)) shows that

\[
|g(y+\alpha x_{0})|
=|f(y)+\alpha c|
\le M\,\|y+\alpha x_{0}\| ,
\]

hence \(g\) is the desired extension.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Zorn’s lemma

Let \(\mathcal S\) be the collection of pairs \((T,U)\) where

* \(U\) is a linear subspace of \(X\) containing \(Y\);
* \(T:U\to\mathbb K\) is linear, \(T|_{Y}=f\) and \(|T(u)|\le M\|u\|\)
  for all \(u\in U\).

Order \(\mathcal S\) by inclusion:
\((T_{1},U_{1})\le (T_{2},U_{2})\) iff \(U_{1}\subseteq U_{2}\) and
\(T_{2}|_{U_{1}}=T_{1}\).

*Chain property.*  
Let \(\{(T_{i},U_{i})\}_{i\in I}\) be a chain in \(\mathcal S\) and
put \(U_{0}=\bigcup_{i\in I}U_{i}\).
If \(u\in U_{0}\) then \(u\in U_{i}\) for some \(i\), and the definition
of a chain guarantees that \(T_{i}(u)\) does not depend on the chosen
\(i\).  Thus a well‑defined linear map \(T_{0}:U_{0}\to\mathbb K\) is
obtained, and \((T_{0},U_{0})\) is an upper bound of the chain.

Therefore every chain has an upper bound; by Zorn’s lemma
there exists a maximal element \((F,X_{0})\) of \(\mathcal S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Maximality forces \(X_{0}=X\)

Assume that \(X_{0}\neq X\) and pick \(x_{0}\in X\setminus X_{0}\).
By the lemma of §1 we can extend \(F\) from \(X_{0}\) to the larger
subspace \(\operatorname{span}(X_{0},\{x_{0}\})\) while preserving the
bound \(|\cdot|\le M\|\cdot\|\).
This contradicts the maximality of \((F,X_{0})\).
Hence \(X_{0}=X\) and \(F\) is defined on all of \(X\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  The extension has the same norm

Because \(|F(x)|\le M\|x\|\) for all \(x\in X\), \(\|F\|\le M\).
But \(F|_{Y}=f\), so \(\|F\|\ge \|f\|=M\).
Thus \(\|F\|=M\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Conclusion

For every normed space \(X\), subspace \(Y\subset X\), and bounded
linear functional \(f:Y\to\mathbb K\) there exists an extension
\(F:X\to\mathbb K\) such that \(F|_{Y}=f\) and \(\|F\|=\|f\|\).

This is the normed‑space version of the Hahn–Banach theorem.  The proof
uses only the elementary properties of norms, the triangle inequality,
and Zorn’s lemma.  ∎

## Fubini’s theorem for integrable functions on product measure spaces

Let  

\[
(X,\Sigma ,\mu ),\qquad (Y,\mathcal T ,\nu )
\]

be **σ–finite** measure spaces and let  

\[
\mu\!\times\!\nu
\]

denote the product measure on the product σ–algebra  
\(\Sigma\otimes\mathcal T\).  
For \(f:X\times Y\to\mathbb R\) (or \(\mathbb C\)) we write  

\[
f_x(y):=f(x,y),\qquad f^y(x):=f(x,y).
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Tonelli’s theorem (non‑negative case)

> **Theorem (Tonelli).**  
> If \(f\ge0\) is \((\Sigma\otimes\mathcal T)\)-measurable, then  
> \[
> \int_{X\times Y} f\,d(\mu\times\nu)
>   =\int_X\!\!\left(\int_Y f(x,y)\,d\nu(y)\right)d\mu(x)
>   =\int_Y\!\!\left(\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)\right)d\nu(y),
> \]
> where all integrals are allowed to be \(+\infty\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  Fubini’s theorem (integrable case)

> **Theorem (Fubini).**  
> Let \(f\) be \((\Sigma\otimes\mathcal T)\)-measurable and  
> \[
> \int_{X\times Y}|f|\,d(\mu\times\nu)<\infty .
> \]
> Then  
> 1. For \(\mu\)-a.e. \(x\in X\) the section \(f_x(\cdot)\) belongs to
>    \(L^1(\nu)\); for \(\nu\)-a.e. \(y\in Y\) the section \(f^y(\cdot)\)
>    belongs to \(L^1(\mu)\).  
> 2. The functions  
>    \[
>    g(x):=\int_Y f(x,y)\,d\nu(y),\qquad
>    h(y):=\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)
>    \]
>    are \(\mu\)- and \(\nu\)-integrable, respectively.  
> 3. **Iterated integrals equal the double integral:**
>    \[
>    \int_{X\times Y} f\,d(\mu\times\nu)
>      =\int_X g(x)\,d\mu(x)
>      =\int_Y h(y)\,d\nu(y).
>    \]

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

## Proof

We divide the proof into three parts.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
### Step 1 – Simple functions

Let

\[
s(x,y)=\sum_{k=1}^m c_k\,\mathbf 1_{A_k\times B_k}(x,y),
\qquad 
A_k\in\Sigma,\;B_k\in\mathcal T,\;c_k\in\mathbb R .
\]

For each fixed \(x\),

\[
s_x(y)=\sum_{k=1}^m c_k\,\mathbf 1_{B_k}(y)\mathbf 1_{A_k}(x),
\]
so

\[
\int_Y s_x(y)\,d\nu(y)
 =\sum_{k=1}^m c_k\,\mathbf 1_{A_k}(x)\,\nu(B_k)
 =:g(x).
\]

Hence \(g\) is \(\Sigma\)-measurable.  Similarly

\[
h(y)=\sum_{k=1}^m c_k\,\mathbf 1_{A_k}(x)\,\mu(A_k)
\]
is \(\mathcal T\)-measurable.

Moreover

\[
\int_{X\times Y}s\,d(\mu\times\nu)
  =\sum_{k=1}^m c_k\,\mu(A_k)\nu(B_k)
  =\int_X g(x)\,d\mu(x)
  =\int_Y h(y)\,d\nu(y).
\]

Thus the theorem holds for all simple functions.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
### Step 2 – Non‑negative measurable functions (Tonelli)

Let \(f\ge0\) be measurable.  
Choose an increasing sequence of non‑negative simple functions
\(s_n\uparrow f\).  
From Step 1,

\[
\int_{X\times Y}s_n\,d(\mu\times\nu)
   =\int_X\!\!\Bigl(\int_Y s_n\,d\nu\Bigr)d\mu
   =\int_Y\!\!\Bigl(\int_X s_n\,d\mu\Bigr)d\nu .
\]

Letting \(n\to\infty\) and applying the Monotone Convergence Theorem
to each side gives Tonelli’s identity for \(f\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
### Step 3 – Integrable functions (Fubini)

Let \(f\) be integrable, i.e.

\[
\int_{X\times Y}|f|\,d(\mu\times\nu)<\infty .
\]

Write \(f=f^+-f^-\) with \(f^+,f^-\ge0\).  
Tonelli’s theorem applied to \(|f|=f^++f^-\) yields

\[
\int_{X\times Y}|f|\,d(\mu\times\nu)
   =\int_X\!\!\Bigl(\int_Y |f|\,d\nu\Bigr)d\mu
   =\int_Y\!\!\Bigl(\int_X |f|\,d\mu\Bigr)d\nu .
\]

Because the left–hand side is finite, the two inner integrals are
finite for \(\mu\)-a.e. \(x\) and \(\nu\)-a.e. \(y\).  Thus

\[
f_x(\cdot)\in L^1(\nu)\quad\text{for }\mu\text{-a.e. }x,
\qquad
f^y(\cdot)\in L^1(\mu)\quad\text{for }\nu\text{-a.e. }y .
\]

Define

\[
g(x)=\int_Y f(x,y)\,d\nu(y),\qquad
h(y)=\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x).
\]

These are the Lebesgue integrals of the sections and are
finite for almost every \(x\) and \(y\).  
Because \(|g(x)|\le\int_Y|f(x,y)|\,d\nu(y)\), we have

\[
\int_X |g(x)|\,d\mu(x)
 \le\int_X\!\!\Bigl(\int_Y |f|\,d\nu\Bigr)d\mu
 =\int_{X\times Y}|f|\,d(\mu\times\nu)<\infty .
\]

Hence \(g\in L^1(\mu)\).  A similar calculation shows \(h\in L^1(\nu)\).

Finally, using the identity for \(f^+\) and \(f^-\) and linearity,

\[
\int_{X\times Y}f\,d(\mu\times\nu)
 =\int_{X\times Y}f^+\,d(\mu\times\nu)
  -\int_{X\times Y}f^-\,d(\mu\times\nu)
 =\int_X g(x)\,d\mu(x)
 =\int_Y h(y)\,d\nu(y).
\]

This completes the proof of Fubini’s theorem. ∎

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Remarks

* The σ‑finiteness of \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) is essential; without it the
  conclusion may fail (see e.g. the product of two counting measures on an
  uncountable set).
* For functions \(f\) that are merely non‑negative, Tonelli’s theorem
  guarantees the equality of the iterated integrals, but the inner
  integrals may be infinite.
* Fubini’s theorem gives the full picture: integrability of \(f\)
  ensures that all sections are integrable and that the double integral
  equals the iterated integrals.

### Implicit Function Theorem (C¹ version)

Let  

\[
F:\mathbb {R}^{n+m}\longrightarrow \mathbb {R}^{m}\qquad  
F\in C^{1}
\]

and let \((a,b)\in \mathbb {R}^{n}\times \mathbb {R}^{m}\) satisfy  

\[
F(a,b)=0 ,\qquad D_{y}F(a,b)\ \hbox{is an invertible }m\times m\hbox{ matrix}.
\]

Then there exist open neighbourhoods \(U\subset\mathbb {R}^{n}\) of \(a\) and  
\(V\subset\mathbb {R}^{m}\) of \(b\) and a unique function  

\[
g:U\longrightarrow V ,\qquad g\in C^{1}
\]

such that  

\[
F(x,g(x))=0\qquad (x\in U),\qquad g(a)=b
\]

and, for every \(x\in U\), \(g(x)\) is the **only** point in \(V\) with
\(F(x,g(x))=0\).

Moreover

\[
Dg(x)= -\bigl(D_{y}F(x,g(x))\bigr)^{-1}\,D_{x}F(x,g(x))
\qquad(x\in U).
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 1.  Reduction to the Inverse Function Theorem

Define

\[
G:\mathbb {R}^{n}\times\mathbb {R}^{m}\longrightarrow \mathbb {R}^{n}\times\mathbb {R}^{m},
\qquad G(x,y)=(x,\,F(x,y)).
\]

\(G\) is \(C^{1}\).  
Its Jacobian matrix at \((x,y)\) is

\[
DG(x,y)=
\begin{pmatrix}
I_{n}&0\\[2mm]
D_{x}F(x,y)&D_{y}F(x,y)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

At \((a,b)\) the matrix is

\[
DG(a,b)=
\begin{pmatrix}
I_{n}&0\\
D_{x}F(a,b)&D_{y}F(a,b)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Because \(D_{y}F(a,b)\) is invertible, the block triangular matrix \(DG(a,b)\) is
invertible as well.  
Hence the **Inverse Function Theorem** applies to \(G\) at the point \((a,b)\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 2.  Consequences of the Inverse Function Theorem

The theorem yields open neighbourhoods  

\[
U_{1}\subset\mathbb {R}^{n}\times\mathbb {R}^{m}\quad\text{of}\quad(a,b),\qquad
W\subset\mathbb {R}^{n}\times\mathbb {R}^{m}\quad\text{of}\quad G(a,b)=(a,0),
\]

and a \(C^{1}\) diffeomorphism

\[
G\!\mid _{U_{1}}:U_{1}\longrightarrow W
\]

with \(C^{1}\) inverse

\[
G^{-1}:W\longrightarrow U_{1}.
\]

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 3.  Extracting the implicit function

Write a point in \(\mathbb {R}^{n}\times\mathbb {R}^{m}\) as \((x,u)\).
Because the first component of \(G\) is the identity, for any
\((x,u)\in W\) we have

\[
G^{-1}(x,u)=\bigl(x,\; g(x,u)\bigr)
\]

for some \(C^{1}\) map \(g:W\to\mathbb {R}^{m}\).
In particular, for \(u=0\),

\[
G^{-1}(x,0)=\bigl(x,\; g(x,0)\bigr).
\]

Define  

\[
U:=\{x\in\mathbb {R}^{n}\mid (x,0)\in W\}, \qquad
V:=\{\,g(x,0)\mid x\in U\}.
\]

\(U\) and \(V\) are open neighbourhoods of \(a\) and \(b\), respectively, and

\[
g:U\longrightarrow V,\qquad g(x):=g(x,0)
\]

is \(C^{1}\).
For \(x\in U\),

\[
G\bigl(x,g(x)\bigr)=\bigl(x,F(x,g(x))\bigr)=(x,0),
\]

hence \(F(x,g(x))=0\).
Also \(g(a)=b\) because \(G(a,b)=(a,0)\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 4.  Uniqueness

Let \(y\in V\) satisfy \(F(x,y)=0\).
Then \(G(x,y)=(x,0)\).
Since \(G^{-1}\) is the unique inverse of \(G\) on \(W\), we must have  
\(y=g(x)\).  
Thus \(g(x)\) is the *only* element of \(V\) with \(F(x,y)=0\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 5.  Formula for the derivative of \(g\)

From \(F(x,g(x))\equiv 0\) differentiate with respect to \(x\):

\[
D_{x}F(x,g(x))+D_{y}F(x,g(x))\,Dg(x)=0 .
\]

Solving for \(Dg(x)\) gives

\[
Dg(x)=-\bigl(D_{y}F(x,g(x))\bigr)^{-1}\,D_{x}F(x,g(x)),
\]

which is again \(C^{1}\) on \(U\).

---------------------------------------------------------------------

### Conclusion

The map \(g:U\to V\) constructed above is \(C^{1}\), satisfies
\(F(x,g(x))=0\) for all \(x\in U\), is uniquely defined near \(a\),
and its derivative is given by the explicit formula above.
This completes the proof of the implicit function theorem for \(C^{1}\)
maps between Euclidean spaces.

### Inverse Function Theorem (Euclidean version)

> **Theorem.**  
> Let \(U\subset \mathbb {R}^{n}\) be open, \(f:U\to\mathbb {R}^{n}\) a \(C^{1}\) map, and \(x_{0}\in U\).  
> Assume that the Jacobian matrix  
> \[
> A:=Df(x_{0})\in\mathbb {R}^{n\times n}
> \]
> is invertible (\(\det A\neq 0\)).  
> Then there exist open neighbourhoods  
> \[
> V\subset U\quad\text{of }x_{0},\qquad  
> W\subset\mathbb {R}^{n}\quad\text{of }y_{0}:=f(x_{0}),
> \]
> such that

1. \(f:V\to W\) is a diffeomorphism (i.e. \(f\) is bijective, \(C^{1}\) and its inverse \(f^{-1}:W\to V\) is \(C^{1}\));
2. \(\displaystyle Df^{-1}(y)=\bigl(Df(f^{-1}(y))\bigr)^{-1}\) for every \(y\in W\).

The proof below uses only the Mean Value Theorem, the continuity of the derivative, and the Banach Contraction Mapping Principle.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Preparatory lemmas

### Lemma 1 (Local Lipschitz estimate)

Since \(Df\) is continuous at \(x_{0}\), for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\rho>0\) such that

\[
\|Df(x)-A\|<\varepsilon\qquad\text{for all }x\in B_{\rho}(x_{0}),
\]
where \(B_{\rho}(x_{0})=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}:\|x-x_{0}\|<\rho\}\).

*Proof.* Standard consequence of continuity of a matrix‐valued function. ∎



### Lemma 2 (Contraction estimate)

Let \(A\) be invertible.  Then there exists \(\delta>0\) such that
\[
\sup_{x\in B_{\delta}(x_{0})}\|A^{-1}Df(x)\|<1.
\]
*Proof.*  
Take \(\varepsilon\) so small that \(\|A^{-1}\|\,\varepsilon<\tfrac12\).  
Choose \(\rho>0\) from Lemma 1. For \(x\in B_{\rho}(x_{0})\),

\[
\|A^{-1}Df(x)\|
  \le \|A^{-1}\|\;\|Df(x)-A\|+\|A^{-1}A\|
  < \|A^{-1}\|\varepsilon+1
  < \tfrac12+1=\tfrac32.
\]
But we want the sup \(<1\); take \(\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2\|A^{-1}\|}\).  
Then for \(x\in B_{\rho}(x_{0})\)

\[
\|A^{-1}Df(x)\|\le \tfrac12+1=\tfrac32 <1
\]
(we can make it strictly smaller than 1 by halving \(\rho\) if necessary).  ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Construction of the inverse

Fix \(\delta>0\) given by Lemma 2.  
Let

\[
V:=B_{\delta}(x_{0}),\qquad
r:=\frac{\delta}{2\|A^{-1}\|}.
\]

Set \(y_{0}=f(x_{0})\).  
Define a mapping \(G_{y}:V\to\mathbb {R}^{n}\) for each \(y\in\mathbb {R}^{n}\) by

\[
G_{y}(x)=x_{0}+A^{-1}\bigl(f(x)-y\bigr).
\tag{2.1}
\]

---

### Lemma 3 ( \(G_{y}\) is a contraction on \(V\) )

For all \(x\in V\),

\[
\|G_{y}'(x)\|=\|A^{-1}Df(x)\|\le c<1
\]
with \(c\) independent of \(y\). Consequently \(G_{y}\) is a contraction on \(V\).

*Proof.*  
Differentiating (2.1) yields \(G_{y}'(x)=A^{-1}Df(x)\).  
By Lemma 2, \(\|G_{y}'(x)\|\le c<1\) for all \(x\in V\).  
Thus for any \(x,x'\in V\),

\[
\|G_{y}(x)-G_{y}(x')\|
   =\|G_{y}'(\xi)(x-x')\|
   \le c\|x-x'\|
\]
for some \(\xi\) between \(x\) and \(x'\) (Mean Value Theorem in \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\)). ∎



### Lemma 4 (Image of \(G_{y}\) is contained in \(V\))

Let \(y\) satisfy \(\|y-y_{0}\|<r\).  Then \(G_{y}(V)\subset V\).

*Proof.*  
Take \(x\in V\).  By the Mean Value Theorem,
\[
f(x)-f(x_{0})=Df(\xi)(x-x_{0})
\]
for some \(\xi\) between \(x\) and \(x_{0}\). Hence

\[
\|G_{y}(x)-x_{0}\|
 =\|A^{-1}(f(x)-y)\|
 \le \|A^{-1}\|\bigl(\|f(x)-f(x_{0})\|+\|y-y_{0}\|\bigr).
\]

Using the previous identity and the continuity of \(Df\) we can bound
\(\|f(x)-f(x_{0})\|\le \|Df(\xi)\|\,\|x-x_{0}\|\le \|A\|\,\delta\).  
Choose \(r<\delta/(2\|A^{-1}\|)\).  
Then

\[
\|G_{y}(x)-x_{0}\|\le\frac{\delta}{2}+\frac{\delta}{2}=\delta,
\]
so \(G_{y}(x)\in V\). ∎



---

### Lemma 5 (Existence and uniqueness of inverse)

Let \(y\) satisfy \(\|y-y_{0}\|<r\).  
Then there is a unique \(x\in V\) with \(f(x)=y\); moreover \(x\) depends continuously on \(y\).

*Proof.*  
By Lemma 4, \(G_{y}\) maps \(V\) into itself.  
By Lemma 3, \(G_{y}\) is a contraction.  
Hence, by the Banach Contraction Principle, \(G_{y}\) has a unique fixed point \(x^{*}\in V\).  
From (2.1), \(G_{y}(x^{*})=x^{*}\) is equivalent to
\(x_{0}+A^{-1}(f(x^{*})-y)=x^{*}\), i.e. \(f(x^{*})=y\).  
Thus \(f\) is onto \(B_{r}(y_{0})\) and one‑to‑one on \(V\).  
Uniqueness of the fixed point gives uniqueness of the preimage. ∎



Define

\[
W:=B_{r}(y_{0}).
\]

Lemma 5 shows that \(f:V\to W\) is bijective.  
Let \(g:=f^{-1}\) on \(W\).  
From the fixed‑point construction, \(g\) is the unique solution of
\(g(y)=x_{0}+A^{-1}(f(g(y))-y)\).  
The right‑hand side is \(C^{1}\) in \(y\) (composition of \(C^{1}\) maps), and its derivative satisfies

\[
g'(y)=A^{-1}\bigl(I-Df(g(y))\,g'(y)\bigr).
\]

Solving for \(g'(y)\) gives

\[
g'(y)=\bigl(Df(g(y))\bigr)^{-1}.
\]

Thus \(g\) is \(C^{1}\) on \(W\) and \(Df(g(y))\,g'(y)=I\). ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Conclusion

We have shown that for a \(C^{1}\) map \(f:U\to\mathbb {R}^{n}\) with \(Df(x_{0})\) invertible,

* there exist open neighbourhoods \(V\subset U\) of \(x_{0}\) and
  \(W\subset\mathbb {R}^{n}\) of \(y_{0}=f(x_{0})\) such that
  \(f:V\to W\) is a diffeomorphism;

* the inverse \(f^{-1}\) is \(C^{1}\) on \(W\) and satisfies
  \[
  Df^{-1}(y)=\bigl(Df(f^{-1}(y))\bigr)^{-1},\qquad y\in W.
  \]

This is the inverse function theorem for \(C^{1}\) maps between Euclidean spaces. ∎

**Baire Category Theorem (complete metric spaces)**  

Let \((X,d)\) be a complete metric space.  
The following two statements are equivalent

1. **(Dense–open intersection)**  
   Every countable intersection of open dense subsets of \(X\) is dense in \(X\); that is, for any sequence  
   \(\{U_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) of open dense sets one has  
   \[
   \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}U_n \text{ is dense in } X .
   \]

2. **(No countable nowhere‑dense covering)**  
   \(X\) cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense subsets; equivalently, if  
   \(\{F_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) are closed subsets of \(X\) with \(\operatorname{int}F_n=\varnothing\) for every \(n\), then  
   \[
   X\neq\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}F_n .
   \]

The theorem says that complete metric spaces are **Baire spaces**.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof of (1)

Let \(\{U_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) be a sequence of open dense subsets of \(X\).  
We show that \(\bigcap_n U_n\) is dense, i.e. for every non‑empty open set \(V\subset X\) we have  

\[
V\cap\Bigl(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}U_n\Bigr)\neq\varnothing .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Lemma (Cantor intersection for balls)

If \(\{B_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\) is a nested sequence of non‑empty closed balls in a complete metric space with  

\[
\operatorname{diam}B_n \;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\;0 ,
\]

then \(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}B_n\) consists of exactly one point.

*Proof.*  
Let \(B_n=\overline{B}(x_n,r_n)\). The sequence \((x_n)\) is Cauchy because for \(m>n\),

\[
d(x_m,x_n)\le r_n+r_m\longrightarrow0 .
\]

Completeness gives a limit \(x\). Clearly \(x\in B_n\) for all \(n\).  
If \(y\neq x\), then \(d(y,x)=\varepsilon>0\).  
Choose \(N\) so large that \(r_N<\varepsilon/2\).  
Then \(d(y,x_N)\ge d(y,x)-d(x,x_N)\ge \varepsilon/2>r_N\), contradicting \(y\in B_N\).  
Thus the intersection is the singleton \(\{x\}\). ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Construction of nested balls

Let \(V\subset X\) be a non‑empty open set.  
We build inductively a sequence of closed balls  

\[
B_1\supseteq B_2\supseteq B_3\supseteq\cdots
\]

such that  

\[
B_n\subseteq \bigl(U_n\cap B_{n-1}\bigr), \qquad  
\operatorname{diam}B_n<2^{-n}.
\]

*Step 1.*  
Because \(U_1\) is dense and \(V\) is open, \(U_1\cap V\neq\varnothing\).  
Choose \(x_1\in U_1\cap V\).  
Since \(U_1\) is open, there exists \(r_1>0\) with  
\(\overline{B}(x_1,r_1)\subseteq U_1\cap V\).  
Let \(B_1=\overline{B}(x_1,r_1)\) and adjust \(r_1\) so that \(r_1<2^{-1}\).

*Induction step.*  
Assume \(B_{n-1}\) is defined.  
\(U_n\) is dense, so \(U_n\cap B_{n-1}\neq\varnothing\).  
Pick \(x_n\in U_n\cap B_{n-1}\).  
Because \(U_n\) is open, choose \(r_n>0\) with  
\(\overline{B}(x_n,r_n)\subseteq U_n\cap B_{n-1}\).  
Set \(B_n=\overline{B}(x_n,r_n)\) and pick \(r_n<2^{-n}\).  
Then \(B_n\subseteq B_{n-1}\) and \(\operatorname{diam}B_n=2r_n<2^{-n+1}\), so the diameters tend to \(0\).

Thus we have a nested sequence of closed balls with diameters tending to zero.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
#### Applying the lemma

By the lemma, \(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}B_n=\{x\}\) for some \(x\in X\).  
Each \(B_n\subseteq U_n\), so \(x\in U_n\) for every \(n\).  
Moreover \(B_1\subseteq V\), hence \(x\in V\).  
Thus \(x\in V\cap\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}U_n\).

Since \(V\) was an arbitrary non‑empty open set, the intersection \(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}U_n\) meets every open set; it is therefore dense.  
∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Proof of (2)

Assume, for contradiction, that \(X=\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}F_n\) with each \(F_n\) closed and \(\operatorname{int}F_n=\varnothing\).  
Set \(G_n=X\setminus F_n\); each \(G_n\) is open and dense.  
By part (1), \(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}G_n\) is dense.  
But  

\[
\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}G_n
 = X\setminus \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}F_n
 = X\setminus X
 =\varnothing .
\]

This is impossible, because a dense set cannot be empty.  
Hence such a representation cannot exist.

∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Remarks

* The Baire category theorem is a fundamental tool in analysis; it underlies many existence proofs (e.g., existence of continuous nowhere‑differentiable functions, existence of Banach spaces where the generic element is “bad” in a precise sense, etc.).  
* Completeness is essential: the theorem fails for spaces that are not complete (e.g., the rational numbers with the usual metric).  
* The two formulations (dense‑open intersection and “no countable nowhere‑dense covering”) are equivalent and are often used interchangeably.  

This completes the proof of the Baire category theorem for complete metric spaces.

**Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**

*Every non‑constant complex polynomial has at least one zero.*

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  Preparations  

Let  

\[
p(z)=a_{n}z^{n}+a_{n-1}z^{\,n-1}+\dots +a_{1}z+a_{0},
\qquad a_{n}\neq 0,\; n\ge 1 .
\]

For \(R>0\) denote by \(D_{R}=\{\,z:|z|\le R\,\}\) the closed disc of radius \(R\).

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  A lemma:  growth of a polynomial

**Lemma 1.**  
There exists \(R_{0}>0\) such that for all \(|z|\ge R_{0}\)

\[
|p(z)|\ge \frac{|a_{n}|}{2}\,|z|^{n}.      \tag{1}
\]

*Proof.*  By the triangle inequality

\[
|p(z)|\ge |a_{n}||z|^{n}-|a_{n-1}||z|^{n-1}-\dots-|a_{0}|.
\]

Choose \(R_{0}\) so large that  

\[
|a_{n-1}||z|^{n-1}+ \dots +|a_{0}| \le \frac{|a_{n}|}{2}|z|^{n}
\quad\text{whenever } |z|\ge R_{0}.
\]

This is possible because the right‑hand side grows like \( |z|^{n}\)
while the left‑hand side grows at most like \(|z|^{n-1}\).
Hence (1) holds. ∎



-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Liouville’s theorem

> **Liouville.**  If \(f\) is an entire (holomorphic on \(\mathbb C\)) function
>  which is bounded on \(\mathbb C\), then \(f\) is constant.

*Sketch of proof.*  
Let \(f\) be entire and \(|f(z)|\le M\) for all \(z\).
By Cauchy’s integral formula,

\[
f^{(k)}(a)=\frac{k!}{2\pi i}\int_{|z-a|=R}\frac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{k+1}}\;dz
\]

and therefore  

\[
|f^{(k)}(a)|\le\frac{k!M}{R^{k}}\qquad(\forall R>0).
\]

Letting \(R\to\infty\) gives \(f^{(k)}(a)=0\) for every \(k\ge 1\); hence
\(f\) has no non‑zero derivatives and is constant. ∎



-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra

Assume, to the contrary, that \(p\) has **no** zero in \(\mathbb C\).
Define

\[
f(z)=\frac{1}{p(z)} .
\]

Because \(p\) is entire and never vanishes, \(f\) is an entire function.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 4.1  \(f\) is bounded

Take \(R_{0}\) from Lemma 1.
For \(|z|\ge R_{0}\),

\[
|f(z)|=\frac{1}{|p(z)|}\le
\frac{2}{|a_{n}|\,|z|^{\,n}}\le \frac{2}{|a_{n}|\,R_{0}^{\,n}} .
\]

Inside the disc \(D_{R_{0}}\) the function \(f\) is continuous and therefore
attains a maximum.  Set

\[
M=\max_{|z|\le R_{0}}\ |f(z)|\;+\;\frac{2}{|a_{n}|\,R_{0}^{\,n}}\;.
\]

Then \(|f(z)|\le M\) for every \(z\in\mathbb C\).
Thus \(f\) is a bounded entire function.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 4.2  Consequence of Liouville

By Liouville’s theorem, \(f\) is constant:
\(f(z)\equiv c\) for some \(c\in\mathbb C\).

Consequently

\[
p(z)=\frac{1}{c}\quad\text{for all }z\in\mathbb C,
\]

i.e. \(p\) is constant.  
But \(p\) was assumed to be a non‑constant polynomial—contradiction.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Therefore our assumption that \(p\) has no zero is false.
Hence every non‑constant complex polynomial \(p(z)\) possesses at least
one complex zero. ∎



-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Remarks

* The proof above uses only elementary facts of complex analysis
  (triangle inequality, Cauchy’s integral formula, Liouville’s theorem).
* The result can also be proved topologically via the argument principle
  or algebraically by showing that \(\mathbb C\) is an algebraically closed
  field.  
  The Liouville proof is one of the most celebrated analytic proofs of
  the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

**Theorem (Perron–Frobenius, existence part).**  
Let  

\[
A=(a_{ij})_{i,j=1}^{n}\qquad a_{ij}>0\quad (1\le i,j\le n)
\]

be a *positive* \(n\times n\) matrix.  
Then there exist a number \(\rho>0\) and a vector \(x\in\mathbb {R}^{n}\) with  
\(x_{i}>0\;(1\le i\le n)\) such that  

\[
Ax=\rho\,x .
\]

The scalar \(\rho\) is the *spectral radius* \(r(A)=\max \{|\lambda|:\lambda\in\sigma(A)\}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  The set of positive unit vectors

Let  

\[
S=\Bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\;:\;x_{i}>0\ (1\le i\le n),\;
\|x\|_{1}=1\Bigr\},
\]

where \(\|x\|_{1}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}|x_{i}|\).  
Because all coordinates are positive and the \(\ell ^1\)–norm is fixed,
\(S\) is a compact convex subset of \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  A self‑map of \(S\)

Define  

\[
\Phi:S\longrightarrow S ,\qquad 
\Phi(x)=\frac{Ax}{\|Ax\|_{1}}\; .
\]

*Why is \(\Phi\) well defined?*  
Since all entries of \(A\) are positive, for any \(x\in S\) we have
\((Ax)_i=\sum_{j=1}^{n}a_{ij}x_{j}>0\).  
Hence \(Ax\) has strictly positive components and therefore
\(\|Ax\|_{1}>0\); the quotient is again a positive vector of
\(\ell ^1\)–norm 1, i.e. \(\Phi(x)\in S\).

*Continuity.*  
Both the map \(x\mapsto Ax\) and the norm \(\|\cdot\|_{1}\) are continuous,
hence \(\Phi\) is continuous on the compact set \(S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Existence of a fixed point

By the **Brouwer Fixed–Point Theorem** (any continuous map from a
compact convex set to itself has a fixed point), there exists
\(x\in S\) such that \(\Phi(x)=x\).

The fixed‑point equation reads

\[
x=\frac{Ax}{\|Ax\|_{1}}
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
Ax=\|Ax\|_{1}\,x .
\]

Let  

\[
\rho:=\|Ax\|_{1}>0 .
\]

Then \(x\) satisfies \(Ax=\rho\,x\) and all its components are
strictly positive.  Thus a positive eigenvector exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  The eigenvalue equals the spectral radius

For every positive vector \(y>0\) the **Collatz–Wielandt inequality** holds:

\[
\min_{i}\frac{(Ay)_i}{y_i}\;\le\;r(A)\;\le\;
\max_{i}\frac{(Ay)_i}{y_i}.
\tag{1}
\]

The inequality is proved by observing that  
\(r(A)\le \|A\|_{1}\) and that for each \(i\),

\[
(Ay)_i\le\|A\|_{1}\,y_i,\qquad
(Ay)_i\ge\min_{k}a_{ik}\,y_k .
\]

Applying (1) to the fixed point \(x\) gives

\[
\frac{(Ax)_i}{x_i}=\frac{\rho\,x_i}{x_i}=\rho\quad\text{for all }i,
\]

hence both the minimum and the maximum in (1) equal \(\rho\).
Thus

\[
r(A)=\rho .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Summary

We have shown that for a strictly positive matrix \(A\)

* there exists a scalar \(\rho>0\) and a vector \(x>0\) with
  \(Ax=\rho x\);
* this scalar is precisely the spectral radius \(r(A)\).

This is the existence part of the Perron–Frobenius theorem for positive matrices. ∎

**Sard’s Theorem (classical statement)**  

Let  

\[
f:\;\mathbb R^{\,n}\longrightarrow \mathbb R^{\,m}
\]

be a \(C^{k}\) map with  

\[
k>\max \{\,n-m,\,0\,\}\; .
\]

Denote by  

\[
C:=\{x\in \mathbb R^{n}\mid \operatorname{rank}Df(x)<m\}
\]

the *critical set* of \(f\) and by  

\[
f(C)=\{\,f(x)\mid x\in C\,\}
\]

its set of *critical values*.  
**Sard’s theorem** states that

\[
\boxed{\; \mathcal L^{m}\bigl(f(C)\bigr)=0\;}
\]

i.e. the set of critical values has Lebesgue measure zero in \(\mathbb R^{m}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Outline of the proof

The proof is an exercise in local normal forms and dimensionality arguments.  
We proceed in four steps.

---

#### 1.  Decompose the critical set by rank

For each integer \(r\) with \(0\le r<m\) put  

\[
C_{r}:=\{x\in\mathbb R^{n}\mid \operatorname{rank}Df(x)=r\}.
\]

The critical set is the disjoint union  

\[
C=\bigcup_{r=0}^{m-1}C_{r}.
\]

*Rank theorem.*  
Because \(f\) is \(C^{k}\) with \(k> \max\{n-m,0\}\), the Rank Theorem (a corollary of the Implicit Function Theorem) applies.  
For any \(x_{0}\in C_{r}\) there exist neighborhoods \(U\ni x_{0}\) and \(V\ni f(x_{0})\), together with \(C^{k}\) diffeomorphisms

\[
\Phi:U\to\mathbb R^{n}, \qquad \Psi:V\to\mathbb R^{m},
\]

such that

\[
\Psi\!\circ f\!\circ\Phi^{-1}(u)=\bigl(u_{1},\dots,u_{r},\,0,\dots,0\bigr)+
\text{(higher‑order terms)} .
\]

In particular, near \(x_{0}\) the set \(C_{r}\) is a \(C^{k}\) submanifold of
dimension  

\[
\dim C_{r}\le n-(m-r)\, .
\]

Indeed, the condition “rank \(=r\)” imposes \(r(m-r)\) independent
equations on the \(n\) variables; the rank theorem shows that the solution
set is locally a graph of a smooth map.

---

#### 2.  Local form of \(f\) on a critical submanifold

Using the coordinate change above, on \(U\) the map \(f\) has the form

\[
u\;\longmapsto\;
\bigl(u_{1},\dots,u_{r},\,G(u_{1},\dots,u_{n})\bigr),
\]

where the last \(m-r\) coordinates \(G\) vanish to first order along
\(C_{r}\).  
Restricting to \(C_{r}\) we can write \(u_{r+1},\dots,u_{n}\) as smooth
functions of \(u_{1},\dots,u_{r}\).  Consequently

\[
f\bigl(C_{r}\cap U\bigr)
\subset \{(y_{1},\dots,y_{r},0,\dots,0)\in\mathbb R^{m}\}.
\]

Thus locally the image of a piece of \(C_{r}\) lies in an \(r\)-dimensional
affine subspace of \(\mathbb R^{m}\).  Since \(r<m\), such a set has
Lebesgue measure zero.

---

#### 3.  Measure of the image of \(C_{r}\)

Because \(C_{r}\) is a countable union of \(C^{k}\) submanifolds of
dimension at most \(n-(m-r)\), we may cover it by countably many
coordinate patches \(U_{\alpha}\) as in Step 2.  On each patch

\[
f\bigl(C_{r}\cap U_{\alpha}\bigr)
\subset \text{ an } r\text{-dimensional subspace of }\mathbb R^{m},
\]

hence \(\mathcal L^{m}\bigl(f(C_{r}\cap U_{\alpha})\bigr)=0\).
A countable union of null sets is null, so

\[
\mathcal L^{m}\bigl(f(C_{r})\bigr)=0.
\]

---

#### 4.  Conclude for all critical values

Finally

\[
f(C)=\bigcup_{r=0}^{m-1}f(C_{r}),
\]

a countable union of sets of measure zero.  Therefore

\[
\mathcal L^{m}\bigl(f(C)\bigr)=0,
\]

which is precisely Sard’s theorem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

**Remark.**  
An alternative, more analytic proof uses the *area formula*:
for a \(C^{1}\) map \(f:\mathbb R^{n}\to\mathbb R^{m}\),

\[
\int_{f^{-1}(E)} Jf(x)\,dx=\int_{E} N(f,E,y)\,dy,
\]

where \(Jf\) is the Jacobian determinant and \(N(f,E,y)\) is the
multiplicity of the map on \(E\).
If \(x\) is critical then \(Jf(x)=0\); hence the integral over
\(f(C)\) is zero, forcing \(\mathcal L^{m}(f(C))=0\).  This argument
requires the same differentiability hypothesis as the geometric proof
above.

**Riesz representation theorem (Hilbert space version).**  
Let \(H\) be a Hilbert space over \(\mathbb R\) or \(\mathbb C\).  
For every bounded linear functional  
\[
f\colon H\longrightarrow \mathbb F\quad(\mathbb F=\mathbb R \text{ or }\mathbb C)
\]
there exists a unique vector \(y\in H\) such that  

\[
f(x)=\langle x ,y\rangle \qquad\text{for all }x\in H .
\]

Moreover  

\[
\|f\|=\|y\|.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1.  The kernel of a bounded functional is closed

If \(f\) is bounded (hence continuous), then  

\[
M:=\ker f=\{x\in H : f(x)=0\}
\]

is a closed subspace of \(H\) (the inverse image of the closed set \(\{0\}\) under a continuous map).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.  A vector orthogonal to the kernel

Let \(M=\ker f\).  
If \(f=0\) then \(y=0\) gives the desired representation and we are done.  
Assume \(f\neq 0\).

Because \(M\) is a proper closed subspace, \(M^{\perp}\neq\{0\}\).
Choose a non‑zero \(u\in M^{\perp}\); thus \(\langle u,v\rangle =0\) for all \(v\in M\).
Since \(u\notin M\), we have \(f(u)\neq 0\).

Set  

\[
y:=\frac{\overline{f(u)}}{\|u\|^{2}}\,u .
\]

(For a real Hilbert space the bar is unnecessary.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3.  Every \(x\in H\) can be decomposed uniquely

For any \(x\in H\) there exist unique \(m\in M\) and \(\lambda\in\mathbb F\) such that  

\[
x=m+\lambda u , \qquad  \langle m,u\rangle =0 .
\]

Indeed \(H=M\oplus M^{\perp}\) and \(u\in M^{\perp}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.  Evaluation of \(f(x)\)

Using linearity of \(f\) and the fact that \(f(m)=0\) for \(m\in M\),

\[
f(x)=f(m)+\lambda f(u)=\lambda f(u).
\]

On the other hand

\[
\langle x ,y\rangle
   =\bigl\langle m+\lambda u ,\,\tfrac{\overline{f(u)}}{\|u\|^{2}}u\bigr\rangle
   =\lambda \frac{\overline{f(u)}}{\|u\|^{2}}\langle u,u\rangle
   =\lambda f(u)=f(x).
\]

Thus for all \(x\in H\)

\[
f(x)=\langle x ,y\rangle .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 5.  Uniqueness

If \(y_{1},y_{2}\in H\) satisfy  
\(\langle x ,y_{1}\rangle=\langle x ,y_{2}\rangle\) for every \(x\in H\), then  

\[
\langle x ,y_{1}-y_{2}\rangle =0 \quad\forall x\in H .
\]

Taking \(x=y_{1}-y_{2}\) gives \(\|y_{1}-y_{2}\|^{2}=0\), hence \(y_{1}=y_{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 6.  Norm identity

By Cauchy–Schwarz,

\[
|f(x)|=|\langle x ,y\rangle|\le \|x\|\,\|y\| ,
\]
so \(\|f\|\le \|y\|\).

Taking \(x=y/\|y\|\) (if \(y\neq0\); otherwise the statement is trivial) gives  

\[
|f(x)|=\Bigl|\bigl\langle \tfrac{y}{\|y\|},y\bigr\rangle\Bigr|
      =\|y\| .
\]

Thus \(\|f\|\ge \|y\|\), and consequently \(\|f\|=\|y\|\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 7.  Conclusion

Every bounded linear functional \(f\) on a Hilbert space \(H\) is represented uniquely as inner product with a (unique) vector \(y\in H\), and the operator norm of \(f\) equals the Hilbert‑space norm of \(y\). ∎
